### --- Page 0114 ---

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES. 63

freely and voluntarily, and independently of any influence over the river.

The burden of proof lies on the upholder of the instrument. The defendant in an action at law might avail himself of this defence by pleading an equitable plea, or "the facts," which however would not, it is conceived, be a good defence against a holder for value without notice.

It is a general rule of universal law that the contracts of a lunatic, an idiot or other person non compos mentis from age or personal infirmity, are utterly void. And the old authorities in the English law, that a man cannot be allowed to stultify himself by alleging his own lunacy, are shaken by the modern decisions:

but it had been before held that if a note be made by a lunatic or person of imbecile mind, known to be so by the payee, it is a fraud in the payee, and the note is void even in the hands of an indorsee, at least if there be anything *unusual on the face [64] of the note. So, if the consideration be executory merely, it was said that it might perhaps be void, though the party dealing with the lunatic were not cognizant of his infirmity. But it was held that a defendant could not set up his own insanity as a defence, unless it were known and taken advantage of by the plaintiff, so that there was a fraud in him. And it still seems that, according to the English law, in order to avoid a fair contract on the ground

1 Eskey v. Lake, 22 L. J., 386; Williams v. Bailey, L. R., H. L.; Ford v. Olden, L. R., 5 Eq., 461; Kempson v. Ashbee, L. R., 10 Chan., Ap. 15. See also the remarks of Lord Selborne in Morris v. Lord Aylesford, L. R., 8 Ch. Ap., and see post, chapter on INTEREST.

2 Lyon v. Home, L. R., 6 Eq., 681.

3 Heap v. Marris, L. R., 2 Q. B. D., 630.

4 Furtosus nullum negotium gerere potest, quia non intelligit quid agit; Inst. Lib., 3, tit. 20,8. 8; Dig. Lib. 50, tit. 1, 5, 40, 124.

5 Kent's Comm., 451; and see the observations of Parke, B., in Gore v. Gibson, 13 M. & W., 623; and Alcock v. Alcock, 3 M. & G., 268 (42 E. G. L. R.)

6 Sentence v. Poole, 3 C. & P., 1 (14 E. C. L. R.); Baxter v. Lord Portsmouth, 2 C. & P. 738 (2 E. C. L. R.); 5 B. & C., 170 (11 E. C. L. B.); 8 Dowl. & R., 614.

7 Ibid.

8 Brown v. Joddrell, 1 M. & M., 105; 3 C. & P., 30 (14 E. C. L. B.), & c.; Levy v. Baker, 1 M. & M., 106; but see Gore v. Gibson, 13 Mees. & W., 623. In Putnam v. Sullivan, 1 Mass., American Reports, it is said by Parsons, C. J., "that perhaps, if a blind man had a note falsely and fraudulently read to him, and he indorsed it, supposing it to be the note read to him, he would not be liable as indorser, because he is not guilty of any laches." It is, however, conceived that he must plead the fraud specially.

### --- Page 0115 ---

64 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES,

of lunacy, the mental incapacity must be known to the other contracting party.

Molton v. Cameron, 4 Exch. Rep., 19; Beavan v. M’Donnell, 9 Exch., 309; Eliot v. Ince, 26 L. J., Chan., 821; 7 De G., M. & G., 473, 8. But the law of America seems more in accordance with several laws, where it has been held that incapacity to contract arising from drunkenness makes a note void and incapable of confirmation. See Byles on Bills, 6th American edition, p. 104. Sanity is to be presumed and the burden of proof is on the party denying it. But after a general derangement has been shown, the burden is upon the other party to show the sanity at the time of doing a particular act: Jackson v. Van Dasen, 5 John. (N. Y.), 144. To set aside promissory notes on the ground of mental incapacity it is not necessary to prove partial derangement. It is sufficient if there appears such weakness of mind as to incapacitate the party to guard himself against imposition and undue influence: Johnsen tr. Chadwell, 8 Humph. (Tenn.), 145. An inquisition of lunacy is not conclusive against any person not a party to it: Den v. Clark, 5 Halst. (N. J.), 697. It is, however, not only prima facie evidence of lunacy, but amounts to full proof until overpowered: Rogers v. Walker, 6 Penn St., 371. The acts of a lunatic before office found are not void, but voidable: Jackson v. Gummer, 2 Cow. (N. Y.), 552. After office found they are void: Pearl v. McDowell, 3 J. J. Mar. (Ky.), 658. In an action by indorsee against the maker, insanity in the payee and indorser at the time of the indorsement is a good defence: Burke v. Allen, 9 Foster, 106. In trover for a promissory note pledged to P. by S. when he was insane, it is no defence that P. did not know of nor have any reason to suspect the insanity, and acted bona fide: Seaver v. Phelps, 11 Pick. (Mass.), 304.

An executed contract by a merchant for the purchase of goods, before the day from which the inquest find him to have been non compos, cannot be avoided by proof of insanity at the time of the purchase, unless there has been a fraud committed on him by the vendor or he has knowledge of his condition: Beals v. See, 10 Penn. St., 56. The executed contract of a non compos mentis for necessaries bona fide supplied stands on the footing of an infant's contract for necessaries. Therefore the executor of a lunatic is liable for necessaries furnished to his testator while non compos mentis before a commission issued, and after the issuing of the commission and before the appointment of a committee: La Rue v. Gilkyson, 4 Penn. St., 373. So a lunatic is bound for medical or surgical services administered to his wife: Pearl v. McDowell, 3 J. J. Mar. (Ky.), 658; Fitzgerald v. Reed, 9 So. & M. (Miss.), 94. Contracts with lunatics are not all absolutely void, but such as are fairly made with them for necessaries, or things suitable to their condition and habits of life, will be sustained: Richardson v. Strong, 13 Ired. (N. C.), 106.

When a person apparently of sound mind and not known to be otherwise, and who has not been found a lunatic, fairly and bona fide purchases property, and receives and uses the same, whereby the contract becomes so far executed that the parties cannot be placed in statu quo, such contract cannot afterwards be set aside, or payment for the goods be refused either by the privileged lunatic or his representative: Wilder v. Weakley, 34 Md., 181; Moore v. Hershey, 90 Penn. St., 196. Where habitual unsoundness of mind is once shown to exist, it is presumed to continue until something is shown to rebut the presumption: State v. Reddick, 7 Kan., 143; Carpenter v. Carpenter, 8 Bush (Ky.), 983. Where, however, the paroxysms of insanity are periodical, and the party generally recovers from them in a few days, the presumption of incapacity does not apply: Carpenter v. Carpenter, 8 Bush (Ky.), 283; Staples v. Wellington, 58 Me., 453; People v. Francis, 38 Cal., 183. Evidence that

<br>

### --- Page 0116 ---

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES. 64

Imbecility of mind cannot be proved under a plea that defendant did not make a promissory note.

It was formerly held that a man could not protect himself from any deed or agreement by pleading drunkenness, unless he also showed that the drunkenness was brought about by the management and contrivance of him who procured the deed or contract. And this may still be the law in a case of partial drunkenness.

But where there is total drunkenness the modern decisions have qualified the old doctrine. Total drunkenness producing a complete and manifest though temporary suspension of reason is of itself a defence to an action on a bill or note. “It is just the same,” says Alderson, B., “as if the defendant had written his name on the bill in his sleep in a state of somnambulism.”

But as an answer to an action on a bill or note, drunkenness must be specially pleaded.

The contracts of a married woman are void at the common law.

Without authority from her husband, therefore, she cannot at the common law charge either him or herself, by making, drawing, accepting or endorsing negotiable instruments; not even if she live apart from him, and have a separate maintenance secured by

the payee of a negotiable instrument payable to order was insane during all the time from the issuing of the paper until his death is admissible to disprove the validity of the transfer: Hannahs v. Sheldon, 20 Mich., 278.

Harrison v. Richardson, 1 Mood. & Rub., 504.

Johnson v. Medlicotto, 3 P. Wms., 130; Cooke v. Clayworth, 18 Vess., 12. Mental incapacity at the time of contracting, produced by drunkenness or any other cause, is a good defence against a contract, whether by deed or parol: Jenners v. Howard, 6 Blackf. (Ind.), 240. Whenever a man loses his memory and understanding he is entitled to legal protection, whether such loss is occasioned by his own imprudence or misconduct or by the act of Providence: Bliss v. The Railroad, 24 N.Y., 424. A note given by one incapable to contract from drunkenness is not merely voidable, but void, and is incapable of confirmation by the subsequent conduct of the maker: Barkley v. Canon, 4 Rich., 8 C., 136. See also Holland v. Barnes, 53 Ala., 83. Drunkenness is no defence against an innocent holder: State Bank v. McCoy, 7 Penn., St. 244.

At least by a person who had notice: Molton v. Camroux, 2 Exch., 487; 4 Exch., 118, etc. In Matthews v. Baxter, a replication of a ratification when sober was held good on demurrer: L. R., 8 Ex., 132.

Gore v. Gibson, 13 M. & W., 623. Marriages have been set aside on this ground: Browning v. Reane, 2 Phil., 69.

Gore v. Gibson, supra.

She cannot, like an infant, convey a title to third persons: Barlow v. Bishop, 1 East, 432; 3 Esp. R., 266, etc.

<br>

### --- Page 0117 ---

65 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES,

deed. Nor after a valid divorce, a mensa et thoro; though it is otherwise after a complete divorce, a vinculo matrimonii, which annuls the marriage to all purposes. And even if she be a sole trader in London by the custom of the city, she is not liable at all in the superior courts, and in the city courts her husband must be joined for conformity, though execution will be against the wife alone.

And it is conceived that though husband and wife are in general liable for the wrongs and frauds perpetrated by the wife, yet they are not liable for a fraudulent representation by her which is parcel of a contract.

Nor can a married woman be estopped by her own representation that she is discovert. But an acceptor may be estopped from disputing her competency.

But if a married woman have a separate estate, and make a promissory note, or accept a bill of exchange, she is liable. And

Marshall v. Rutter, 8 T. R., 545. In one case the court of C. P. refused to discharge out of custody a married woman, who had been arrested as the drawer of a bill of exchange; Jones v. Lewis, 7 Taunt, 54 (2 B.C. L. R.); 2 Marsh., 385, s. c.

Lewis v. Lee, 3 B.&C., 21; 10 C.L. & B., 90, s. c.

Beard v. Webb, 2 B. & P., 93. A promissory note made by a married woman during coverture is void, and a promise to pay the same made by her after her husband's death, without any new consideration moving her thereto, will not support an action against her: Vance v. Wells, 6 Ala., 737; Hetherington v. Nixon, 46 Ala., 297; Phillips v. Wicks, 36 N.Y. (S.C.), 254; McClure v. Harris, 7 Heisk. (Tenn.), 379. Where the wife purchased goods without the knowledge of her husband, and gave her note for them, the husband was held not to be liable: Moses v. Fogartie, 2 Hill (S.C.), B., 335. A wife may be agent for her husband, though they keep separate stores, and bind him by notes signed in her own name: Abbott v. Mackinley, 2 Miles (Penn.), 220. If a husband give his wife authority to give notes, the notes, to be binding on the husband, must purport on their face to have been given by the wife as agent on behalf of the husband: Minard v. Mead, 7 Wend. (N.Y.), 68. A feme covert is not liable on a note executed by herself, even though her husband has been absent in another State for many years: Chouteau v. Merry, 3 Mo., 954. Husband and wife executed a joint note for a debt of the wife contracted before marriage. The husband died, and the wife promised to pay the note; held that the note did not extinguish the original debt, and was capable of ratification; Parker v. Cowan, 1 Heisk. (Tenn.), 518.

Liverpool Association v. Fairhurst, 9 Exch., 422. Wright v. Leonard, 11 C.B., N.S., 258 (103 E.C.L. R.).

Cunnon v. Farmer, 3 Exch. 598.

See the chapter on Acceptance.

Formerly only in equity: Bullpin v. Clarke, 17 Ves., 366; Hulme v. Tenant, 1 Bro. C.C., 16; Stewart v. Kirkwall, 3 Madd., 387; Johnson v. Gallagher, 30 L.J., Chan., 298; McHenry v. Davies, L.B., 10 Eq., 88; 30 L.J., Chan., 866; Davies v. Jenkins, L.R., 6 Chan. Div., 723; Morrell v. Cowan, p. 166, ibid. Query, where

126

<br>

### --- Page 0118 ---

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES. §6

If, while she has a separate estate, she give a security for money lent, and after her husband’s death promise to repay it, such promise is binding at law on herself and her executors. But if at the time the note was given she had not a separate estate, no such promise, after the death of her husband, will be valid. A promissory note given by a husband to his wife for money advanced by her to him out of her separate estate constitutes a declaration of trust in favor of the wife.

And if the husband has been transported, and is not returned to this kingdom, whether or no the term of his transportation be expired; or if he be an alien, and never was within the kingdom; or if the husband has been abroad and not heard of for seven years, after which period the legal presumption of his death arises, in any one of these three cases she is liable in law for her contracts as a single woman. Where the husband was transported for fourteen years, but instead of going abroad was confined in the hulks at Portsmouth, it was held that his wife, carrying on business in her own name for the benefit of the family, might be made bankrupt, and that a bill, accepted by her under such circumstances, constituted a good petitioning creditor’s debt.

Where a bill or note is given to a single woman, and she marries, the property vests in her husband, and he alone can indorse it. There is a restraint on anticipation. See Butler v. Cumpston, 38 L. J., Chan., 35. A married woman is liable upon a promissory note given for the price of stock on a farm, of which she was seized, to her separate use: Batchelder v. Sargent, 47 N. H., 262.

1. Lee v. Mugeridge, 5 Taunt., 36 (1 F.C. L.R.).

2. Lloyd v. Lee, 1 Stra., 91; Littlefield v. Shee, 2 B. P. Ad., 811 (22 E.C.L.B.).

3. Murray v. Glasse, 23 L. J., Chan., 126.

4. Carrol v. Blencowe, 4 Esp., 27; Sparrow v. Carruthers, cited 2 W. Bls., 1197, and more fully 1 T.R., 7. See Derry v. Duchess of Mazarin, 1 Ld. Raym., 147.

5. Kay v. Duchess de Pine, 3 Camp., 123.

6. Ex parte Franks, 7 Bing., 762 (20 E.C.L.B.). As to property of a felon, see met, p. 69.

7. Connor v. Martin, 3 Wilson, 5; 1 Stra., 816, s.c. A note made payable to a married woman is in law a note to the husband, and becomes instantly his property; and her indorsement transfers no property in the note: Savage v. King, 17 Me., 1; Shuttleworth v. Moyes, 8 Mass., 229; Jones v. Warren, 4 Dana (Ky.), 333; Tryon v. Sutton, 13 Cal., 490; Holand v. Moody, 12 Ind., 170. A wife, with the consent of her husband, may indorse in her own name a promissory note made payable to her during coverture, and pass a good title to the indorsee: Stevens v. Beals, 10 Cush. (Mass.), 291; Slawson v. Lorin, 40 Allen (Mass.), 210. Notes payable to bearer may be passed by delivery by a feme covert who owned them: Cobb v. Duke, 36 Miss., 0. A feme covert may indorse in her maiden name a note which was left her before...

<br>

### --- Page 0119 ---

66 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES,

and husband and wife must join in the action upon it; but if payable to bearer, marriage may operate as an indorsement, so as to enable the husband to sue alone. If not recovered upon, or reduced into possession during their joint lives, it reverts to the woman, if she survive, or goes to the husband, as her administrator, if he survive.

If after marriage the bill or note be made to the wife alone, the interest vests in the husband; he alone can indorse it. And his indorsement is effectual, though the instrument be part of her separate estate, and be indorsed by her husband in fraud of her, to marriage, provided her husband's assent be given: Miller v. Delamater, 12 Wend (N. Y.), 433. A note given to an unmarried woman, and by her subsequently delivered in free gift to her husband, may be sued on by him in his own name: White v. Callanan, 19 Ind., 43. A second indorser cannot, in an action against him on the bill, dispute the legal capacity of the payee to indorse it, on the grounds that she was a married woman: Prescott Bank v. Caverly, 7 Gray (Mass.), 217. When a man transmitted a note to his wife drawn to her order, which she indorsed in her own name and sold by his authority, he was held liable as indorser: Hancock Bank v. Joy, 21 Me., 568. Where a husband procures promissory notes to be made to his wife by a third person, on a consideration paid by himself, the wife may maintain an action against the maker in her own name: Rynders v. Crane, 3 Daly (N. Y., C. P.), 339. A promissory note taken in the name of husband and wife survives to the wife on the decease of the husband: Sanford v. Sanford, 5 Lans. (N. Y.), 486; 61 Barb. (N. Y.), 293. If a man indorses a note payable to himself and gives it to his wife, the legal title will remain in him, and pass to his assignee in insolvency: Gay v. Kingsley, 11 Allen (Mass.), 36. As to the liability of married woman as indorser: Treadwell v. Hoffman, 5 Daly (N. Y., C. P.), 207.

1 Com. Dig. Baron and Feme, N.

2 MacNeilage v. Holloway, 1 B. & Ald., 218. As to some observations of Lord Ellenborough in this case, see the judgment in the Court of Queen’s Bench in Hart v. Stevens, 14 L. J., Q. B., 149; 6 Q. B., 913 (56 ELC. L. B.), 8. C.

3 Co. Litt, 351, b; Coppin v. ——, 2 P. Wins, 497; Day v. Padrono, 2 M. & S., OG.

4 Connor v. Martin, 1 Stra., 516; 3 Wils., 5, 8 c.; Barlow v. Bishop, 1 East, 403; Muson v. Morgan, 2 Ad. & Ellis, 30 (29 B.C. L. R.); 4 Nev. & ML, 45, 8 c. by that the wife may convey a title by indorsing in her husband's name, by his authority: Ibid. And under her husband’s authority she may indorse her own name: Prestwick v. Marshall, 7 Bing., 505 (20 B.C. L. R.); 5 M. & P., 513; 40 & P., 594. And if, after an indorsement in her own name, the acceptor, sealing the bill with the indorsement upon it, promises to pay, that amounts to an admission by the acceptor that the indorsement was by the husband's authority: Cotes v. Davis, 1 Camp., 485. Where, in an action by the indorsee of a bill against the acceptor, the declaration alleged the bill to have been drawn and indorsed to the plaintiffs by a woman, to which the defendant pleaded that she was married, a replication that she drew and indorsed as the agent of her husband was held no departure and good: Prince v. Brunatte, 1 Bing., N.C., 435 (27 E.C. L. B.); 1 Scott, 312; 3 Dust, 352, 8 c.

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES. 67

an innocent holder for value. But if the husband die without a recovery on it, or reducing it into possession, the note belongs at law, to the wife, and not to the husband’s executors, and she must bring the action. If the consideration for the note were the husband’s money, it is conceived that the wife would be a trustee for the husband’s executors. The wife may join in an action on the instrument; but the husband may sue alone. If he sue alone he lets in, by way of set-off, debts due by himself; if he joins his wife in the action, perhaps he lets in, as a set-off, debts due by her dun sola.

If a note be given after marriage to husband and wife jointly as payees, it is conceived that the legal interest in the note survives to the survivor; so held as to an investment in stock.

But now claims by or against husband and wife may be joined with claims by or against either of them separately.

What amounts to a reduction of the wife’s chose in action into possession is a question of considerable nicety. It is conceived that indorsing a note over is such a reduction. But the bankruptcy of the husband is not a reduction of the wife’s choses in action into possession; and therefore the assignees of a bankrupt cannot maintain an action in their own names alone, on a promissory note made to the wife of the bankrupt before her marriage. Nor is the receipt of interest by the husband a reduction into possession.

Dawson v. Prince, 27 L. J., Chan., 169. Query; whether the fact of a bill being drawn in favor of a married woman be notice, actual or constructive, that it is part of her separate estate; this presumption is perhaps stronger since the passage of the Married Women’s Property Act, 1870.

Betts v. Kimpton, 2 B. & Ad., 273 (22 E.C. L. R.); Richards v. Richards, 2 B. & Ad., 417; Gaters v. Madely, 6 M. & W., 423; Hart v. Stevens, 14 L. J., Q. B., 143; 6 Q. B., 97 (51 E.C. L. R.), &c; Scarpellini v. Atcheson, 14 L. J., Q. B., 433; Howard v. Onkea, 18 L. J., Exch., 485; 3 Exch., 136, &c. See this last case as to the form of pleading. Coverture of the plaintiff is only pleadable in abatement: Guyard v. Sutton, C. B., 153 (54 E.C. L. B.).

Philliskirk v. Pluckwell, 2 M. & Sel., 396.

Philliskirk et Ux. v. Pluckwell, 2 M. & Sel., 393; Arnould v. Revoult, 18 B. & B., 433 (5 E.C. L. R.); 4 Moore, 70, &c.

Burrough v. Moss, 10 B. & C., 558 (21 E.C. L. R.); 5 M. & R., 296, &c.

Ibid.; but not debts due from himself alone: Jones v. Cuthbertson, L. R., 8 Q. B., 504. Re Gadbury, 32 L. J., 330. Ord. XVII, r. 4.

Scarpellini v. Atcheson, 14 L. J., Q. B., 333; 7 Q. B. (53 E. C. L. R.), Rep., 864, &c.

Sherrington v. Yates, in error, 12 M. & W., 855, reversing Yates v. Sherrington, M. & W., 42.

Hart v. Stephens, 6 Q. B., 937 (51 E.C. L. B.). Legg v. Legg, 8 Mass., 99; Howes

68 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES.

sion, nor a direction by husband to banker to keep it separate from other moneys, followed by a bequest in his will.

Before the passing of the Married Women’s Property Act, if a single woman, being a party liable on a bill or note, married, her husband became responsible, and they must have been sued jointly. If (the debt being still unsatisfied) he dies, she is liable, and not his executors; if she dies, her representatives are liable, if there be assets, but not her husband, except in his representative capacity.

Where a joint and several promissory note was during marriage given to a feme executrix, by her husband and two other persons, it was held that after her husband’s death she might sue the other makers. And though a note given by a wife to her husband is void, yet, if indorsed over by the husband, it is valid as between the husband and the indorsee.

OM

v. Bigelow, 13 Mass., 384; Stanwood v. Stanwood, 17 Mass., 57; Tucker v. Gordon, 5 N. H., 564; Hayward v. Hayward, 20 Pick. (Mass.), 517; Strong v. Smith, 1 Met. (Mass.), 476; Miller’s Estate, 1 Ash. (Penn.), 323; Nitzinger’s Estate, 2 Ash. (Penn.), 455; Killcrease v. Killcrease, 7 How. (Miss.), 311; Taliaferro v. Taliaferro, 4 Call. (Mass.), 83; Bell v. Bell, 1 Kelly (Ga.), 637; Clark v. M’Creary, 12 S. & M. (Tigs), 347. An assignment by a husband of a present interest of his wife in personal property is a sufficient reduction to possession, and passes his property to the assignee: Browning v. Headley, 2 Rob. (La.), 310; Forrest v. Warrington, 2 Dessau (S. C.), 254; Mattheney v. Guess, 2 Hill Ch. (S. C.), 63; Thomas v. Kelso, 7 B., Mon. (Ky.), 521; Rogers v. Bumpass, 4 Tred. (N. C.), 385; Barnes v. Pearson, 6 Tred. (N. C.), 49; Swoyers’ Appeal, 5 Penn. St., 377; Sisters’ Case, 4 Rawle (Penn.), 463.

An assignment under an insolvent law, being a voluntary act by the husband, in order to entitle himself to the benefit of the law, defeats the wife’s right of survivorship: Richwine v. Heim, 1 Penn. Rep., 373; Glasgow v. Sands, 3 Gill. & Johns., 96. As to assignment in bankruptcy, see Poor v. Hazleton, 15 N. H., 564. An assignment by the husband of the wife's choses in action as collateral security does not deprive her of the right of survivorship if he die before they are reduced to possession: Hartman v. Dowden, 1 Rawle (Penn.), 279; Latourette v. Williams, 1 Barb. (N. Y.), 9. A husband’s disclaimer of conversion to his own use, at the time of reducing his wife’s chose in action to possession, may be established by his subsequent admissions, but they must be deliberate, positive, precise, clear and consistent: Gray’s Estate, 1 Barr. (Penn.), 327; Timbers v. Katz, 6 W. & S. (Penn.), 200; Hind’s Estate, 5 Whar. (Penn.), 135. A husband who survives his wife is entitled to all her choses in action, whether reduced into his possession in his lifetime or not; and in case of his death they go to his personal representatives: Whitaker v. Whitaker, 6 John. (N. Y.), 112; Revel v. Revel, 2 Dev. & Bat. (N. C.), 372; Pever v. Karwen, 2 Dessau (S. C.), 419; Lee v. Wheeler, 4 Ga., 541.

2 Mitchinson v. Hewson, 7 T. R., 348.

### --- Page 0122 ---

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES. 69

Payment of a sum due on a bill or note to a married woman will not discharge the party making it, unless she had authority, express or implied, to receive payment. It should be made to her husband.

By the 33 & 34 Vict., c. 93, s. 1, coming into operation on the 1st day of August, 1870, the wages and earnings of married women earned independently of their husbands, and money or property acquired by any literary, artistic or scientific skill, and all investments of the same are deemed to be for their separate use independently of their husbands, and they alone can give good receipts.

By sec. 7, any woman married since the above date, and succeeding to any personalty as next of kin, or taking a sum of money not exceeding £200 under any deed or will, shall have it to her separate use.

By sec. 11, a married woman may maintain an action in her own name to recover separate property, being hers under this act, or before marriage, if her husband have agreed in writing, and the remedies, both civil and criminal are to be taken in her name as if she were still unmarried.

By sec. 12, a husband married after passing of the act was not to be liable for the debts of his wife contracted before marriage, but she and her separate estate, if any, remained so; but this section is repealed by the 37 & 38 Vict., c. 51, s. 1, which enacts that a husband and wife married after the date of the act, i.e., 30th July, 1874, are to be sued jointly for the wife’s debt incurred before marriage. Sec. 2 confirms the liability of the husband in such actions, and in those founded on torts of the wife before marriage, to such assets as he may or ought to have received from his wife, provided he so plead; otherwise he will be deemed to have confessed assets.

Formerly by attainder the felon’s personal property and choses in action vested in the crown, without office found. The felon, till he had undergone his punishment, was incapable of taking. Therefore, if a bill were endorsed to him, he acquired no title to it.

1 Bayley, 256; Thrasher v. Tuttle, 9 Shep. (Me.), 335.  
4 See Summers v. City Bank, L. B., 9 C. P., 580.  
5 Bullock v. Dodds, 2 B. & Ald., 258,

70 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES,

(70) but now, by the 33 & 34 Vict., c. 28, though he can bring no action and make no contract till after expiration of sentence, the administrator or interim curator may do so.

A contract in favor of an alien enemy, not residing in this country by the king’s license, is void at law and in equity. Hence a bill drawn by an alien enemy on a British subject in England, and indorsed to a British subject abroad, cannot be enforced even after the restoration of peace.

In general, a corporation can only contract by writing under their common seal.

1 Willison v. Patteson, 7 Taunt., 439 (2 E.C.L.R.); 1 Moore 333, s.c.; Brandon v. Nesbitt, 6 T.K., 25.

2 At common law bills of exchange and promissory notes, being simple contracts, cannot be under seal, at least so as to retain their negotiable qualities: Bag, Ab. tit. Merch. L.; Black. Com., book 2, part 2, c. 5; Story on Bills of Exchange, c. iv., § 61; Story on Promissory Notes, c. iv., § 90. Whence it follows that corporations cannot, in the absence of power to that effect, either express or implied, accept, draw or indorse negotiable instruments; their bonds therefore or securities under seal, even though professing to be negotiable, are in the hands of a transferee liable to all the equities that may subsist between the original parties: In re Natal Investment Company, L.R., Chan. Ap., 355; China Steam Company, 38 L.J., Chan., 199; L. &., 7 Chan. Ap., 240, s.c. This rule, making assignments of choses in action subject to all equities subsisting between the original parties, must, however, yield whenever a contrary intention appears from the nature or terms of the contract; thus, where a company had issued bonds or securities under seal, professing to be negotiable, with the express intention that such instruments should be available in the hands of others besides the obligee, it was held that the title of a transferee or subsequent holder was not in equity affected by set-off or other equity existing between the original parties. See Blakely Ordnance Company, L.R., 3 Chan. Ap., 154, where such was the manifest intention of the agreement; see, too, Watson v. Mid Wales Railway Company, L.R., 2 C.P., 593, being a case of a Lloyd's bond transferred before the set-off accrued; Ex parte Chorley, L.B., 11 Eq., 157; 40 L.J., 153, where, though the bond was void as between the original parties, the company had registered a transfer to an innocent holder; In re Hercules Insurance Company, L.R., 19 Eq., 302, where, though the instrument was not negotiable, the company had accepted notice of transfer; Dickson v. Vale of Neath Railway Company, L.B., 40 Q.B., 44; 59 L.J., 178, s.c., where an agreement had been suppressed; General Estates Company, L.R., 3 Chan. Ap., 758; Imperial Land Company of Marseilles, L.R., 11 Eq., 478; 40 L.J., 93. These decisions, however, seem to have proceeded on the ground that the company ought to be estopped from taking advantage of their own wrong in professing to be able to issue negotiable instruments under seal. See the case of The Blakely Ordnance Company, L.R., 3 Chan. Ap., 153, where the legal effect of such a document is discussed, and its negotiability, and indeed its validity, at law questioned; Weeks v. Propert, L.R., 8 C.P., 427, where a director who had issued an unauthorized debenture was held liable as for a breach of warranty. See, too, Webb v. Herne Bay Commissioners, L.R., 5 Q.B., 612; 39 L.J., Vl. In a leading case one of these instruments was stolen, and ultimately came into the hands of a holder for value without notice, who, however, it was held, could not re-

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES, 71

*But to this rule there are exceptions;’ and among 71]
them is the power of issuing bills or notes enjoyed by a company incorporated for the purposes of trade, the very object of whose institution requires that they should exercise this privilege.’ But a company incorporated for carrying on public works is not a corporation within the above exception.°

cover against the company, on the ground that such an instrument not being at law negotiable, his title must be deduced through the thief, a person incapable of acquiring or transmitting title: Couch v. Credit Foncier Company, L. R., 8 Q. B., 374. A decision, however, commented on in the Ex. Cham., Goodwin v. Robarta, L. R., 10 Ex., 356; L Ap. Ca., 477. An instrument in the form of an ordinary bond issued by a corporation, payable to A. or bearer at a certain place, with interest coupons attached, is a promissory note: Aurora v. West, 22 Ind., 88. An order drawn by one officer of a corporation on another may be treated as a promissory note of the corporation: Chicago R. R. Co. v. West, 37 Ind., 211. Sealed note not negotiable: Corgin v. Junction R.R., 3 Houst. (Del.), 288.

1 The reader will find them enumerated in the case of East London Waterworks Company v. Bailey, 4 Bing., 283 (13 E.C. L. B.); and see Henderson v. Australian Company, 5 E. & B., 409 (85 E.C. L. R.); Haigh v. North Bierley Union, 1 E., B. & B., 973 (96 E. C. L. B.).

2 Broughton v. Manchester Waterworks Company, 3 B. & A.L., 1 (5E.C. L. R.). The old doctrine that a corporation can contract only under its corporate seal is now repudiated: Chestnut Hill Turnpike Co. v. Rutter, 4 Serg. & Rawle (Penn.) 16; Bank U.S. v. Dandridge, 12 Wheat. (U. S.), 64; Bank of Columbia v. Patterson, 7 Cranch (U. S.), 299; Fleckner v. Bank U. S., 8 Wheat. (U. S.), 338; Hamilton v. Lycoming Ins. Co., 9 Penn. St., 339. Corporations carrying on business under no restraining acts may make promissory notes and draw bills of exchange when these are the usual and proper means to accomplish the purpose of their organization: Lucas v. Pitney, 3 Dutch. (N. J.), 221; Came v. Brigham, 39 Me., 35; Clark v. School District, 3 R. I., 199; Mitchell v. Rome R. R. Co., 17 Ga., 574; Hart v. Ins. Co., 21 Mo., 91. An insurance company may make a valid promissory note: Barker v. Mechanic Ins. Co., 3 Wend. (N. Y.), 94. A note in the form “I, G. C. L., treasurer” of a corporation, “‘promise,” etc., is the note of the corporation: Mann v. Chandler, 9 Mass., 335. But see Tucker v. Bass, 5 Mass., 164. Power to advance money for a corporation will not authorize signing a note for them: Webber v. Williams College, 93 Pick. (Mass.), 302. A factor employed by the general agent of a corporation to sell the goods manufactured and to purchase stock has power to buy on credit, but not to give the note of the corporation: Emerson v. Providence Manufacturing Co., 12 Mass., 237. The general agent of a corporation can give their note for purchases necessary to carry on their business: Odiorne v. Maxey, 13 Mass., 178; White v. Westport Mfg. Co., 1 Pick. (Mass.), 215; Butts v. Cuthbertson, 6 Ga., 166. A bill of exchange directed to “‘John A. Wells, Cashier Farmers’ and Mechanics’ Bank of Michigan,” and accepted by writing across the face thereof, “Accepted, John A. Wells, Cashier,” is drawn upon and accepted by the bank, and not by Wells in his individual capacity: F. & M. Bank v. Troy City Bank, 1 Doug. (Mich.), 457. A corporation authorized by its charter to employ its stock solely in advancing money upon goods and the sale of such goods on commission may lawfully accept bills drawn on account of future consignments: Munn v. Commission Company, 15 John. (N. Y.), 44.

3 A railway company can neither accept, draw nor indorse a bill of exchange:

<br>

### --- Page 0125 ---

A CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES,

Without a special authority, express or implied, a corporation has no power to make, indorse, or accept bills or notes. Any defence might be raised by demurrer to the declaration, plea denying acceptance, or, if there were a power not duly exercised, by general traverse.

A corporation might, like natural persons, sue in assumpsit. The old doctrine that when a corporation is plaintiff the consideration must not be executory, so that promises by it need to be alleged, seems to be overruled. And a corporation is liable to be sued in the ordinary forms of action on negotiable instruments, wherever it has the power to issue them.

ibid.; Overend, Gurney & Co. v. Mid Wales Railway Co, L. R. 10. P., 499. This disability, however, seems not to extend to companies incorporated here for carrying on works abroad. See case of Peru Railway Company, L. B., 2 Chan., Ap., 617.

1 Ibid., p. 8, Bayley, J.

2 Hill v. Manchester and Salford Waterworks Company, 5 B. & Ad., 866 (27 E. C. L. B.).

3 Mayor of Stafford v. Till, 4 Bing. 75 (13 E. C. L. R.); 12 Moore, 260, &c.

4 Church v. Imperial Gas Company, 6 Ad. & E., 861 (33 E. C. L. R.); Mayor of Ludlow v. Charlton, 6 M. & W., 815; Paine v. Guardians of the Strand Union, 15 L. J., M. Ca., 89; 8 Q. B., 326 (55 E. C. L. B.), &c.; Lamprell v. Billericay Union, 3 Exch., 283.

5 Murray v. East India Company, 5 B. & Al., 204 (7 B. C. L. R.). A cashier has prima facie authority to indorse, on behalf of the bank, securities held by it, and any restriction on this authority must be proved by the party contesting it: Wild v. Passamaquoddy Bank, 3 Mas. (U. S.), 505; Fleckner v. United States Bank, 8 Wheat. (U. S.), 357; Everett v. United States, 6 Port. (Ala.), 160; Elliott v. Abbott, 12 N. H., 549; Crockett v. Young, 1 S. & M. (Miss.), 241; Harper v. Calhoun, 7 How. (Miss.), 203; Farrar v. Gilman, 19 Me., 440; F. & M. Bank v. Troy City Bank, 1 Doug. (Mich.), 457; Badger v. Bank of Cumberland, 26 Me., 428. A corporation is liable on a draft drawn or accepted by an authorized agent, though the name of the corporation is not used, if it be drawn or accepted under a name adopted by the corporation: Course v. Port Henry Iron Co., 12 Barb. (N. Y.), 27.

Manufacturing and other like corporations, when not expressly prohibited by their charters, may make and receive promissory notes and bills of exchange in carrying on their lawful business: Oxford Iron Co. v. Spradley, 46 Ala., 98; San Antonio v. Gould, 34 Tex., 49; Monument Nat. Bk. v. Globe Works, 101 Mass. 57. A railroad company has an inherent authority to take and negotiate a promissory note in the ordinary course of their business: Goodrich v. Reynolds, 31 Ill., 490. A corporation is liable on indorsement by its president or cashier: Aiken v. Marine Bank, 16 Wis., 679; Clark v. Titcomb, 42 Barb. (N. Y.), 122; State Bank v. Fox, 3 Blatchf. (U. S. C. C.), 431; Patten v. Moses, 49 Me., 255; Goodrich v. Reynolds, 31 Ill., 490. Notes signed by agents or officers: Dow v. Moore, 47 N. H., 419; Yowell v. Dodd, 3 Bush (Ky.), 581; Capbart v. Dodd, Id., 534. A draft signed by the secretary of an insurance company is not binding upon such company in the absence of evidence that it was authorized or customary: First Nat. Bank v.

### --- Page 0126 ---

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES. 12

*The capacity of corporations and banking companies to make, draw or accept negotiable instruments is further narrowed by the following enactment, contained in the various statutes passed for protecting the privileges of the Bank of England: "That it shall not be lawful for any body, politic or corporate, whatsoever, or for any other persons whatsoever, united or to be united in covenant or partnership, exceeding the number of six persons in England, to borrow, owe or take up any sum or sums of money on their bills or notes payable at demand, or at any less time than six months from the borrowing thereof, during the continuance of the privilege of banking granted to the Governor and Company of the Bank of England."

It has been held that these restrictions do not affect a commercial firm consisting of more than six persons.

But in consequence of the panic in the latter part of the year 1825, the Bank of England consented to forego a portion of their exclusive privilege; and the 7 Geo. 4, c. 46, enacts, accordingly, that corporations or copartnerships of more than six in number, carrying on business more than sixty-five miles from London, may issue bills or notes payable on demand, and that such corporations or copartnerships may issue notes or bills amounting to 50 pounds, payable in London or elsewhere at any period after date or sight.

The third section declares that any such corporation or partnership may discount bills not drawn by or upon them.

Each offence against the provisions of the act subjects to a penalty of 500.

Hogan, 47 Mo., 472. An agent of a corporation may have authority to transfer a note by indorsement without authority to bind the corporation as indorser. The authority of an agent of a corporation to indorse a note may be shown by other evidence than by the by-laws; Brown v. Donnell, 49 Me., 421. Maker of a promissory note to a corporation, transferred to a bona fide holder before maturity, is estopped to deny the legal existence of the corporation: Camp v. Byrne, 41 Dfo., 820. And see Gillett v. New Market Bank, 7 Il. Ap., 499; Chamberlain v. Pacific Co., 54 Cal., 103.

39 & 40 Geo. 3, c. 28, s. 15.

* For the history and exclusive privileges of the Bank of England more at large, see the case of The Bank of England v. Anderson, 3 Bing., N. C., 589 (32 E. C. L. B.); 4 Scott, 50; Keen, 328.

* Wigan v. Fowler, 1 Stark., 459 (2 E. C. L. B.).

The limitation of 50 pounds appears to be abolished by the 3 & 4 Will. 4, c. 83, s. 2, and 7 & 8 Vict., c. 32, s. 26. As to the mode of recovering penalties, see 8 & 9 Vict., c. 46, s. 5.

### --- Page 0127 ---

02 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES.

The act by which the bank charter was renewed in 1833, the 3 & 4 Will. 4, c. 98, continued the privileges bestowed on the Bank of England by the 39 & 40 Geo. 2, and subsequent acts, subject to termination of twelve months’ notice to be given after the 1st August, 1844. The privileges of the bank are now further continued by the 7 & 8 Vict. c. 32, subject to termination on twelve months’ notice to be given after the 1st August, 1855.

The 3 & 4 Will. 4, c. 98, provides that no bank of more than six persons shall issue in London, or within sixty-five miles thereof, bills or notes payable on demand, leaving the rights of country bankers to make their notes payable in London.

The 3 & 4 Will. 4, c. 98, further declares that other corporations and companies of more than six persons may carry on the business of banking in London, provided they do not issue bills or notes at less than six months’ date.

That the notes of the branch banks of England shall be made payable where issued.

The Bank of England can issue bank notes unstamped, and has the exclusive privilege of doing so within the city of London and three miles thereof.

No person who is not a banker issuing his own notes on the 6th of May, 1844, can now issue bank notes.

Bank notes under 5l. payable to bearer on demand are prohibited in England.

Banks of ten, formerly six, or fewer than six persons, existing as banks of issue before the 6th of May, 1844, may issue bills and notes and promissory notes payable to bearer on demand, on unstamped paper (except within the city of London and three miles thereof), within the provisions of 9 Geo. 4, c. 23, s. 1.

Banking corporations and companies of more than ten (formerly

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES, mo

six) persons cannot issue in London or within sixty-five miles thereof any bill or note payable on demand.

Every member of a banking partnership is liable to the payment of outstanding notes, though he were not a partner when they were issued.

But a more lengthened and minute inquiry into the provisions of these and other statutes regulating the rights and duties of the Bank of England and other banks of issue would be a digression from the main subject of this work. Such a discussion would find a more appropriate place in a treatise on the law of Banks of Issue, Deposit and Exchange.

The law as to the liability of joint stock companies drawing, accepting or indorsing bills involves some nice distinctions, and is not yet very clearly settled.

As to joint-stock companies at the common law, it is conceived to be a general rule that if the directors accept simply in their own names, with or without authority to do so, they, and they only, are liable at law on the bills. And that they are liable at law not only to holders who are strangers, but to holders who may be also holders of letters of allotment, or holders of scrip.

If, however, having authority to bind the company by bills, the directors regularly accept, in the name of the company, a bill drawn on the company, every member of the company is liable as a joint acceptor to any holder, not being also a member of the company.

An authority to make contracts and bargains and to transact all matters requisite for the affairs of the company will not in general authorize the directors to draw bills. But a limited authority to draw bills will receive a fair and reasonable construction.

L. B.); 1 Scott N. R. B. 701, s. c. See also the provisions of 7 Geo. 4, c. 46; 7 & 8 Vict., c. 32, s. 26; 8 & 9 Vict., c. 76; 20 & 21 Vict., c.

### --- Page 0129 ---

74 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES,

Directors signing joint and several note, though for themselves and the other shareholders, are personally responsible. But not necessarily so if the note be joint only. And it has been held that if the secretary’s name is countersigned as secretary, he also is liable.

75] If a bill be drawn on several trustees or directors who have power to bind each other, an acceptance by one in his own name is the acceptance of all.

Notice of a fact to one member of a joint-stock company is not notice to all, as in a case of a private partnership.

A bill drawn on the agent of a joint-stock company, he being a member of it, and accepted by him per procuration for the company, binds him personally as a member.

The stat. 7 & 8 Vict., c. 110, s. 45 (since repealed), enacted that where the directors were authorized by deed of settlement or by-law to issue or accept bills or notes, they should be made or accepted by two directors, and expressed to be made or accepted on behalf of the company, and countersigned by the secretary. That they might be indorsed in the name of the company by any officer authorized by deed or by-law. That on instruments properly made the company might be sued, but the signing officers were not liable.

The liability of a company formed under this act could not be limited by the deed of settlement, and a proviso in a bill of exchange limiting the liability is repugnant and void.

On this statute it was held that an acceptance in this form, "A and B., directors appointed by resolution to accept this bill," was an acceptance within the statute.

3 Bottomley v. Fisher, 31 L. J., Exch., 417; 1 H. & Colt., 211.

### --- Page 0130 ---

CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES. 49

The registered deed is notice of its contents to all who deal with the company.

The statute 25 & 26 Vict., c. 89, s. 47, amended by 30 & 31 Vict. c. 131, enacts that bills and notes made, accepted or indorsed in the name of the company, by any person acting under the authority of the company, express or implied, shall bind the company.

But if any person on behalf of a limited company registered under the act signs or indorses a bill, cheque or note on which the name of the company is not duly mentioned, he is liable to a penalty of 50l., and is moreover made personally responsible to the holder.

By the 25 & 26 Vict., c. 89, s. 95, official liquidators appointed under that act have power, with the sanction of the court, to draw, accept, make or indorse bills or notes in the name and on behalf of the company. By sec. 133, the same or perhaps wider powers are conferred on liquidators appointed under a voluntary winding-up. But one cannot accept on behalf of all, even under a resolution to that effect.

If persons who fill official situations, as churchwardens, overseers, surveyors, commissioners, managers of joint-stock banks, agents and secretaries to companies, and the like, give bills or notes on which they describe themselves in their official capacity, they are nevertheless personally liable. Thus, drafts on a banker, signed by commissioners under an inclosure act "as commissioners," bind the commissioners personally. So does a promissory note given by A. and B. as churchwardens and overseers.

1 Ridley v. Plymouth Company, 2 Exch., 711; Balfour v. Ernest, 28 L. J., C. 170; O. C. B., N.S., 601 (94 E. C. L. B.), s. c.; Royal British Bank v. Turquand, 5 & B., 213 (85 E. C. L. B.); 6 E. & B., 327 (88 E. C. L. B.).

2 Lindus v. Melrose, supra. As to what is a making in the name of the company, see further, Aggs v. Nicholson, 1 H. & N., 165.

3 And described as limited. See sec. 5.

4 See, 42; Penrose v. Martyr, 28 L. J., Q. B., 28; E. B. & E., 499 (96 E. C. L. R.), s. c. But it seems he is not liable as an acceptor of a bill drawn on the company: Eastwood v. Bain, 23 L., Ex., 74; 5 H. & N., 738, s. c. Query, whether he be liable for a false representation. (Ibid.)

5 Rea v. Merchant and Keate, Birmingham Bank, L. U., 3 Chan. Ap., 651, where the bill holders, however, were allowed to prove for money advanced. Ex parte Agra & Masterman, L. B., 6 Chan. Ap., 206, decided the same point as to renewed bills. See also Bolognesi’s case, L. R., 5 Chan. Ap., 567.

6 Eaton v. Bell, 5 B. & Ad., 34 (T. E. G. L. R.); Nichols v. Diamond, 9 Exch., 154; Bottomley v. Fisher, 1 H. & C., 211.

7 Rew v. Petit, 1 Ad. & E., 196 (28 E.C.L.B.); 3 Nev. and M., 456, s. c., nom.

### --- Page 0131 ---

6 CAPACITY OF CONTRACTING PARTIES

So it is conceived that the legal interest in a bill or note given an officer by his name of office vests in the person who happens to be fill the office at the time. Thus, a note given to the manager of a joint-stock banking company “vests at law in the person who fills that office when the note is given.” And where a note was made payable to the trustees acting under A.’s will, parol evidence was held admissible to show who they were and what the trusts were.

But a bill or note payable at a certain time after date to the secretary or other officer for the time being of a company is void, the payee being uncertain at the time of making.

The manager, as well as any other bona fide holder, may of course sue in his own name on any bills indorsed in blank belonging to a banking company.

And where a note was given to the treasurer of a loan society for the time being, under the 6 & 6 Will. 4, c. 28, neither the treasurer when the note was given, nor his successor in office, could maintain an action on the note, for the additional reason that the acts of parliament establishing loan societies contemplated proceedings by complaint before a justice of the peace.

But by the 3 & 4 Vict., c. 110, ss. 16 and 17, the treasurer or clerk for the time being may sue on such a note in any county court or court of conscience or request.

Crew v. Petit; Price v. Taylor, 29 L. J. Ex., 331; 5 H. & N., 540; and tide ante, p. 37.

The personal liability of churchwardens and officers is not transferred to their successors by the 11 & 12 Vict., c. 91. See Chambers v. Jones, 5 Exch., 229.

But a promissory note to the trustees of a chapel or their treasurer for the time being was held good, for the trustees were held to be the payees and the treasurer merely an agent: Holmes v. Jaques, Law Rep., 1 Q. B., 376. See the chapter on IRREGULAR INSTRUMENTS.

4 Law v. Parnell, 30 L. J., 17; 7 C. B., N.S., 282 (97 E. C. L. B.), s. c.

Bills of exchange and promissory notes are usually, but it is apprehended not necessarily, written on paper. It is conceived that they might be written on parchment, linen, cloth, leather or any other convenient substitute for paper, not being a metallic substance.  
They may be written in any language and in any form of words. A bill or note, or any other contract, may be written in pencil as well as in ink. “There is,” says Abbott, C. J., “no authority for saying that, when the law requires a contract to be in writing, that writing must be in ink. There is not any great danger that our decision will induce individuals to adopt the mode of writing by pencil in preference to that in general use. The imperfection of this mode of writing, its liability to obliteration, and the impossibility of proving it when so obliterated, will prevent its being generally adopted.” Contracts written and signed in pencil [*79] are constantly admitted as written contracts at Nisi Prius.

### --- Page 0133 ---

79 FORM OF BILLS AND NOTES,

and testamentary writings in pencil often in the Ecclesiastical Courts.

The signature or indorsement of negotiable instruments may be by mark.

It is proper, though not necessary, to superscribe the name of the place where the bill or note is made.

But a check on a banker must, formerly, unless stamped as a bill, have expressed the place where drawn, and such place must have been within fifteen miles of the banker’s place of business.

The 9 Geo. 4, c. 65, prohibits the circulation of all negotiable notes or bills under £2, or on which less than £5 shall remain undischarged, payable to bearer on demand, and which were made, or purport to be made, in Scotland, or Ireland, or elsewhere out of England, under the penalty of £20 to be recovered in a summary way. 

Note: and if there be no date, it will be considered as dated at the time it was made. Under the old pleading, if it was stated to

1 Rhymes v. Clarkson, 1 Phil., 22; Green v. Skipworth, 1 Phil., 53; Dickenson c. Dickenson, 2 Phil., 173.

2 George v. Surrey, 1 M. & M., 516. As to acceptance, see post, p., 181.

3 55 Geo., 3, c. 184, s. 13; 9 Geo., 4, c. 49, s. 15. See the chapter on Checks, and the recent statutes there referred to.

4 This does not extend to drafts on bankers: see sec. 4.

5 De la Courtier v. Bellamy, 2 Show., 422; Hague v. French, 3 B. & P., 173; Giles v. Bourne, 6 M. & Sel., 73; 2 Chit., R., 309, s. c. Parol evidence is admissible to show from what time an undated instrument was intended to operate: Davis v. Jones, 25 L. J., C. P., 91; 17 C. B., 629; (84 E. C. L. B.), &c. A date is not essential: Michigan Ins. Co. v. Leavenworth, 30 Vt., 11; McSparran v. Neeley, 91 Penn. St., 17, and in an action against the maker, it may be shown that there was a mistake in the date: Drake v. Rogers, 32 Me., 524. And if it bears interest from its date, the date of its delivery may be shown and interest computed from that time: Richardson v. Ellett, 10 Tex., 190. A promissory note which is not dated will be considered as dated at the time it was made, or at the time of its delivery: Seldonridge v. Connable, 32 Ind., 375. The place where or the time when a note is made need not be specified therein to render it binding on the maker: Wexel v. Cameron, 31 Tex., 614. If a note bears date on Sunday, it may be shown that it was in fact executed and delivered on a different day: Aldridge v. Branch Bank, 17 Ala., 45. Otherwise it is void unless subsequently ratified: Bosler v. McAllister, 13 Ind., 365. If delivered on any other day, the fact that it is dated, or was in fact made on Sunday will not defeat a recovery upon it: Bank v. Mayberry, 48 Me., 198; King v. Fleming, 72 Ill., 21; Flanagan v. Meyer, 41 Ala., 133; Bank v. Thompson, 42 N. H., 376; Lovejoy v. Whipple, 18 Vt., 370; Drake v. Rogers, 32 Me., 624; Dahoney v. Dahoney, 7 Bush (Ky.), 217; Vinton v. Peck, 15 Mich., 287; Tricher v. Bank, 31 Ark., 123; Fritsch v. Haessler, 40 Mo., 556; S. P. Mosley v. Vanhooser, 6 Lea (Tenn.), 285.

<br>

### --- Page 0134 ---

FORM OF BILLS AND NOTES. 79

have been drawn on a particular day, but the declaration did not state the date appearing on the bill, that was sufficient on a motion ‘y arrest of judgment or on a demurrer.’

The date expressed in the instrument is (except when it is tendered by assignees of a bankrupt as evidence of a petitioning creditor’s debt), prima facie evidence of the time when the instrument was made.*

Formerly promissory notes, payable to bearer on demand, must not have had printed dates, under the penalty of 5024 But the statute prohibiting them is now repealed.

In general, a bill or note may be post-dated. But under the

Love v. Wells, 25 Ind., 503, a note signed and delivered on Sunday, but bearing date on another day, is valid in the hands of a bona fide holder: Id. A promissory note given on Sunday for an antecedent debt is valid: Kauffman v. Hamm, 95 Mo., 357. A note made and delivered on Sunday is good in the hands of a bona fide holder: State Bank v. Thompson, 42 N. H., 369. The fact that interest commences to run from the date of a note which is dated on Sunday, but in fact delivered on a week day, does not invalidate it: Marshall v. Russell, 44 N. H., 507. And if made and delivered upon Sunday, if it is ratified by the parties thereto upon a week day, it is an operative note: Benson v. Drake, 55 Mo., 556. A note is not invalidated by being antedated: Gray v. Wood, 2 H. & J., (Md.), 328; Michter v. Selin, 8 S. & R. (Penn.), 425. A note takes effect by delivery and from the time of delivery; but a delivery, and at the time of the date will be presumed, until the contrary appear: Woodford v. Dorwia, 3 Vt., 82; Lansing v. Gaine, 2 Johns. (N. Y.), 500. Where a bill was drawn in blank as to date and name of drawer, but at four months after date, and the person to whom it was delivered to be negotiated filled up the date with a day anterior to the delivery and left it with a creditor of his own, who knew that it was antedated, as security for an antecedent debt, it was held to be void in his hands: Goodman v. Simon, 19 Mo., 106. Id.

2 Wright v. Lainson, 2 M. & W., 739; 6 Dowl., 146, s. c.; see post.

Anderson v. Weston, 6 Bing., N. C., 296 (37 E.C. L. R.); Scott, 893, s. c.; Taylor v. Kinloch, 1 Stark., 175 (2 E.C. 1. R.); Obbard v. Betham, 1 M. & M., 486; Smith v. Barons, 1 M. & Rob., 347; but see Cowie v. Harris, 1 M. & M., 141 (2 E.C. L. R.); 4 M. & P., 722, s. c.; Rose v. Rowcroft, 4 Camp., 245. And this rule applies to written documents in general: Sinclair v. Baggaley, 4 M. & W., 312; Davies v. Lowndes, 7 Scott's New Rep., 213; Potez v. Glossop, 2 Exch., 195; Harrison v. Clifton, 17 L. J. + Exch., 233; and the cases cited in the note to Potez v. Glossop. All the Irish judges, in Butler v. Mountgarret, considered the point as finally settled: but in the same case, 7 H. L. Cases 647, Lord Wensleydale expressed a doubt whether the cases above referred to had been rightly decided. The weight of authority, however, is in favour of the rule as laid down in the text, and it would be difficult to conduct investigations at Nisi Prius without such a presumption.

55 Geo., 3, c. 184, 8, 18. § 23 & 24 Vict., c. 111, s. 19.

Pasmore v. North, 13 East, 517. See Austin v. Bunyard, 27 L. J., 217; Foster v. Mackworth, L. R., 2 Exch., 163; 36 L. J., 94, s. c.; Emmanuel v. Roberts, 9 B. & S., 121 (41 E. C. L. R.); Bull v. Sullivan, L. R., 6 Q. B., 209; 40 L. J., 141; Gatty v. Fry, L. R., 2 Exch. D., 265.

SO FORM OF BILLS AND NOTES.

Old acts if this was done so as to postpone the time of payment beyond the period of two months after the making, or so as to make it in effect payable at a longer interval than sixty days after sight and thus evade the higher scale of duty for bills at long dates, a penalty of 100l. was incurred, and the instrument was inadmissible in evidence.

But an unstamped bill or note issued by bankers under the provisions of 9 Geo. 4, c. 23, must not be postdated, under the penalty of 100l.

All negotiable bills, notes or drafts for 20s., or any sum between 20s. and 5l., must formerly have been dated before or at the time of issuing, under the penalty of 20l.

The usual allegation that a bill or note was made on a particular day is not matter of description, and the day need not be proved as laid. It would have been otherwise if the declaration went on to describe the instrument as bearing date on a particular day.

Misdescription of the date of a bill in an agreement is immaterial if the bill were in existence and present. For "presentia corporis tollit errorem nominis."

The sum for which a bill is made is usually superscribed in figures: in a note or check the figures are commonly subscribed. The superscription or subscription of the sum payable is not necessary if the sum be stated in the body of the note, but it will aid an omission in the body; as, where the word fifty was written in the body of the note without the word pounds.

155 Geo. 3, c. 184, s. 12.

Field v. Wood, 6 Dowl., P. C. 23; 7 Ad. & El., 114 (84 E.C.L.R.); 2 N&P, 117, s. c.; Serle v. Norton, 9 M. & W., 309. Unless to order, see sec. 13, and Emmanuel v. Roberts, sup.

Sec. 12.

17 Geo. 3, c. 30, revived by 7 Geo. 4, c. 6; repealed as to checks, 17 & 18 Vict., c. 83, s. 9; temporarily repealed by 26 & 27 Vict., c. 105, 32 & 33 Vict., c. 35, 34 & 35 Vict., c. 40, and 33 & 34 Vict., c. 72.

Coxon v. Lyon, 2 Camp., 307, u.; Smith v. Lord, 14 b. J., Q. B., 112; 2 D. & L. 100, s. c.

Way v. Hearne, 32 L. J., 34.

Eliot's case, 2 East, P. C., 951; 1 Leach, 175, s. c. The check mark or figures of a note form no part of its legal obligation and can be used to control the body of the note only when the meaning is doubtful: Riley v. Dickens, 19 Ill. 293; Mears v. Graham, & Blackf. (Ind.), 144; Smith v. Smith, 1 R. I., 393; Corgan v. Frew, 39 MI, 31. A note for three hundred dollars, and in figures $300, is a good note for that.

FORM OF BILLS AND NOTES, 8]

The time of payment is regularly and usually stated in the beginning of the note or bill; but, if no time be expressed, the instrument will be payable on demand.

amount, if the maker so intended when he signed it, which is a question for the jury: Burnham v. Allen, 1 Gray (Mass.), 496; Ohm v. Yung, 63 Md., 432.

Whitlock v. Underwood, 3 Dowl. & R., 366; 2 BL & C, 157 (9 E.G. L. R.), s.c.; Down v. Halling, 4 B. & C., 333 (10 E.C.L.R.), 6 Dowl. & R., 455; 20. & P., 1, s.c.; Bayley 5th ed., 109; and the words may be added without avoiding the instrument: Aldous v. Cornwell, L. &., 3 Q. B., 573; 37 L.J., 201. But on a motion to set aside an annuity, the court will not assume that even a Bank of England note, or a draft on a banker, is payable on demand. See the cases collected in the recent case of Abbott v. Douglass, 1 C. B., 491 (50 E.C.L. R.). Holmes v. West, 17 Cal., 623; Salinas v. Wright, 11 Tex., 572; Porter v. Porter, 51 Me., 376; Keyes v. Fenstermaker, 24 Cal., 329. A note specifying no time of payment is in legal effect payable immediately and is valid and negotiable: Libbey v. Mikeborg, 23 Minn., 33; Dodd v. Denney, 6 Or., 156; Bacon v. Page, 1 Conn., 404; Pindar v. Barlowe, 31 Vt., 529; Thuyek v. Meador, 24 Ark., 191; Keyes v. Fenstermaker, 24 Cal., 329; Porter v. Porter, 51 Me., 376; Meador v. Dollar Savings Bank, 56 Ga., 605; Bank v. Price, 52 Ia., 570; Jackett v. Spencer, 20 Barb. (N.Y.), 180; Jones v. Brown, 11 Ohio St., 601; Salinas v. Wright, 11 Tex., 572; Palmer v. Palmer, 36 Mich., 487; Herrick v. Wolverton, 41 N.Y., 591; Caldwell v. Rodman, 5 Jones (N.C.), L., 189; Wheeler v. Warner, 47 N.Y., 519; Seymour v. Continental Ins. Co., 44 Conn., 300; Jones v. Brown, 11 Ohio St., 601; and the fact that it is payable with interest does not operate to create a presumption that it was not to be paid immediately, and the Statute of Limitations commences running on such notes from their date: Wilks v. Robinson, 3 Rich. (S.C.), L., 102; Wheeler v. Warner, 47 N.Y., 519; Mitchell v. Easton, 37 Minn., 335; Burnham v. Allen, 1 Gray (Mass.), 496; Hill v. Henry, 17 Ohio, 9; Howland v. Edmunds, 2d N.Y., 807; Caldwell v. Rodman, 5 Jones (N.C.), L., 189; Schreiber v. Richmond, 73 Wis., 12; Curren v. Witter, 63 Wis., 16; Lanison v. Lambert, 12 N.J., L., 217; Taylor v. Whitman, 3 Grant Cas. (Penn.), 138; and the fact that the note is payable at a particular place does not make it necessary that the person seeking to enforce the note against the maker should either allege or prove that it was presented at such place for payment before the commencement of the action. But in order to hold an indorser, the note being due before the indorsement was made, it is treated as being substantially the same as a note payable at a fixed time and then indorsed by the payee after maturity, and in order to charge the indorser, payment must be demanded of the maker within a reasonable time and notice of non-payment given to the indorser: Bussinghorst v. Wilby, 45 Ohio St., 333; Tyler v. Young, 30 Pa. St., 84; Hart v. McClellan, 80 Me., 95; Denny v. Palmer, 5 Tred. (N.C.), L., 610; Mudd v. Harper, 1 Md., 110; Berry v. Robinson, 9 John (N.Y.), 121; Gray v. Bell, 2 Rich. (S.C.), L., 67; Leavitt v. Putnam, 3 N.Y., 494; Coate v. Barnard, 18 Pick. (Mass.), 260; Gunn v. Madigan, 23 Wis., 164; Kirkpatrick v. McCullough, 3 Humph. (Tenn.), 171; and in making such demand the indorsee is charged with due diligence, which is a question of fact for the jury where the facts are in dispute: Passonhorst v. Wilby, ante; Van Housen v. Van Alston, 3 Wend. (N.Y.), 79; Walker v. Stetson, 14 Ohio St., 69; Tyler v. Young, 30 Pa. St., 144; McKinney v. Crawford, 8 S.& S. (Penn.), 351; Addis v. Johnson, 1 Vt., 136. A note promising to pay a sum of money “on demand, with interest within six months,” is payable on demand: Jillson v. Hill, 4 Gray (Mass.), 316. A note promising to pay “on demand, with interest annually payable four months from date,” is a promise to pay on demand, and annual

10 145

### --- Page 0137 ---

81 FORM OF BILLS AND NOTES,

Negotiable bills or notes under 5/. must formerly have been made payable within the space of twenty-one days from the date. But now there is no limitation as to the time when the bill or note is to be made payable. The bill or note may be made payable on demand or at sight (which is now the same thing, 34 & 35 Vict., c. 74) or at any certain period after date, or after sight, or at usance, “[a] bill of exchange be made payable at never so distant a day, if it be a day that must come, it is no objection to the bill.”

The expression after sight, on a bill of exchange, means after acceptance, or protest for non-acceptance, and not after a mere private exhibition to the drawee, for the sight must appear in a legal way. But if a note is made after sight, the expression merely imports that payment is not to be demanded till it has been again exhibited to the maker;* for a note being incapable of acceptance, the

interest after four months from date: Shaw v. Shaw, 43 N. H., 170. A note given to a mutual insurance company, payable at such times and in such portions as the directors may require, is in legal effect payable on demand, that is, at its date, from which time the Statute of Limitations begins to run: Colgate v. Buckingham, 39 Barb. (N.Y.), 177. In Smithers v. Junker, 41 Fed. Rep., 101, it was held that a note given for value received in the following words:

“CHICAGO, Nov. 1st, 1883.  
For value received I promise to pay to S. F. Smithers, two thousand and forty-eight and ½ dollars, payable at my convenience, and upon this express condition that I am to be the sole judge of such convenience and time of payment.  
A. JUNKER.”

might be enforced by an action after the expiration of a reasonable time, on demand and refusal of payment.

It was held that such an obligation does not give the maker the sole right to say when it would suit his convenience to pay it, but that he must exercise a reasonable and honest judgment as to the time of payment. Such contracts must be construed liberally in favor of the payee: Kincaide v. Higgins, 11 Bibb. (Ky.), 395; Lewis v. Tipton, 10 Ohio St., 88; Raymond v. Schotenfels, 15 Ia., 457; Works v. Horshey, 10 Ia., 310.

¹ 17 Geo. 3, c. 30, temporarily repealed. See post, p. 87.

² 2 Willes, C. J., in Colehan v. Cooke, Willes, 396.

³ Marius 19, cited by Lord Kenyon in Campbell v. French, 6 T. R., 212. So in America it has been held that after sight means after acceptance, and not after mere presentment: Byles on Bills, 6th American edition, p. 127. A bill payable so many days after sight means legal sight, and the bill begins to run from the presentment and acceptance, and not from the time of mere presentment: Mitchell v. de Grand, 1 Mass. (U.S.), 176.

⁴ Holmes v. Kerrison, 2 Taunt., 323; Sturdy v. Henderson, 4 B. & Al., 592 (6 E. C. B. R.); Sutton v. Toomer, 7 B. & C., 416 (4 B. C. L. R.); 1 M. & By. 125, 90.5; Vixon v. Nuttall, 1 C., M. & B., 307; 6 C. & P., 320 (25 E. C. L. B.).

<br>

### --- Page 0138 ---

FORM OF BILLS AND NOTES. $1

The word “sight” must, on a note, bear a different meaning from the same word on a bill.

Foreign bills are commonly drawn at one, two or more usances, or, as it is sometimes expressed, at single, double, treble or half usance. Usance signifies the usage of the countries between which bills are drawn with respect to the time of payment. If a foreign bill be drawn, payable at sight, or at a certain period after sight, the acceptor will be liable to pay according to the course of exchange at the time of acceptance, unless the drawer express that it is payable according to the course of exchange at the time it was drawn, en especes de ce jour... Where half usance stands for half a month, it is fifteen days. And, in the case of all bills payable in England, month means calendar month.

The bill or note must be certainly payable at some time or other.

The order to pay need be in no particular form; any expression amounting to an order or direction is sufficient. The word “pay” itself is not indispensable. Any synonymous or equivalent expression will suffice, as “Credit in cash.”

The payee should be particularly described, so that he cannot be confounded with another person of the same name, and must be a person who is capable of being ascertained at the time the instrument is made. It is sufficient that the payee be so designated,

1 Poth, 174; Bank & Officer, 59 Tenn., 173.

2 Jide post, IRREGULAR INSTRUMENTS,

3 Hamilton v. Spottiswood, 4 Exch, 200.

4 Beawes 3; Marius II. In France, “si vous plaira payer” is the common language of a bill; Morris v. Lee, 2 Ld. Raym. 1397; 1 Stra. 629, &c. Query, whether a mere written request, without any words of demand, amount to a bill. Lord Kenyon held this instrument to be a bill: “Mr. Nelson will much oblige Mr. Webb by paying to J. Rul, or order, twenty guineas on his account”: Rult v. Webb, 1 Exch., 129. But Lord Tenterden held the following instrument not to be a bill: — “Mr. Little, please to let the bearer have seven pounds, and place it to my account, and you will oblige your humble servant, R. Slackford”: Little v. Slackford, 1 M. & M. 171. “The paper,” says his lordship, “does not purport to be a demand made by a party having a right to call on the other to pay. The fair meaning is, ‘you will oblige me by doing it.’” But see Russell v. Powell, 4 M. & W., 448. Where an instrument is capable of being interpreted either as a bill of exchange or a promissory note, the person who receives it may at his option treat it as either. Therefore, an instrument which is in the form of a note, but which in addition is addressed to a third person, who accepts it, is a promissory note, and may be so declared on: Brazelton v. MacMurray, 44 Ala., 323.

6 Wilson v. Collingridge, 9 C. B., 570 (67 E. C. L. R.).

7 Yates v. Nash, 29 L. J., C. P., 306; &c. B., N.S., 581 (98 E. C. L. B.).

### --- Page 0139 ---

82 FORM OF BILLS AND NOTES,

though he be not named.’ But if the bill get into the hands of a wrong payee, unless it be payable to bearer, he can neither acquire nor convey a title. One Christian drew a bill on the defendant in London, payable to Henry Davis. The bill got into the hands of another Henry Davis than the one in whose favor it was drawn, was accepted by the defendant, and by the wrong Henry Davis was indorsed to the plaintiff. Held that the indorsement of his own name by Henry Davis was, under the circumstances, a forgery, and (dissentiente Lord Kenyon) could convey no title to the plaintiff. If the name be spelt wrong, parol evidence is admissible to show who was intended. If there be father and son of the same name it will be intended payable to the father till the contrary appear. But if the son be found in possession of the note, and he indorse, that is evidence that he, and not the father, is payee. A note payable to A., or to B. & C., or his or their order, is not a promissory note within the statute. A note in this form—“15 l. 5 s. balance due to A. C., I am still indebted, and do promise to pay,” or in this—“Received of A. B. 100 l., which I promise to pay on demand, with lawful interest,” sufficiently designates the payee. A note payable “to the trustees acting under A.’s will” is a good note, and parol evidence is admissible to show who the trustees are and what are the trusts.

1 Storm v. Sterling, 3 E. & B., 832 (77 E.C. L. R.); Cowie v. Stirling, 6 E.&B., 333 (88 E.C. L. R.).

2 Mead v. Young, 4 T. B., 28. Every negotiable note must be negotiated by the person (or his representatives) to whom the note was made payable, and not by a person of the same name: Foster v. Shattuck, 2 N. H., 446. It is competent for the holder of a promissory note or other instrument to declare upon it as a promise made to himself in a name different from his own, and to prove that he was the person intended: Patterson v. Graves, 5 Blackf. (Ind.), 593; Jester v. Hopper, 8 Eng. (Ark.), 43.

3 Willis v. Barrett, 2 Stark., 29 (3 E.C. Lb. &.).

4 Sweeting v. Fowler, 1 Stark., 106 (2 E.C. L. R.); Wilson v. Stubbs, Hobart, 330; see Bro. Ab. Addition, 18, 34, 43, 9 to 6; 13 Dyer, 5.

5 Stebbing v. Spicer, 19 L. J., C. P., 24; 8 C. B., 827 (65 E.C. L. R.), S. c.

6 Blanckenbagen v. Blundell, 2 B. & AL., 417.

7 Chadwick v. Allen, 1 Stra., 706.

8 Green v. Davies, 4 B. & C., 235 (10 F.C. L. R.); 6 D. & B., 306, S. c.

9 Meeginson v. Harper, Tyr. 96; 2 C. & M., 322, S. c. The name of the payee need not be inserted in the bill if he be so certainly described as to be easily ascertained: Adams v. King, 16 Hl., 169. A note payable to the administrator of A.’s estate is a good promissory note: Moody v. Threlkeld, 13 Ga., 50. To hold one liable as the drawer of a bill his name must be either inserted in it or subscribed to.
