### --- Page 0423 ---

292, NOTICE OF DISHONOR.

eral indorsements notice of the dishonor be given by the holder to his immediate indorser, and to him only, but an unbroken chain of notices, each given in due time, hang regularly from indorsee to indorser, back to a distant indorser or to the drawer, the latter is liable either to his indorser or to the holder. Thus, where all the parties lived in London, and the holder on the day of dishonor gave notice to the fifth indorser, and the fifth on the following day to the fourth, he on the day after to the third, the third on the next day to the second, and the second on the following day to the first, it was held in an action by the second against the first indorser that due notice had been given. And it would also have been sufficient in an action by the holder at the time of dishonor, against the fifth indorser, and in an action by the fifth indorser against the first.

But if there be any laches in the circulation of the notice back through the several parties, even though the neglect of one be compensated by the extraordinary diligence of another, laches once committed discharges all the antecedent parties, and subsequent notices are invalid, for they are given by parties who are no longer liable on the bill. “It is not enough that the drawer or indorser receives notice in as many days as there are subsequent indorsers, unless it is shown that each indorsee gave notice within a day after receiving it; as if any has been beyond the day, the drawer and prior indorsers are discharged.” Nor

1 Hilton v. Shepherd, 6 East, 14, n.

2 Smith v. Mullett, 2 Camp., 208; Marsh v. Maxwell, 2 Camp., 210; Jameson v. Swinton, 2 Camp., 373; 2 Taunt., 224, a.c.; Wilson v. Swabey, 1 Stark., 34 (2 E. C. L. R.).

3 Harrison v. Ruscoe, 15 L. J., Exch., 10; 15 M. & W., 231, s.c.

4 Per Lord Ellenborough, in Marsh v. Maxwell, 2 Camp., 210, n.; Smith v. Mullett, 2 Camp., 208. See Rowe v. Tipper, 13 C. B., 249 (76 E. C. L. R.).

Where there are several successive indorsers of a bill of exchange or promissory note, whether the indorsements be upon actual negotiation for value or for the purpose of collection only, the holder may send notice of its dishonor to his immediate indorser; and if that indorser, after receiving such notice, give seasonable notice to his immediate indorser, the latter is liable to his immediate indorser though he does not receive notice as soon as if it were transmitted to him by the holder immediately upon the dishonor; and so of each successive indorser: Evcle Bank v. Hathaway, 5 Met. (Mass.), 212; Butler v. Duval, 4 Yerg. (Tenn.), 205; Furwer v. Rand, 16 Me., 453. Each party has a full day to give notice, but the over diligence of one shall not be made to supply the want of diligence of another: Brown v. Ferguson, 4 Leigh. (Va.), 37; Simpson v. Tierney, 5 Hump. (Tenn.), 419; Am. L. Ins. & T. Co. v. Emerson, 4 Sm. & M. (Miss.), 177; Safford v. Wrehoff, 1 Tilla (N.Y.), 113; Whitman v. Farmers’ Bank, 8 Port., 258; Etting v. Schuylkill Bank, 432.

<br>

### --- Page 0424 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR. 243

Can a party in such a case, by waiving his own discharge, waive the discharge of antecedent parties? Defendant was the eighth, plaintiff the eleventh, indorser of a bill. The instrument passed through several subsequent hands, was dishonored at maturity, and returned to the immediate indorsee of the plaintiff. It remained in his hands three days, and then the plaintiff paid it, and gave notice to the defendant, who received the notice in a shorter interval from the day of dishonor than would have elapsed had each party through

© Bair, 355; Smith v. Roach, 7 B. Mon. (N.Y.), 17; Carmena v. Bank of La., 1 La. Ann., 369; Crocker v. Getchell, 22 Me., 392; Manchester Bank v. Fellows, 32 N.H., 302; Fitchburg Bank v. Perley, 2 Allen (Mass.), 433. An indorser is not bound to notify his prior indorser, even though he knows of the protest, until he receives notice himself in due course: West River Bank v. Taylor, 7 Bos. (N.Y.), 466. Notice of the protest of a bill may be transmitted through the several indorsers to the drawer; and though the route be circuitous, and delay may be occasioned, yet such notice, sent with due diligence throughout, will render the drawer and all the indorsers liable: Triplett v. Hunt, 3 Dana (Ky.), 128. A notice given by the holder to the several indorsers enures to the benefit of the indorsers or preceding parties, so that the first indorser who has received notice of its non-payment from the holder but not from the second indorser, is liable to the second indorser in the same manner as though notice had been received from him: Marr v. Johnson, 9 Yerg. (Tenn.), 1. An agent of the holder is allowed one day to give notice to his principal of a default, and the principal is entitled to one day, after he receives notice, to give or forward notice by mail to the drawer or indorser: Ellis v. The Comm. Bank, 7 How. (Miss.), 294; Crawford v. Branch Bank, 7 Ala., 205; Ohio L. Ins. & T. Co. v. McCague, 18 Ohio, 54; Hill v. Planters’ Bank, 3 Hump. (Tenn.), 670; McNeil v. Wyatt, Id., 125; Foster v. McDouald, 3 Ala., 34; Carmena v. Bank of La., 1 La. Ann., 369; Colt v. Noble, 5 Mass., 167; Church v. Barlow, 9 Pick. (Mass.), 547; see Johnson v. Hart, 1 Bailey (S.C.), 482; U.S. Bank v. Goddard, 5 Mass. (U.S.), 366; Fish v. Jackman, 19 Me., 467; Lawson v. Farmers’ Bank, 1 Ohio St., 206; U.S. v. Barker, 2 Paine (U.S. C.C.), 340. Neglect to give notice to the first indorser does not discharge a subsequent indorser who had notice: Matthews v. Fogg, 1 Rich. (S.C.), 369; Wilcox v. Mitchell, 4 How. (Miss.), 272; Baker v. Morris, 25 Barb. (N.Y.), 133; Struthers v. Blake, 30 Penn. St., 189. A failure to notify other indorsers, although the attempt is made, will not discharge one who is himself properly notified: Westfall v. Farwell, 13 Wis., 504. The holder of a note is required to charge with notice only the indorser to whom he desires to look for payment, and it belongs to each indorser to see for himself that prior indorsers are duly fixed if he would have his remedy over against them: Spencer v. Bullon, 18 N.Y., 327. Notice to the drawer by the acceptor enures to the benefit of the indorsers: Brailsford v. Williams, 15 Md., 150. Where the holder transmits to the last indorser notice of non-payment, and the latter on receipt of it sends notice to each of the prior parties, such notice is sufficient to charge each of them: Renshaw v. Triplett, 3 Mo., 213. See further, West River Bank v. Taylor, 7 Bos. (N.Y.), 466; S.C., 34 N.Y., 128; Terbill v. Jones, 15 Wis., 203; Wamesit Bank v. Buttrick, 11 Gray (Mass.), 387; Linn v. Horton, 17 Wis., 151; Haskell v. Beardman, 8 Allen (Mass.), 38. The discharge of an indorser by not giving him notice does not discharge the other indorsers. The holder is only bound to give notice to the indorser whom he intends to hold liable: Crane v. Trudeau, 19 La. Ann., 307.

§ 433

<br>

### --- Page 0425 ---

294 NOTICE OF DISHONOR.

whose hands the bill was returned taken the full time allowed by law for giving notice. Abbott, C. J.: “In this case the plaintiff was clearly discharged by the laches of the holder. Then can he, by paying the bill, place the prior indorsers in a worse situation than that in which they would otherwise have been? I think he cannot do so, and that in paying this bill he has paid it in his own wrong, and cannot be allowed to recover upon it against the defendant.”

As notice may be given by leaving it at the counting-house, so notice to an agent for the general conduct of business is sufficient notice to the principal. But notice to a man’s attorney or solicitor is not sufficient. A verbal message left at the drawer’s house with his wife has been held sufficient. “A person, not a merchant,” says Bolland, B., “who draws a bill of exchange, undertakes to have someone at his house to answer any application that may be made respecting it when it becomes due.”

If the drawer of a bill become bankrupt, notice must nevertheless be given to him, whether a trustee has been appointed or not. If the bankrupt have absconded, there being as yet no assignees, and a messenger be in possession, notice should be given to the messenger and to the petitioning creditor.

If the party be dead, notice should be given to his personal representatives.

4 Turner v. Leach, 4 B. & Ald., 451 (6 E. C. L. R.).

2 Cross v. Smith, 1 M. & Sel., 045,

3 Ibid.

4 Housego v. Cowne, 2 M. & W., 348.

5 Ex parte Baker, L. R., 4 Ch. D., 795; Ex parte Moline, 19 Ves., 216; Rhode v. Proctor, 4 B. & C., 517 (10 E.C. L. R.); 6 D. & Ry., 610, 6.0.5) Ex parte Johnson. 3 Deac. & Chitty, 433; 1 Mont. & Ayr., 622; Ex parte Chappell, 3 M. & Ayr., 490; 3 Dew., 298, 8, etc.

6 So in Scotland notice must be given to the party who represents the estate. (Thomp., 535.)

I am aware of no actual English decision to this effect, but it has been decided so in America, and if there be no personal representatives, a notice sent to the residence of the deceased party’s family is sufficient: Merchant’s Bank v. Birch, 17 John’s Rep., 25; Bayley, American ed., 418.

The administrator of an indorser appointed before the maturity of the note, who has given due notice of his appointment, is entitled to notice: Oriental Bank v. Blake, 22 Pick. (Mass.), 206. See as to notice where the indorser is dead, Planters’ Bank v. White, 2 Humph. (Tenn.), 112; Cayuga Bank v. Bennett, 5 Hill (N. Y.), 236; Barnes v. Reynolds, 4 How. (Miss.), 114. A notice addressed through mail in due time to the “legal representative” of A., deceased, the indorser,

<br>

### --- Page 0426 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR, 295

Where a bill is accepted payable at a particular place, it is not necessary, in an action against the acceptor, to have given him notice of the dishonor. “Bills of Exchange,” says Abbott, C. J., “of late years have been made payable by the acceptor, either at the houses of his friends or agents, they being expressly named in the acceptance, or at banking houses, or at houses merely described by their number in a certain street. It is most convenient that the same rule should be laid down as applicable to all these cases. The most plain and simple rule to lay down is this: that the effect of an acceptance in any of these forms is a substitution of the house, banker or other person therein mentioned, for the house or residence of the acceptor, and, consequently, that the presentment at the house or to the party named in the acceptance is equivalent to presentment at the house of the acceptor. This rule will, I think, be equally applicable to the case of every acceptance, and will be convenient and advantageous to the public.’ A fortiori it is unnecessary to have given the acceptor such a notice in any action against the drawer.”  
Where partners are jointly liable on the bill, notice to one is sufficient.

to the last residence of the deceased, is sufficient, though it does not appear that the administrator or executor ever received it: Pillow ct. Hardeman, 3 Humph. (Tenn.), 538. Notice to an executor before probate is sufficient: Shoenberger v. Lancaster Savings Institution, 28 Penn. St., 459. The death of the maker, and the fact that the indorser is his executor, does not dispense with the necessity of notice. As executor he is not personally bound, and the purpose of demand and notice is to make him so, and to warn him of the fact that the holder looks to him individually: Grota v. Gyger et al., 81 Penn. St., 271.

1 Treacher v. Hinton, 4 B. & Ald., 413 (6 E. C. L. R.); Smith v. Thatcher, 4 B. & Ald., 200 (6 E. C. L. R.); Pearse v. Pemberthy, 3 Camp., 261.

7 Edwards v. Dick, 4 B. & Ald., 212 (6 E. C. L. R.).

5 Porthouse v. Parker, 1 Camp., 83; Bignold v. Waterhouse, 1 M. & Sel., 259. But it is conceived that notice to a private member of a joint stock banking company would not suffice. See Powles v. Page, 3 C. B., 16 (54 E. C. L. B.); In re Carew, 31 Beav., 39.

Mr. Justice Story doubts whether, in the case of joint parties not partners, notice to one only would bind even him: Story on Promissory Notes, p. 36. Joint owners of a note, who jointly indorse the same, do not thereby constitute themselves partners quasi hoc, so that notice of the dishonor of a bill to one will charge both. Both must have notice: Sayers v. Frick, 7 W. & S. (Penn.), 383; Wills v. Green, 5 Hill (N. Y.), 232. But where they are partners notice to one is sufficient; Bouldin v. Page, 24 Mo., 594; Miser v. Trovinger, 7 Ohio St., 281. Where a partnership indorses a note, notice of its dishonor given to a surviving partner is sufficient to hold the legal representatives of the deceased partners, although the holder knew of the decease of such partner before the maturity of the note: Dabney v. Stidger, 435

<br>

### --- Page 0427 ---

296 NOTICE OF DISHONOR.

If a man, not a party to a bill, assigns without indorsement, he is not entitled to notice of dishonor.

[206] And, as a general rule, a man transferring by delivery without indorsement a bill or note payable to bearer is not entitled to notice.

We have already seen that a transferor by mere delivery of a negotiable instrument, made or becoming payable to bearer, is not in general liable, either on the instrument or on the consideration. He therefore (unless in some excepted cases) requires no notice of dishonor.

But we have also seen that if the bill or note payable to bearer were delivered on account of a pre-existing debt, that delivery is not prima facie a sale of the bill or note. On dishonor, therefore, of the bill or note, the liability of the transferor for the original debt revives. But in such a case the transferee will have made the bill or note his own, unless he have given due notice of dishonor.

And we have further seen that as there may be an express contract that the instrument shall not amount to payment if dishonored, so there are many circumstances from which a jury may infer that the intention and understood contract of the parties was that the instrument was not to be payment if dishonored.

It is conceived that in all cases where in consequence of the dishonor of bills or notes, made or becoming payable to bearer, a remedy

4 S. & M. (Miss.), 749; Cocke v. Bank, 6 Hump. (Tenn.), 51. See People’s Bank v. Keech, 26 Md., 521. The rule that service of notice upon one member of a firm is sufficient does not hold where a notice is served on a partner living elsewhere, while none is served on a partner residing in the town where the demand is made: Hume v. Watt, 5 Kan., 34.

1 Van Wart v. Woolley, 3 B. & C., 439 (10 B.C. L. R.); 5 D. & R., 374; M. & M., 520, s.c.; Swinyard v. Bowes, 5 M. & Scl., 62. But a notice has been held to be in time, although an allowance be made for its transmission through a party not indorsing. See Clode v. Bayley, 12 M. & W., 51.

A party who purchases a bill and transmits it on account of goods ordered by him without indorsing it is not entitled to notice of its dishonor: Van Wart v. Smith, 1 Wendell, 219.

2 Ante, chapter on TRANSFER.

3 “The person,” says Abbott, C. J., “who delivers a bill to another, without indorsing his own name upon it, he does not subject himself to the obligations of the law merchant; he cannot be sued on the bill, either by the person to whom he delivers it or by any other. And as he does not subject himself to the obligations we think he is not entitled to the advantages. If the holder of a bill sell it without his own indorsement, he is, generally speaking, liable to no action in respect of the bill. If he deliver it without his indorsement upon any other consideration, antecedent

436

<br>

### --- Page 0428 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR. 297

arises on the consideration, the transferror is entitled to notice of dishonor.

A man merely guaranteeing the payment of a bill, but not a party to it, is not discharged by the neglect of the holder to give him notice of dishonor unless he has been actually prejudiced by such neglect.

And though a man indorse a bill, yet if he also give a bond conditioned for its payment, absence of due notice of dishonor is no plea to an action on the bond.

Let us now inquire, seventhly, what are the consequences of neglect to give due notice. The law presumes that if the drawer has not had due notice, he is injured, because otherwise he might have immediately withdrawn his effects from the hands of the drawee, and that if the indorser has not had timely notice, the remedy against the parties liable to him is rendered more precarious. The consequence, therefore, of neglect of notice is that the party to whom it should have been given is discharged from all liability, whether on the bill or on the consideration for which the bill was paid.

Or concomitant, the nature of the transaction and all circumstances regarding the bill must be inquired into in order to ascertain whether he is subject to any responsibility. If the bill be delivered and received as an absolute discharge, he will not be liable; if otherwise, he may be. The mere fact of receiving such a bill does not show it was received in discharge: Van Wart v. Woolley, 3 B. & C., 445 (10 E. C. L. R.).

There is great confusion in the cases on this subject, but the authorities are canvassed in the judgment of Mr. Justice Coleridge in Turner v. Stones, 1 Dowl. & L., 131. That learned judge says, "I think the obligation on the holder is to give notice promptly to the party from whom he receives the note."

Warrington v. Furbor, 8 East, 242; 6 Esp., 89, s. c.; Phillips v. Asting, 2 Taunt., 205; Swinyard v. Bowes, 5 M. & S., 62; Holbrow v. Wilkins, 1 B. & C., 10 (8 E. C. L. R.); 2 D. & Ry., 59, s. c.; Van Wart v. Woolley, 3 B. & C., 439 (10 E. C. L. R.); 6 Dowl. & B. R. 374; M. & M., 220, 5. c.; Walton v. Mascall, 13 M. & W., 72; Hitchcock v. Humphrey, 5 M. & Gr., 559 (44 E. C. L. R.); Donley v. Camp, 22 Ala., 639. See Buckner v. Liebig, 38 Mo., 188; Baker v. Robinson, 63 N. C., 191; Brenner v. Weaver, 1 Kan., 488; Pickett v. Hawes, 14 Iowa, 460; Forest v. Stewart, 14 Ohio St., 246; Sibley v. Van Horn, 13 Iowa, 209; Baker v. Kelley, 41 Miss., 696; Weller v. Hawes, 19 Iowa, 443; Foster v. Tolison, 13 Rich. (S. C.), L., 31; Parkman v. Brewster, 10 Gray (Mass.), 271; Second National Bank v. Gaylord, 34 Iowa, 246.

Murray v. King, 5 B. & Ald., 163 (7 E. C. L. R.).

Bridges v. Terry, 3 Taunt., 130. Where the drawer of a check not paid on presentation has been injured for want of notice of such non-payment, he is not thereby discharged from the payment of the whole check, but only to the extent of the actual injury he has sustained by the want of notice: Pack v. Thomas, 13 S. & M. (Miss.), 11. See ante, the difference between checks and other negotiable paper.

<br>

### --- Page 0429 ---

297 NOTICE OF DISHONOR,

The old doctrine on this subject was that it lay on the defendant to prove that he had been injured by the want of notice; but it is now settled that the want of notice is a complete defence, and that evidence tending to show the defendant was not prejudiced by the neglect is inadmissible, except in an action against the drawer who had no effects in the hands of the drawee. And if a man who is discharged for want of notice nevertheless pays the bill, he cannot recover against prior parties. But where an agent drew a bill on his principal for goods bought by the agent for the principal, and the bill was dishonored, of which the agent had no notice, but the agent being afterwards arrested on the bill, paid it, and sued his principal on the contract of indemnity, which the law implies in favor of the agent in such cases; it was held that the agent's not having insisted on the absence of notice as a defence to the action against himself did not preclude him from recovering the amount of the bill against his principal.

But eighthly, and lastly, there are cases in which notice is excused or waived.

[+298] *Notice may be dispensed with and excused by a prior agreement on the part of the party otherwise entitled to it, that it shall not be necessary to give him notice. Thus, where the drawer stated to the holder a few days before the bill became due that he would call and see if the bill had been paid by the acceptor, it was held that he had dispensed with notice.

Where the drawer has countermanded payment, notice of dishonor to him is dispensed with, although it may be still necessary to present.

If the drawer had no effects at any time during the currency of the bills in the hands of the acceptor, and will have no remedy against the acceptor or any other person if he be obliged to pay the bill, he cannot in general have been prejudiced by want of

1 Mogadara v. Holt, 1 Show., 317; 12 Mod., 15, 8. c.  
2 Dennis v. Morrice, 3 Esp., 158; Hill v. D & R. B. N. P. C., 59.  
3 Huntley v. Sanderson, E. C. & M., 467; 3 Tyr., 409, 5. c.  
4 Phipson v. Kneller, 4 Camp., 255; 2 Stark., 116; E. C. L. B., 8. c. See Burgh v. Legee, 5 M. & W., 498; and Brett v. Levett, 13 East, 214; but see Le parte Higvold, 1 Desac., 724; Murray v. King, 5 B. & Ald., 165; E. C. L. B.; Soward v. Palmer, 2 Moore, 274; 8 Taunt., 277 (4 ELC. L. B.), 8. c.  
5 Hill v. Heap, D. & R. B. N. P. C., 57; Prideaux v. Collier, 2 Stark., 9 (3 E. C. L. B.).

<br>

### --- Page 0430 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR. 298

notice, and therefore cannot set that up as a defence.’ But this decision, substituting knowledge for notice, has been much regretted. “I have always thought,” says Abbott, C. J., “that it would have been better never to have considered knowledge as equivalent to notice: I cannot consent to carry the law one step further.” 

‘Therefore it has been held that in order to be liable without notice, the drawer must have had no remedy against the acceptor or any other person. Hence a bill be drawn for the accommodation, not of the drawer, but of the acceptor, as the drawer might sue the acceptor, he is entitled to notice.’ And if the drawer in such a case chooses to pay without notice, he cannot sue the acceptor for money paid to his use, although he may sue on the bill. And *where a bill was [#299] drawn for the accommodation of an indorser, and neither such indorser nor the drawer had any effects in the hands of the acceptor, it was held that a subsequent indorsee, in order to recover against the drawer, was bound to give him notice, for the drawer had a remedy over against his immediate indorser. So

*Bickerdike v. Bollman, 1 T. R., 406; Lafitte v. Slatter, 6 Bing., 623 (19 E. C. L. R.); Carew v. Duckworth, L. R., 4 Ex., 313; Wirth v. Austen, L. R. 10 C. P., 689. As we have already seen, absence of effects will excuse presentment for payment as against the drawer. See p., 220. Hence where a check is dishonored for want of funds, the drawer is chargeable without either presentment for payment or notice of dishonor, unless he had a reasonable expectation that it would be honored. A check dishonored for want of funds may be treated by the holder as a nullity or a fraud, and if the drawer has no funds in the drawee’s hands, the payee may sue immediately after non-acceptance without giving notice: Baker v. Gallagher, 1 Wash. (U.S.C.C.), 461; Reed v. Wilkinson, 2 Wash. (U.S.C.C.), 514; Tarver v. Nance, Ala., 712; Hubble v. Fogartie, 3 Rich. (S. C.), 413. Want of funds is no excuse for want of notice: Howard v. Jarvis, 5 Sneed (Tenn.), 375. As to notice when the drawee has no effects of the drawer in his hands, and is not bound to accept: Durnim v. Hendrick, 4 Tex., 495; Cole v. Wintercost, 12 Tex., 118; Harrison v. Trader, 29 Ark., 85; Schuchardt v. Hall, 36 Md., 590.

* Cory v. Scott, 3 B. & Ald. 619 (5 E.C. L. B.); Carter v. Flower, 16 M. & W., 741.

* Ex parte Heath, 2 Ves. & B., 240; 2 Rose, 141, s.c.; Cory v. Scott, 3 B. & Ald., 619 (6 B.C. L. B.); Bayley, 294, 5th ed.; Sleigh v. Sleigh, 19 L. J., Exch., 345; 5 Exch., 514, s.c.

* Sleigh v. Sleigh, 19 L. J., Exch., 345; 5 Exch., 514, s.c.

6 Norton v. Pickering, 8 B. & C., 610 (15 E.C. L. R.); 3 Man. & R., 23; Dans. & L., 210, s.c.; Cory v. Scott, 3 B. & Ald., 619 (5 E.C. L. R.); overruling Wahwyn v. Quintin, 1 B. & P., 652; and see Brown v. Maffey, 15 East, 216; Ex parte Heath, 2 Ves. & B., 249; 2 Rose, 141, s.c. And the indorser is entitled to notice, although the bill were drawn and accepted and indorsed by him for the purpose of raising funds for a company in which he as well as the holder was a shareholder: Maltasa v. Siddle, 6 C. B., N.S., 494 (95 E. C. L. R.). The indorser of a promissory note for the accommodation of the drawer is entitled to strict notice: French v. Bank of

439

<br>

### --- Page 0431 ---

299 NOTICE OF DISHONOR.

It is no excuse for neglect of notice to an indorser, that the drawer had no effects in the acceptor’s hands. “That circumstance,” says Lord Kenyon, “will not avail the plaintiff—the rule extends only to actions brought against the drawer; the indorser is in all cases entitled to notice, for he has no concern with the accounts between the drawer and the drawee.” Nor will the absence of effects in the hands of the maker of a promissory note be any excuse for want of notice to the indorser, at all events unless the indorser be the person who is to pay, and who has no remedy over against any one; nor will it suffice to allege that he has not been damnified by the absence of notice. An intimation from the drawee that he cannot meet the bill, but that the drawer must take it up, will not relieve the holder from the necessity of giving the drawer notice. But if the acceptor give the drawer money for that purpose, such sum [#300] is recoverable from the drawer by the holder as money paid to his use. Though the acceptor, at the time of dishonor, have no effects of the drawer in his hands, yet, if he ever

Columbia, 4 Crench. (U.S.), 141; Bogg v. Keil, 1 Mo., 743; Holland v. Turver, 10 Conn., 308; Rea v. Dorrence, 18 Me., 137. The maker of a note for the accommodation of the payee is not released by the failure to protest the note and give him notice, though it is known to the holder that he is an accommodation maker: Hansbrough v. Gray, 3 Gratt. (Va.), 356; Lewis v. Hanchman, 2 Penn. St., 416. An accommodation drawer of a bill of exchange is entitled to notice of its dishonor, although he had no funds in the hands of the drawee: Sherrod v. Rhodes, 5 Ala., 683; Reid v. Morrison, 2 W. & S. (Penn.), 401; Evans v. Norris, 1 Ala., 511; Shirley v. Fellowes, 9 Porter, 300. An indorser is chargeable without notice if he indorsed for the drawer’s accommodation only, and had no expectation that the drawee would pay: Farmers’ Bank v. Van Meter, 4 Rand, (Va.), 553. No notice of non-payment of a note is required from an accommodation indorser to those for whose accommodation he indorsed in order to hold them liable to him: Fulton v. Maccracken, 18 Md., 528. Demand and notice are not necessary to fix drawer for whose use the bill was accepted and indorser: Barbaroux v. Waters, 3 Met. (Ky.), 304. An indorser for whose accommodation the maker signs the note is not entitled to notice of non-payment: First National Bank v. Ryerson, 23 Iowa, 503. Draft drawn by agent as principal drawer not entitled to notice: Raymond v. Mann, 45 Tex., 301. Two firms with common partners, notice of dishonor not necessary: N.Y. Co. v. Selma Bank, 51 Ala., 305.

Wilks v. Jacks, Peake, 202; Fuster v. Parker, L.R., 2 C.P., Div. 18. But if the indorser have had funds put into his hands by the drawer, out of which he is to pay the bill, notice to the indorser is unnecessary: Corney v. Mendez de Costa, 1 Esp., 902; Carter v. Flower, 16 M. & W., 751. Where the intention of the parties to an accommodation bill was that the last indorser should meet it, the previous indorsers were held entitled to notice: Turner v. Sampson, L.B., 2 Q.B.D., 23.

Carter v. Flower, 16 M. & W., 751.

Stabbs v. O’Rines, 1 Esp., 332.

Baker v. Birch, 3 Camp., 107.

440

<br>

### --- Page 0432 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR, 300

had any after the drawing of the bill, or if, without effects, the drawer had any reasonable ground for expecting that the bill would be honored, he is entitled to notice. "The case of Bickerdike v. Bollman," says Lord Ellenborough, "went upon the ground that the drawer had no effects in the hands of the drawee at the time of the bill drawn, and the other cases followed on the same ground. But no case has gone the length of extending the exemption further to cases where the drawee had effects of the drawer in his hands at the time of the bill drawn, though the balance might vary afterwards and be turned into the opposite scale. When there are no effects of the drawer in the hands of the drawee at the time when the bill is drawn, the drawer must know that he is drawing on accommodation; but, if he have effects at the time, it would be very dangerous and inconvenient, merely on account of the shifting of a balance, to hold notice not to be necessary. It would be introducing a number of collateral issues in every case upon a bill of exchange to examine how the account stood between the drawer and the drawee from the time the bill was drawn down to the time it was dishonored." Where the drawer had goods in the hands of drawees to the amount of 1,500l., but owed them 10,000l., and the drawees had appropriated the goods to the satisfaction of the debt, it was held that notice of dishonor to the drawer was still essential. Lord Ellenborough observing: "If a man draws upon a house with whom he has no account, he knows that the bill will not be accepted; he can suffer no injury from want of notice of its dishonor, and, therefore, he is not entitled to such notice. But the case is quite otherwise where the drawee has a fluctuating balance in the hands of the drawer. There notice is peculiarly requisite. Without this how can the drawer know that credit has been refused him and that his bill has been dishonored? It is said here that the effects in the hands of the drawees were all appropriated to discharge their own debt; but that appropriation should appear by writing, and the defendant should be a party to it."

And, in general, though the drawer had no effects in the hands of the drawee, yet, if he had any reasonable ex-

1 Orr v. Marinnis, 7 East, 359; 2 Smith, 328, &c.; Legge v. Thorpe, 12 East, 171; Brown v. Matty, 15 East, 216; Hammond v. Dufrene, 3 Camp., 145; Thackray v. Blackett, 3 Camp., 164.

2 Query, as to the necessity of a writing.

3 Blackburn v. Doren, 2 Camp., 503.

<br>

### --- Page 0433 ---

301 NOTICE OF DISHONOR.

Expectation that the bill would be honored, he is entitled to Notice of dishonor, as if he have consigned goods to the drawee, though, in fact, they never came to hand, or accepted bills for him. So where it, being indebted to the drawer, represented to him that A. owed him money, and the drawer in consequence drew a bill on A., which A. accepted but did not pay, it was held that the drawer was entitled to notice of dishonor; for he had reason to expect either that B. would take up or that the acceptor would pay the bill, and might by want of notice be induced to relax in his endeavors to procure payment of the debt owing by B. But the drawer of a bill, who has no effects in the hands of the drawee, except that he has supplied him with goods on credit, which credit does not expire till long after the bill becomes due, is not entitled to notice, for the goods are not such as can properly be set against the drawing, nor can there be any reasonable expectation that the bill will be paid till the expiration of the credit.

Legge v. Thorpe, 12 East, 171; Rucker v. Hiller, 16 East, 43; 3 Camp., 217.8, c.; Spooner v. Gardiner, 1 B. & M., 84; Walwyn v. St. Quintin, 1 Bos. & Pul., 652; Ex parte Heath, 2 Ves. & B., 240.

Lafitte v. Slatter, 6 Bing., 623 (19 B.C. L. R.); 4 Z. & P., 457, s.c. The burden of proving that the defendant has been injured by receiving no notice, where that is alleged, but where it is proved that he had no funds in the hands of the acceptor, lies on the defendant: Fitzgerald v. Williams, 6 Bing., N.C., 68 (387 ELC. L. R.); 8 Scott, 271, s.c.

Claridge v. Dalton, 4 M. & Sel., 226. As to the form of the allegation in pleading, see Thomas v. Fenton, 16 L. J., Q. B., 362; 5 D. & L., 23, s.c.

If the drawer had no effects in the hands of the drawee at the date of the bill, and no reasonable ground to expect it would be honored, he is chargeable without notice: Hopkirk v. Page, 2 Brock. (U.S.C.C.), 20; Eichelberger v. Finley, 7 Ill. & J. (Md.), 381; Warder v. Tucker, 7 Mass., 452; Valk v. Simmons, 4 Mass. (U.S.), 113; Cathell v. Goodwin, 1 H. & G. (Md.), 468; Hoffman v. Smith, 1 Caines, 157; Savage v. Merle, 5 Pick., 88; Armstrong v. Gay, 1 Stew. (Ala.), 175; Dolifus v. Frosch, 1 Den. (N.Y.), 367; Foard v. Womack, 2 Ala., 368; Kinsley v. Robinson, 21 Pick. (Mass.), 327; Cook v. Martin, 5 S. & M. (Miss.), 379; Spear v. Atkinson, 1 Tred. (N.C.), 262; Rhett v. Poe, 2 How. (U.S.), 437. In the matter of Brown, 2 Story (U.S. C.C.), 502; Stewart v. Desha, 11 Ala., 844; Younge v. Ruff, 3 Strobh. (S.C.), 811; Richie v. McCoy, 13 S. & M. (Miss.), 541. The drawer is entitled to notice of the dishonor of a bill if he had reasonable ground to believe it would be honored, though he had no funds in the drawee’s hands: Austin v. Rodman, 1 Hawks (N.C.), 195; Stanton v. Blossom, 14 Mass., 116; French’s Ex. v. Bank of Columbia, 4 Cranch (U.S.), 141; Robinson v. Ames, 20 Johns., 146; Grosvenor v. Stone, 8 Pick. (Mass.), 83; Campbell v. Pettenyill, 7 Me., 126; Hills v. Norris, 2 S. & P. (Ala.), 124. Though the drawer has no funds in the hands of the drawee and no ground to expect the bill to be honored, yet the indorser is entitled to notice in all cases, unless he has received funds from the drawer to take up the bill: Scarborough v. Harris, 1 Bay, 442.

<br>

### --- Page 0434 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR. 892

If the drawer of a bill make it payable at his own house, this is evidence to go to the jury that it is a bill drawn for the accommodation of the drawer himself, of the dishonor of which it is not necessary to apprise him. "I cannot understand," says Lord Tenterden, "why the drawer should with his own hand make the bill payable at his own house, unless he was to provide payment of it when at maturity."

Ignorance of the party’s residence will excuse neglect to give

(8.C.), 178; Barton v. Baker, 15 S. & R. (Penn.), 331; Warder v. Tucker, 7 Mass., 452; Fotheringham v. Price's Ex., 1 Bay (S. Ca.), 200; Denniston v. Imbrie, 3 Wash. C. C., 401; Randolph v. Darieux, 4 Id., 61; Walker v. Walker, 2 Eng. (Ark.), 542. An indorser of a promissory note who, before the note falls due, takes an assignment of all the property and estate of the maker, for the express purpose of meeting his responsibilities, is not entitled to the usual notice of non-payment: Mechanics’ Bank v. Griswold, 7 Wend. (N.Y.), 165; Barton v. Baker, 15 S. & R. (Penn.), 334; Coddington v. Davis, 3 Denio, 610 (N.Y.), 610; Duvall v. Farmers’ Bank, 9 G. & J. (Md.), 31. The mere taking of security by an indorser from the maker of the note does not dispense with a demand and notice, unless sufficient funds have come into his hands to satisfy the note, or all the property of the maker has been transferred to the indorser: Spencer v. Harvey, 17 Wend. (N.Y.), 489; Marine Bank v. Smith, 18 Me., 99; Cramer v. Perry, 17 Pick. (Mass.), 332; Woodman v. Eastman, 30 N.H. 359; Durham v. Price, 5 Yerg. (Tenn.), 300; Watkins v. Crouch, 5 Leigh (Va.), 522; Watt v. Mitchell, 6 How. (Miss.), 131; Barrett v. Charlestown Bank, 2 McMaull. (S.C.), 191; Kramer v. Sanford, 4 W. & S. (Penn.), 328; Burrows v. Hannegan, 1 McLean (U.S. C.C.), 309; Stephenson v. Primrose, 8 Port. (Ala.), 155; Kyle v. Green, 14 Ohio, 495; Denny v. Palmer, 5 Tred. (N.C.), 610; Develing v. Ferris, 18 Ohio, 170. The burden of proof is on the holder of a bill to show that the drawer had no funds in the drawee’s hands in order to excuse want of notice: Baxter v. Graves, 2 Mar. (Ky.), 152; Ralston v. Bullitt, 3 Bibb (Ky.), 261; Thompson v. Stewart, 3 Conn., 172. Where the indorser has discharged the maker of a note from liability by assignment and release, a notice of non-payment would be of no use to him, and therefore he is not entitled to it: Burk v. McKay, 2 How. (U.S.), 66.

See, also, Hawes v. Austin, 35 Ill., 396; Lawrence v. Schmidt, Id., 440; Commercial Bank v. Barksdale, 36 Mo., 553; Merchants’ Bank v. Easley, 44 Mo., 286; Mehlberg v. Fisher, 54 Wis., 607; Walker v. Rogers, 40 Ill., 278; McRae v. Rhodes, 22 Ark.; Sullivan v. Deadman, 23 Id., 14.

An accommodation drawer is entitled to notice, even though he had no funds in the hands of the drawee: Merchants’ Bank v. Easley, 44 Missouri, 286. The drawer is entitled to notice of dishonor when by the course of business between him and the drawee he had a right to expect that the bill would be paid, as well as where he had funds in the drawee’s hands at the time of drawing: Louisiana State Bank v. Buhler, 22 La. Ann., 83; Dunbar v. Tyler, 44 Miss., 1. Evidence that on presentation the drawer said that he had no money to pay will not be sufficient to excuse notice: Mehlberg v. Fisher, 54 Wis., 607.

Sharp v. Bailey, 9 B. & C. 417 E.C.L.R.; 4 M. & R. 480. Query, whether a notice of dishonor be necessary where the drawer dies before maturity, and an indorser is sued who is the drawer’s executor. See Caunt v. Thompson, 18 L. J., C.P., 127; 7 C.B., 400 (62 E.C.L.B.).

<br>

### --- Page 0435 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR,

notice of dishonor, so long as that ignorance continues without neglecting to use the ordinary means for acquiring information.

“It would be hard,” observes Lord Ellenborough, “when the holder of a bill does not know where the indorser is to be found, if he lost his remedy by not communicating immediate notice of the dishonor of the bill, and I think the law lays down no such rigid rule. The holder must not allow himself to remain in a state of passive and contented ignorance; but, if he uses reasonable diligence to discover the residence of the indorser, I conceive that notice given as soon as this is discovered is due notice of the dishonor of the bill within the usage and custom of merchants.” Where the holder, in order to discover the residence of the indorser, had merely made inquiries at a certain house where the bill was made payable, Lord Ellenborough said, “Ignorance of the indorser’s residence may excuse the want of due notice, but the party must show that he has used reasonable diligence to find it out. Has he done so here? How should it be expected that the requisite information should have been obtained where the bill was payable? Inquiries might have been made of the other persons whose names appeared on the bill, and application might have been made to persons of the same name with the defendant, whose addresses are set down in the directory.” Due diligence has, however, been held to be a question of fact. After the residence of the party is discovered, the holder has the same time to give notice as he would have had in the first instance.

1 Bateman v. Joseph, 2 Camp., 463; 12 East, 433, S.C.; Browning v. Winnear, Gow, 81; Harrison v. Fitzhenry, 3 Esp., 240; Baldwin v. Richardson, 18 B. & C., 240 (5 E. Cl. R.); 20. & B.R., 285, S.C. In this last case the traveller of a tradesman received in the course of business a promissory note, which was afterwards dishonored. The principal, not knowing the address of the next preceding indorser, wrote to his traveller to inquire into it, and several days elapsed before he received an answer. He then gave notice, and it was held sufficient. See Chapwait v. Curlewis, 2 Moo. & Rob., 254.

2 Beverage v. Burgis, 3 Camp., 262.

3 Bateman v. Joseph, 12 East, 438; 2 Camp., 463, S.C.; Hilton v. Shepherd, 6 East, 14, U.; Siggers v. Browne, 1 M. & Rob., 629; Hewitt v. Thompson, 1 M. & Rob., 543. In these two last cases the letters containing notice of dishonor had miscarried, and the jury were directed to consider whether the generality or indistinctness of the description which the defendant had given of himself in the bill had led the plaintiff into error.

4 Firth v. Thrush, 8 B. & C., 387 (15 E.C.L.R.); 2 M. & B., 357; Dans. & L., 151, S.C.; Allen v. Edmundson, 17 L. J., Exch., 291; 2 Exch., 719, S.C.; Dixon v. Johnson, 1 Jur., N.S., 70. Where the holder of a bill of exchange, after the exer-

Nemo ad impossibile tenetur; and therefore it should seem on general principles that the death or dangerous illness of the holder

of due diligence to ascertain the residence of the indorser, sends him a notice of the dishonor of the bill, and afterwards discovers that he was not rightly informed, and ascertains the true residence, it is not necessary for him to send another notice: Lambert v. Ghiselin, 9 How. (U.S.), 552.

What is diligence? See Shepard v. Citizens’ Ins. Co., 8 Mo., 272; Planters’ Bank v. Bradford, 4 Humph. (Tenn.), 39; Brener v. Wightman, 7 W. & S. (Penn.), 264; Rhett v. Poe, 2 How. (U.S.), 157; Carroll v. Upton, 2 Sandf. (N. Y.), 171; Rawdon v. Redfield, ibid., 178; Carroll v. Upton, 3 N. Y., 272; Lambert v. Ghiselin, 9 How. (U.S.), 552; Johnson v. Lewis, 1 Dana (Ky.), 182; Davis v. Herrick, 6 Ham. (Ohio), 55; Bank of Columbia v. Lawrence, 1 Pet. (U.S.), 578; Van Hosen v. Van Alstyne, 3 Wend. (N. Y.), 75; Sice v. Cunningham, 1 Cow. (N. Y.), 397; Nash v. Harrington, 1 Aiken (Vt.), 39; Bronaugh v. Scott, 5 Call (Va.), 78; Harris v. Robinson, 4 How. (U.S.), 386; Pierce v. Pendar, 5 Mete., 352; Wheeler v. Field, 6 Mete. (Mass.), 200; Thorn v. Rice, 15 Me., 263; Spencer v. Bank, 3 Hill (N. Y.), 520; Winans v. Davis, 18 N.J.L., 276; Hoopes v. Newman, 28 & M. (Miss.), 71; Godley v. Goodloe, 6 Ibid., 255; Remer v. Downer, 23 Wend. (N. Y.), 620; 25 Id., 277; Belden v. Lamb, 17 Conn., 441; Haly v. Brown, 5 Penn. St., 178; Ashley v. Gunton, 15 Ark., 415; Porter v. Judson, 1 Gray (Mass.), 175; Moore v. Hardcastle, 11 Md., 486; Newberry v. Trowbridge, 4 Mich., 391; Linderman v. Guldin, 34 Penn. St., 54; Boyd v. City Savings, 15 Gratt. (Va.), 601; Middletown Bank v. Morris, 23 Barb. (N. Y.), 616; Heiss v. Corcoran, 15 La. Ann., 694; Early v. Preston, 1 P. & H. (Va.), 223; Adams v. Leland, 5 Bos. (N. Y.), 411; Brighton Bank v. Philbrick, 40 N. H., 506; Blodgett v. Durgin, 32 Vt. 361; Libby v. Adams, 32 Barb. (N. Y.), 542. The holder is presumed to know the residence of his immediate indorser, and is bound to inform the notary or agent employed to give notice: Lawrence v. Miller, 16 N. Y., 235. Due diligence of notary is not sufficient if the holder knew: Randall v. Smith, 34 Barb. (N. Y.), 452. It is the duty of the holder to inform the notary of the residence of the maker and indorser; and if unknown to the holder, he must inquire of those whose names are on the bill as to the residence he does not know; and if there are none such on the note he must use due diligence to ascertain it: Smith v. Fisher, 24 Penn. St., 222. What is due diligence in giving notice is a question of law when the facts are admitted; when the facts are disputed, the court should give hypothetical instructions, leaving the facts to the jury: Linville v. Welsh, 29 Mo., 203; Wyman v. Adams, 12 Cush. (Mass.), 210. See further as to what is due diligence both in making demand and giving notice: Howland v. Adrian, 30 N. J. L., 41; First National Bank v. Needham, 29 Iowa, 219; Nat. Bank of the Metropolis v. Williams, 46 Mo., 17; Boyd v. Orton, 16 Wis., 495; Palmer v. Whitney, 21 Ind., 58; Walker v. Stetson, 14 Ohio St., 89; Bartlett v. Isbell, 31 Conn., 290; White v. Steddard, 11 Gray (Mass.), 258; West River Bank v. Taylor, 7 Bos. (N. Y.), 466; Ray v. Porter, 42 Ala., 327; Tyson v. Union, 43 Id., 455; Alexander v. Parsons, 3 Lans. (N. Y.), 333; Peters v. Hobbs, 25 Ark., 67; James v. Wade, 21 La. Ann., 548; Dunhart v. Tyler, 44 Miss., 1; Durden v. Smith, Id., 548; Earnest v. Taylor, 25 Tex. (Suppl.), 37; Harper v. Kenner, 19 La. Ann., 63; Van Wickle v. Downing, Id., 83; Holtz v. Buppe, 37 N. Y., 684; Staylor v. Ball, 24 Md., 183; Garvie v. Dowrie, 33 Cal., 176; Bird v. Heirs of Doyal, 20 La. Ann., 541; Judson v. Goodwin, 37 Ill., 286; Whitridge v. Rider, 22 Md., 548; Johnson v. Bank of N. A., 5 Roberts (N. Y.), 804; Peters v. Hobbs, 25 Ark., 76; Tate v. Sullivan, 40 Md., 464; Mitchell v. Young, 21 La. Ann., 279; Puig v. Carter, 20 La. Ann., 414; Jarvis v. Garnett, 29 Mo., 268, 445

<br>

### --- Page 0437 ---

303 NOTICE OF DISHONOR,

or his agent, or other accident not attributable to the holder’s negligence, rendering notice impossible, may excuse it! But where an indorser left home on account of the dangerous illness of his wife at a distance, and a letter containing notice of dishonor of a bill lay unopened at his shop during his absence, till after the proper time for giving his indorser notice, Lord Ellenborough held that these circumstances afforded no excuse for the delay.

Where a bill is drawn by several persons upon one of themselves, since the acceptor is likewise a drawer, notice of dishonor is superfluous, as the dishonor must be known to one of them, and the knowledge of one is the knowledge of all.

The death, bankruptcy or insolvency of the drawee, however notorious, constitutes no excuse for neglect of notice.

Nor an agreement or understanding between the parties that the instrument shall not be payable till after a certain event.

Notice of dishonor need not be given if the bill be on an insufficient stamp.

Cessation of mails and commercial intercourse during the civil war is a sufficient excuse for want of notice: House v. Adams, 12 Wright, 201; Apperson v. Brynn, 5 Cold. (Tenn.), 341; Morgan v. Bank of Louisville, 4 Bush. (Ky.), 82; Apperson v. Union Bank, 4 Cold. (Tenn.), 480.

1 Poth., 144; Pardessus du Contrat de Change, 426; Thompson, 483, 548.

2 Turner v. Leach, Chit. 5th ed., 330. A state of war between the country of the drawer and that of the drawee will excuse notice, but it must be given within a reasonable time after peace: Hopkirk v. Page, 2 Brock. (U.S. C.C.), 20. The blockade of a city is a sufficient excuse for not sending notice: House v. Adams & Co., 48 Penn. St., 261.

3 Porthouse v. Parker, 1 Camp., 82. But in case of fraud a different rule would prevail: Bignold v. Waterhouse, 1 M. & Sel., 259. And it may be doubtful how far this rule would hold in the case of a joint stock company. If the drawer be a partner of the firm on which the bill is drawn, the holder need not prove notice to him of its dishonor: Gowan v. Jackson, 20 John. (N.Y.), 176; see Dwight v. Seavil, 2 Conn., 654. Where co-partners purchase goods together and give a note therefor, with one of them as maker and the other as indorser, the latter is not liable on his indorsement, unless he be duly notified of the dishonor of the note: Foland v. Boyd, 22 Paun. St., 476; Morris v. Husson, 4 Sandf. (N.Y.), 93. “This is not like the case where a note has co-partners for the makers, and some of them for indorsers, and where of course the knowledge of the dishonor by the makers is chargeable on them as indorsers”: Lowrie, C. J.

Russell v. Lanstaffe, 2 Dong., 514; Esdaile v. Sowerby, 11 East, 114; Boultbee v. Stubbs, 18 Ves., 21; but see 3 Brev. C.C., 1. The insolvency and absconding of the drawee are no excuse for not giving notice to the indorser: May v. Coffin, 4 Mass., 341; Barton v. Baker, 1 S. & R. (Penn.), 334; Gibbs v. Cannon, 9 S. & R. (Penn.), 201; Hunt v. Wadleigh, 25 Me., 271; and see McClellan v. Clark, 2 Brev. (S.C.), 106; Kiddell v. Ford, 3 Id., 178; Lawrence v. Langley, 14 N.H., 70.

5 Free v. Hawkins, 8 Taunt., 92 (4 F.C.R., L.R.); 1 Moore, 28, 8, c.

6 Cundy v. Marnott, 1 B. & Ad., 696 (20 B.C.L.R.), 446

<br>

### --- Page 0438 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR. 304

Nor to the indorser of a promissory note not negotiable.  
The consequence of neglect of notice will be waived by a subsequent promise to pay. And a payment of part or an acknowledgment of liability, though after action brought, will be evidence of notice.*  
It makes no difference that such promise, payment or acknowledgment was made under a misapprehension of the law, for every man must be taken to know the law;° otherwise a premium is held out to ignorance, and there is no telling to what extent this excuse might be carried. But if the promise or acknowledgment  

1 Plimley v. Westley, 2 Bing., N.C., 249 (29 F.C. L. R.); 2 Scott, 423; 1 Hodges, 324, s.c. The indorsement of a note not negotiable is a collateral and not an original undertaking. If by the payee he will be holden as indorser, but if by a person not a party to the note, as guarantor, but in both cases demand and notice is necessary to hold the indorser: Parker v. Riddle, 11 Ohio, 102. If a note is indorsed, however long a time after it becomes due, the indorsee is bound to prove a demand and notice in an action against the indorser: Berry v. Robinson, 9 Johns. (N. Y.), 121; Stackman v. Riley, 2 McCord (S. C.), 398; Allwood v. Haselden, 2 Bailey (S. C.), 457; Poole v. Jolleson, 1 McCord (S. C.), 199; Rugeby v. Davidson, 2 Rep. Con. Ct. (S. C.), 33; Dwight v. Emerson, 2 N. H., 159; Keenan v. McRea, 3 S. & P. (Ala.), 249; Benton v. Gibson, 1 Hill, S. C., 56; Greeley v. Hunt, 20 Me., 455; Colt v. Barnard, 18 Pick., 260; Kirkpatrick v. McCullough, 3 Humph. (Tenn.), 171; Kennon v. McRae, 7 Port. (Ala.), 45; Chadwick v. Jeffers, 1 Rich. (S.C.), 397; Bean v. Arnold, 16 Me., 251; Williams v. Probst, 10 Watts (Penn.), 111; Matthews v. Fogg, 1 Rich. (S. C.), 369; Gray v. Bell, 2 Id., 67; 3 Id., 71; Sanborn v. Southard, 25 Me., 409; Branch Bank v. Gaffney, 9 Ala., 153.

2 Vaughan v. Fuller, 2 Stra., 1246; Horford v. Wilson, 1 Taunt., 12; Lundy v. Robertson, 7 East, 231; 3 Smith, 225, s.c.; Brett v. Levett, 13 East, 213; Wood v. Brown, 1 Stark, 217 (2 E. C. L. R.); Hopes v. Adler, 6 East, 16, n.; Dennis v. Morrice, 3 Esp., 158; Rogers v. Stephens, 2 T. R., 713; Dixon v. Elliott, 5 C. & P., 437 (24 E. C. L. R.); Margetson v. Aitken, 3 C. & P., 338 (14 E. C. L. R.); Dans. & L., 157, & c.; Lecaan v. Kirkman, 6 Jur., N. S., 17.

3 Hopley v. Dufresne, 15 East, 275.

* Many of the cases cited below fail in drawing the proper distinction between the effect of a promise as a waiver of notice and its effect as evidence of notice. In Kilby v. Rochussen the court held a subsequent promise to be sufficient evidence of due notice, but would have amended if necessary by adding an averment of waiver: 18 C. B., 357 (36 E.C. L. B.). Given v. Bank, 85 Ill., 442; Smith v. Lowusdale, 6 Or., 78; Tardy v. Boyd, 26 Gratt. (Va.), 631; Knapp v. Runals, 37 Wis., 135; Rindge v. Kimball, 124 Mass., 209; Hayes v. Werner, 45 Conn., 246; Britt v. Lawsop, 15 Hann. (N. Y.), 123; Smalley v. Wright, 40 N. J. Law, 471; Moyer’s Appeal, 87 Penn. St. 129; Boyd v. Bank, 32 Ohio St., 526; Hale v. Danforth, 46 Wis., 554; Bryant v. Wilcox, 49 Cal., 47; Bank v. Conoway, 4 Houst. (Del.), 206; Freeman v. O'Brien, 36 Iowa, 406; Ray v. Smith, 17 Wall. (U.S.), 411; Armstrong v. Chadwick, 127 Mass., 156; Williams v. Bank, 9 Heisk. (Tenn.), 441. Waiver of demand and notice by payee above his indorsement will hold good as to all who follow: Parsley v. Heath, 69 Me., 90. Or, more correctly speaking, ignorance of the law cannot excuse.

° Bilbie v. Lumley, 2 East, 469.

<br>

### --- Page 0439 ---

30-4 NOTICE OF DISHONOR.

be made under a misapprehension of fact, as if the bill had been presented for acceptance and acceptance have been refused, a promise to pay in ignorance of that circumstance, is no waiver of the consequence of laches. But a promise to pay will entirely dispense with proof of presentment or notice, and will throw on the defendant the double burden of proving laches, and that he was ignorant of it... Where it is only as to part of the sum, the plaintiff can only avail himself of it as a waiver pro tanto. A drawer of a bill for 200£, who had not received due notice of dishonor, said, “I do not mean to insist on want of notice, but I am only bound to pay you 70£." Abbott, C. J.: “The defendant does not say that he will pay the bill, but that he is only bound to pay 70£. I think the plaintiff must be satisfied with the 70£." The acknowledgment or promise may be made by the attorney for the defendant, or by his clerk, who has the management of the case. It need not be made to the plaintiff, but may be made to another party to the bill, or to a stranger. A promise to pay made by the drawer in expectation that a bill will be dishonored, but before it is dishonored, does not dispense with notice for it is to be understood as a promise on condition that due notice is given.

It seems, however in some recent cases to have been considered that a promise to pay is only evidence from which a jury may presume that a notice has been received. But that is not so. A

1 Goodall v. Doley, 1 T. R., 712; Blesard v. Just, 5 Burr, 2672; Wme Wast, Bartholomew, 1 B. & P., 326; Stevens v. Lynch, 2 Camp. 332; 12 East, 32, s.

2 Taylerr. Jones, 2 Camp., 105; Stevens v. Lynch, 12 East, 33; 2 Camp., 392. See instances of promises held insufficient in Dennis v. Morrice, 3 Esp., 158; Cumming v. French, 2 Camp., 106, n.; and see Rouse v. Redwood, 1 Esp., 156; Standage v. Creighton, 5 C. & P., 406 (21 E.C. L. R.); and Borradaile v. Lowe, 4 Tann., 93, where it is said that an indorser can only be rendered liable by an express promise; and see Pickin v. Graham, 1 Cro. & Mee., 725; 3 Tyr., 923, s. c.

3 Fletcher v. Froggatt, 2 C. & P., 569 (12 E. C. L. R.).

4 Standage v. Creighton, 5 C. & P., 106 (24 E. C. L. R.).

5 Potter v. Rayworth, 13 East., 417; Gunson v. Metz, 1 B. & C., 193 (S. E. C. L. B.); 2 P. & Ry., 334, s. c.; Fletcher v. Froggatt, 2 C. & P., 569 (12 E. C. L. R.). In Rabey v. Gilbert it was held that suffering judgment by default in an action at the suit of a second indorsee was evidence of notice or of a waiver of notice in an action by the first indorsee: Rabey v. Gilbert, 30 L. J., Exch., 171; 6 H. & N., 536, s. c.

6 Pickin v. Graham, 1 C. & M., 725; 3 Tyr., 923, s. c.; and see Prideaux v. Collier, 7 Stark, N. P. C., 57 (3 B. C. L. R.), and Baker v. Birch, 3 Camp., 107.

7 Hicks v. The Duke of Beaufort, 4 Bing. N. C., 229 (38 E. C. L. R.); 5 Scot. 598, s. c.; and see Booth v. Jacobs, 3 Nev. & M., 351; Pickin v. Graham, 1 Cro. & Mee.,

NOTICE OF DISHONOR, 3805

promise to pay, if made before the time for giving notice has expired, is a dispensation; if made after that time it is a waiver, independently of any question of actual notice.

Z.: 3 Tyr., 923.8. c.; but see Lundie v. Robertson, 7 Fast, 231; 3 Smith, 225, 8. c., Haddock v. Bury, 7 Fast, 236, n.; Anson v. Bayley, B. N.P., 276; Hopley v. Dufresne, 15 Enst, 275; Norris v. Solomanson, 4 Scott, 257; where the defendant said he had no intention but to pay the Hill, and should not avail himself of the informality of the notice, held evidence to go to the jury of notice: Bronwell v. Bonney, 1 Q. B., 39 (41 E.C., G.B.R.).

Cordery v. Colville, 32 L. J., C. P., 211; 14 C. B., N.S., 374 (108 E.C. L. R.), 6. c.; Woods v. Dean, 32 L. J., Q. B., 1; 3 Best & Smith, 104 (113 E.C. L. B.), s. c.; Kilby v. Rochussen, 18 C. B., 357 (86 E.C. L. R.). When it appears that the holder of a negotiable paper has been guilty of laches in an action against an indorser or drawer, the holder cannot recover on a subsequent promise without showing that it was made with full knowledge of the laches; but where the fact of laches does not appear, a promise after maturity to pay the bill is presumptive proof of demand and notice: Tebbets v. Dowd, 23 Wend., 369. A promise made by the drawer to the payee to pay the same, after legal discharge by want of notice of its dishonor, is if made with full knowledge of the facts, binding upon him: Cram v. Sherburne, 14 Me., 43; Walker v. Walker, 2 Eng. (Ark.), 542; Hopkins v. Liswell, 12 Mass., 52; Martin v. Ingersoll, 8 Pick. (Mass.), 1; Beck v. Thompson, 4 Ah. & J. (Md.), 531; Ladd v. Kenney, 2 N. H., 310; Thornton v. Wynn, 12 Wheat. (U.S.), 183; Robbins v. Pinkard, 58 & M. (Miss.), 51; Moore v. Tucker, 3 Ired. (N.C.), 347; Gardiner v. Jones, 2 Murph. (N.C.), 429; Barkalow v. Johnson, 1 Harrison, 397; Farrington v. Brown, 7 N.H., 71; Davis v. Gowen, 17 Me., 387; Baltin v. Betcke, 11 Iowa, 204; Porter v. Hadenpuy, 9 Mich., 11; Tabey v. Berly, 26 Ill., 426; Campbell v. Varney, 12 Iowa, 43; Blodgett v. Durgin, 32 Vt., 361; Loose v. Loose, 43 Penn. St., 533; Golladay v. Bank, 2 Head (Tenn.), 57; Landrum v. Trowbridge, 2 Met. (Ky.), 281; Edwards v. Tandy, 36 N.H., 540; Sigerson v. Matthews, 2 How. (S.C.), 496. Admission by indorser that he had received notice and would have to pay is sufficient: Long v. Crawford, 48 Md., 220. A part payment, a promise to pay, or an acknowledgment of liability by the indorser of a promissory note, after the note becomes due, is prima facie evidence not only of notice but of presentment: Bank of the U.S. v. Lyman, 20 Vt., 668; Bibb v. Peyton, 118 & M. (Miss.), 275; Ridgway v. Day, 13 Penn. St., 205. A part payment of a note by the indorser, not explained or qualified by any accompanying circumstances will be held sufficient evidence of waiver of notice. But where the payment is made with the money of the maker and by his request, the indorser acts as mere agent of the maker, and the transaction is so qualified and explained as to preclude all idea of an actual or intended waiver on the part of the indorser: Whitaker v. Morrison, 1 Branch, Fla., 25.

A promise in ignorance of the fact that no notice has been given will not be sufficient: Crain v. Colwell, 8 Johns. (N.Y.), 384; Jones v. Savage, 6 Wend. (N.Y.), 632; Otis v. Vick, Walker v. ; Miller v. Hadley, Anth. (N.Y.), 68; Fleming v. McClure, 1 Brev. (S.C.), 428; Hunt v. Wadleigh, 23 Me., 271; Warder v. Tucker, 7 Mass., 419; Freeman v. Boynton, Id., 483; Garland v. Salem Bank, 9 Id., 465; May v. Coffin, 4 Id., 341; Otis v. Hussey, 3 N. H., 346; Trimble v. Thorn, 16 Johns. (N.Y.), 152; Kennon v. McRea, 7 Port. (Ala.), 175; U.S. Bank v. Southard, 17 N.J.L., 473; Spurlock v. Union Bank, 4 Hump., 336. Whether particular conversations amount to a waiver of notice of refusal to accept is a question for the jury: Carmichael v. Pennsylvania Bank, 4 How. (Miss.), 567. If the indorser, after the maturity of the...

<br>

### --- Page 0441 ---

306 NOTICE OF DISHONOR,

“Though a party may waive the consequence of laches in respect of himself, he cannot do so in respect of antecedent parties.”

[*306]

Hill, even supposing himself liable to pay the same, takes security from the maker, this will not amount to a waiver of the objection of want of due presentment or notice; but the indorser will be deemed to have taken the security merely contingently in cases of his ultimate liability: The Otsego Co. Bank v. Warren, 18 Barb. (N. Y.), 290. An agreement by the drawer and indorser with the holder before the bill is due that the holder should take any security that the acceptor could give, or make any arrangement he might deem proper to receive payment, without waiving their liabilities, was held not to dispense with demand and notice: Bank v. Spell, 2 Hill (N. Y.), 366; Carter v. Burley, 8 N. H., 558; Creamer v. Perry, 17 Pick. (Mass.), 382. A declaration by an indorser to a third person that he would pay the note without suit is no waiver of demand and notice: Allwood v. Haseldon, 2 Bailey (S. C.), 457; see Robbing v. Pinckard, 58 S. & M. (Miss.) 51. A waiver of notice of a demand does not dispense with the demand itself: Backus v. Shiphard, 11 Wend. (N. Y.), 629; Buchanan v. Marshall, 22 Vt., 561; Drinkwater v. Tibbits, 17 Me. 16. A waiver of protest held to be a waiver of demand and notice: Coddington v. Davis, 1 N. Y., 186; see Wall v. Bay, 1 La. Ann., 312; Scott v. Greer, 10 Penn St., 103. A waiver of protest by an indorser is not a waiver of notice: Ball v. Greaud, 14 La. Ann., 305. See on the subject of waiver of demand and notice, Russell v. Cronkhite, 32 Barb. (N. Y.), 232; Power v. Mitchell, 7 Wis., 161; Edwards v. Tandy, 36 N. H., 340; Amoskeag Bank v. Moore, 37 Id., 539; Byram v. Hunter, 36 Me., 217. The clearest evidence is necessary to show a waiver of demand and notice: Oswego Bank v. Knower, Hill & Denio (N. Y.), 122.

Part payment by one joint indorser with a full knowledge of all the facts is a waiver of the want of due notice, the other being notified: Sherer v. Eastern Bank, 7 Cusey 134. By indorsing the note “A. C. accountable,” defendant waived demand and notice: Furbear v. Caverly, 12 N. H., 74. An indorser, over whose name is written “without demand or notice,” is holden as an original promisor: Lowell v. Gage, 33 Me., 35. The indorser of a note does not waive demand and notice by taking a mortgage to secure his liability: Seacord v. Miller, 15 N. Y., 55. After the indorser has been fixed by demand, protest and notice, mere forbearance of the holder to bring his action will not discharge an indorser: Ashley v. Gauton, 10 Ark., 415. When an indorser of a note took wrongful possession of the note he was held not entitled to notice: Havens v. Talbott, 11 Ind., 323.

Waiver or promise by indorser after discharge, with full knowledge of the facts: Butler v. Murrison, 18 La. Ann., 363; Hazelton v. Colburn, 1 Robt. (N. Y.), 35; Gawtry v. Doane, 48 Barb. (N. Y.), 148; Baskerville v. Harris, 11 Miss., 685; Van Wickle v. Downing, 19 La. Ann., 83; Ford v. Dallam, 3 Cold. (Tenn.), 67; Walker v. Ragers, 40 Ill., 278; Blum v. Bidwell, 20 La. Ann., 43; Hazard v. White, 26 Ark., 155; Janes v. Wade, 21 La. Ann., 543; Cheshire v. Taylor, 29 Iowa, 492; Matthews v. Allen, 42 Mass., 594; Salisbury v. Renick, 44 Mo., 554; Stone v. Smith, 30 Tex., 196; Harrison v. Bailey, 99 Mass., 620; Lewis v. Brehme, 33 Md., 416; Hughes v. Bowen, 15 Iowa, 446; Keys v. Fenstermaker, 24 Cal., 329; Arnold v. Dresser, 8 Allen (Mass.), 435; Croghan v. Dinsmore, 9 Bosw. (N. Y.), 453; Morgan v. Peet, 32 N. Y., 201.

Raseow v. Hardy, 12 East 434; Turner v. Leach, 4 B. & Ald., 451 (6 B.C. 1 Ro); Marsh v. Maxwell, 2 Camp., 210, n.; and see ante, p. 229. As to what will be a waiver of demand and notice, see Fisher v. Price, 37 Ala., 407; Jaceard v. Anderson, 30 Mo., 91; Airey v. Person, Id., 424; Sheldon v. Horton, 43 N. Y., 93; Carpenter v. Reynolds, 450.

<br>

### --- Page 0442 ---

NOTICE OF DISHONOR, 306

No laches can be imputed to the crown; and therefore, if a bill be seized under an extent before it is due, the neglect of the officer of the crown to give notice of the dishonor will not discharge the drawer or indorser. A prior dispensation with notice, as absence of effects, must be specially alleged. So must the impossibility of giving notice, or any other excuse for not giving it. And a subsequent promise, when used as a waiver of notice, must also be specially pleaded. But a subsequent promise to pay, when used as evidence of the fact of notice, need not.

After the bill is due, a promise to pay, or a part of payment, or the offer of it, or any admission of liability, whether before or after the period for giving notice has expired, is prima facie evidence of notice; but though there be no evidence to repel the inference, the jury are not bound to draw it. A letter from the defendant containing no promise of payment, but merely an ambiguous allusion to the bill being dishonored, was held sufficient to warrant the jury in finding that the defendant had received due notice of dishonor. And the sending a person by the defendant,

307 NOTICE OF DISHONOR.

The drawer, to a remote indorsee two days after the bill had become due, to inform him, that he, the drawer, had been defrauded of the bill, and that he should defend any action upon it, was left by Lord Tenterden to the jury as evidence to prove notice of dishonor.[*307] And a statement by the defendant that he should pay the bill, and not avail himself of the informality of the notice, has been held to be evidence of due notice. And a conditional promise to pay, although the condition be not complied with, is still evidence.

Notice to produce a notice of dishonor is not necessary.
