### --- Page 0460 ---

INTEREST, 322

In a declaration or plea, grounded on the statute of 12 Anne, st. 2, c. 16, it was not necessary to negative the exception introduced by the 2 & 3 Vict., c. 37. The exception must have come from the other side.

And in stating that exception it lay on the party introducing it to aver not only that the contract was after passing of the statute of Victoria, but that it did not relate to the land.

1 Thibault v. Gibson, 12 M. & W., 88, Washbourne v. Burrows, 16 L. J., Exch., 266; 1 Exch., 107, 8. c 469

<br>

### --- Page 0461 ---

CHAPTER XLXIV.

[323]
OF THE ALTERATION OF A BILL OR NOTE,

Effect of Alteration at Common Law, . . . 323  
Of Deeds, . . . 323  
Of Bills and Notes, . . . 327  
UNDER THE STAMP ACTS, . . 325  
Where an Alteration will not vitiate, . . . 325  
Before Bill issued, . . . 326  
In Correction of Mistake, . . 327  

When the Alteration of the Instrument extinguishes the Debt, . 303  
Renewal of altered Bill, . . . 328  
When Alteration need not be pleaded, . . . 329  
When it must be pleaded, . . . 329  
Requisites of Plea, . . . 329  
BURDEN OF PROOF, . . . 329  

In treating of the alteration of a negotiable instrument, we will consider the effect of alteration; first, at common law; and, secondly, under the Stamp Acts.  

First, at common law. If a deed, well and sufficiently made in its creation, shall be afterwards altered by erasure, interlining, addition, drawing a line through the words, though they be still legible, or by writing new letters upon the old in any material place or part of it, either by the party that hath the deed, or any other whomsoever, unless the alteration be by him who is bound by the deed (for he shall not take advantage of his own wrong), or by his consent, the deed has lost its force, and is become void.  

And by recent solemn decision, a deed, bill of exchange, promissory note, guaranty, or any other executory written contract, is avoided by an alteration in a material part, made while it is in the custody of the plaintiffs, although that alteration be by a stranger.  

1 Sheppard’s Touchstone, 68. And a deed is not, it seems, vacated at common law if the alteration, though material, were with the consent of all the parties: Markham v. Gonaston, Cro. Eliz., 627; Zouch v. Clay, 2 Lev., 85; Com. Dig. Fait, F. 1.  

2 Davidson v. Cooper, 11 M. & W., 773; affirmed in error, 13 M. & W., 343; Bank of Hindustan v. Smith, 35 L J.C. P., 241. An alteration by a stranger, though material, will not render the instrument inoperative: Nichols v. Johnson, 10 Conn., 192; Medlin v. Platt Co., 8 Mo., 235; Davis v. Carlisle, 5 Ala., 707; Ford v. Ford, 17 Pick. (Mass.), 418; Waring v. Smyth, 2 Barb. Ch. (N.Y.), 119; Lee v. Alexander, 9 B. Mon. (Ky.), 25; Labbering v. Kohlbrecher, 22 Mo., 596. Alteration by payee's wife, without his knowledge, avoids the note: Morrison v. Welty, 18 Md., 169. An alteration by the agent of the maker in good faith does not avoid it: Van Brunt v. Egoff, 95 Barb. (N.Y.), 501.

<br>

### --- Page 0462 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE. 323

For a person who has the custody of an instrument is bound to preserve it in its integrity. And as it would be avoided by his

Where a note or bill is complete and perfect on its face when delivered, if material additions are made thereto or alterations are made therein: even though by a person to whom it has been entrusted by the maker to be delivered to the payee, it is thereby invalidated, even in the hands of an innocent holder for value; and this is so even though the alterations or changes were made by taking advantage of spaces which have been left in the note or bill by the maker thereof: Wood v. Steele, 66 Wall. (U.S.), 80; Worrall v. Gheen, 39 Penn. St., 388; Greenfield Sav. Bank v. Stowell, 123 Mass.,196; McGrath v. Clark, 56 N. Y., 34; Goodman v. Eastman, 4 H., 455; Holmes v. Trumper, 22 Mich., 427; Burrows v. Klunk, 3 L. R.A. (Md.), 576; Bank v. Lockwood, 13 W. Va., 416; Knoxville Nat. Bank v. Clark, 51 Iowa, 264; Trigg v. Taylor, 27 Mo., 246.

There are a class of cases, however, in which it has been held that where the maker of the note has been guilty of negligence leaving spaces which makes it possible for alterations and changes to be made without rendering it obvious that such changes have been made, that note is not invalidated because of the negligence of the maker in that respect. This doctrine is predicated upon the principle that where one of two innocent parties must suffer, that one must suffer whose negligence has permitted the third party to commit the wrong: Young v. Grote, 4 Bing., 253; Scotland Co. Bank v. O'Connell, 23 Mo., App., 165; Blakely v. Johnson, 13 Bush. (Ky.), 196; Isnard v. Torries, 10 La. Ann., 103; Vanduser v. Howe, 21 N. Y., 531; Garrard v. Hadden, 67 Penn. St., 82.

But there is a strong disposition on the part of the Courts to repudiate this doctrine and the doctrine of the case of Young v. Grote, ante, which is generally relied upon as an authority for this rule, has been seriously questioned if not entirely overruled by the English Courts. See Baxendale v. Bennett, L. & E., 3 Q. B., Div., 520; also, Bank of Ireland v. Evans, 5 H. W. L. C., 389.

Where blanks are left in a note or bill by the maker, the holder of such an obligation may fill the same, as the holder is treated as being invested by the maker with the power to make the instrument complete and perfect and to insert anything therein essential to make it so: Angle v. N. W. L. Ins. Co., 92 U. S., 392; Vanduser v. Howe, 21 N. Y., 531; Page v. Morrell, 3 Keys. (N. Y.), 117; Kitchen v. Place, 41 Barb. (N. Y.), 465; Weyerhauser v. Dunn, 100 N. Y., 155.

In the case last cited the form of the note in question as signed by the indorser gave no indication that it was to draw interest at all and left no blank for that purpose. At its commencement, in the place usually occupied by the date, a blank was left between the word “Indianapolis” and the figures “1850.” Another blank existed at the beginning of the note before the words “after date.” The printed form ran on the usual way until a remaining blank was left between the words “to the order of” and the word “dollars,” which ended the body of the note. The Court held that the holder of the note was justified in inserting in the note the name of the payee and the principal sum to be paid, but was not authorized to write in after the word “dollars” the words “with interest” as there was nothing after the word “dollars” to indicate to the eye that there was any incompleteness in the instrument or that anything had been intentionally or accidentally omitted.

In Cannon v. Grigsby, 116 Ill., 151, the note had a blank after the words “payable at,” which was filled in by the payee by inserting the words “Blandinaville, Illinois.” The rate of interest was also changed, and it was claimed that the partner who signed the firm name to the notes subsequently ratified the alteration and the court so found. It was held that the latter question was one of fact, and

471

<br>

### --- Page 0463 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE,

fraud in altering it himself, so it shall be avoided by his laches in suffering another to alter it.

That the finding of the lower court was conclusive. Contra, and holding that an alteration by one joint maker without the consent of the others invalidates it. See Mersman v. Werges, 112 U.S. 141. Whether it injures or benefits the other maker, see Wood v. Steele, 6 Wall. (U.S.) 80.

As to the effect of filling blanks where there is an obvious omission, see Angle v. N.W. Ins. Co., 92 U.S. 330; Elliott v. Levings, 64 Ill., 213; McGrath v. Clark, 86 N.Y., 84; Kitchen v. Place, 41 Barb. (N.Y.), 462; Redlich v. DoH, 54 N.Y., 234.

The rule may be said to be, that, where a party to a negotiable instrument entrusts it to another, obviously incomplete, the instrument carries on its face implied authority to perfect it by filling the blanks; but he may not make a new instrument by erasing what is written or printed nor by filling blanks repugnant to the plainly expressed intention of the same, as shown by its printed or written terms: Clifford J. in Angle v. Ins. Co.; ante, Goodman v. Louis, 20 How. (U.S.) 361; Banks v. Neal, 22 Id., 108; Bank v. Douglass, 31 Ct. 180.

The party to whom the instrument is entrusted is treated as agent of the maker to fill the blanks necessary to perfect it: Violet v. Patton, 5 Cranch (U.S.), 142.

A material alteration of a note or bill by a party without authority invalidates it and destroys its enforceability as a contract or obligation. Such alterations are treated as fraud in law and rescind all the parties affected thereby: Smith v. Mace, 44 U.S. 553; Porter v. Doby, 2 Rich. Eq. (S.C.) 49; Richmond M'f'g Co. v. Davis, Blackf. (Ind.) 412; Washington Savings Bank v. Cecky, 51 Mo. 272; Wheelock v. Freeman, 13 Pick. (Mass.) 165; Boston v. Benson, 12 Cush. (Mass.) 61; Stoddard v. Pennimore, 108 Mass. 366; Hirschmann v. Budd, L.R., 8 Exch., 171.

A material alteration is one which changes the obligations or relations of the parties to the note or bill or one which changes in some respects the legal effect thereof and it is immaterial whether the change is prejudicial or not: Johnson v. May, 76 Ind. 293; Herrick v. Baldwin, 17 Minn. 209; Creighead v. McLoney, 99 Penn. St. 211; White v. Johns, 24 Minn. 387; Robinson v. Reed, 46 Ia. 219; Laub v. Payne, 46 Ind. 550; Osborne v. Van Houten, 40 Mich. 444; Kelly v. Trumble, 74 Ill. 423; Sharp v. Orme, 61 Ala. 263; Crawford v. Dexter, 5 Sawyer (U.S.C.C.), 201; Burnhane v. Brewster, 79 Ill. 515; Kline v. Raymond, 70 Ind. 271; Rowley v. Jewett, Ind. 492.

In this country, alterations made by a stranger are treated as mere spoliation which does not discharge a party to the instrument and it may be declared on as originally made: Drum v. Drum, 133 Mass. 566; Colston v. Amos, 57 N.Y. 253; Lisle v. Rogers, 18 B. Mon. (Ky.) 523; Davis v. Carlisle, 6 Ala. 707; Union National Bank v. Roberts, 45 Wis. 373; Piersall v. Grimes, 30 Ind. 129; Longenberger v. Croager, 48 Cal. 147; Robinson v. Berryman, 22 Mo. App. 509; Brooks v. Allen, 62 Ind. 401; Lee v. Alexander, 9 B. Mon. (Ky.) 25; Dinsmore v. Duncan, 57 N.Y. 573.

An alteration made by the holder of a note or bill defeats his right to recover thereon: Van Auken v. Hornbeck, 14 N.J.L. 175; First National Bank v. Fricke, 75 Mo. 173; Thompson v. Massey, 41 Ohio St. 307; Brooks v. Allen, 62 Ind. 41.

Fraud is presumed: Love v. Dilley, 64 Md. 235; Eckert v. Pickel, 59 Ill. 545.

The question as to whether the alteration is material is one of law for the court: Steele v. Spencer, 1 Pet. (U.S.) 552; Dowers v. Jewel, 2 N.H. 543; Hill v. Calvin, 4 How. (Miss.) 231; Overton v. Matthews, 35 Ark. 146.

But see Moye v. Herndon, 30 Miss. 110; also Boyd v. Botherson, 10 Wend. (N.Y.) 93, where the question of alteration and its materiality were both left to the jury.

An alteration made merely to correct a mistake is not material; as correcting the

<br>

### --- Page 0464 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE. 324

The rules relating to alteration or erasure of deeds apply (at least for the most part) to other written contracts, and to bills and notes.

Name of one of the parties: Derby v. Thrall, 44 Vt., 413. See also on the general subject, Shepard v. Whetstone, 51 Ia., 497; Jessup v. Dennison, 2 Dis. (Ohio), 1503; Hunt v. Adams, 6 Mass., 519; McRaven v. Crisler, 53 Miss., 542; Driker v. Franz, 7 Bush (Ky.), 243; Ames v. Colburn, 11 Gray (Mass.), 390. An alteration which is both fraudulent and material is held to discharge both the debt and the note: Ballard v. Pravklin Ins. Co., 81 Ind., 239; Meyer v. Hunecke, 55 N.Y., 412; Woodsworth v. Anderson, 63 Ia., 503; Smith v. Mace, 44 N.H., 552; but where the alteration is not fraudulent it does not discharge the debt although it invalidates the obligation: States Savings Bank v. Schafer, 9 Neb., 1; Lewis v. Cramer, 3 Md., 206; Hunt v. Gray, 35 N.J.L., 227; Lewis v. Schenck, 18 N.J. Eq., 459; York v. Jones, 43 N.J.L., 332; Warren v. Leighton, 3 Harr. (Del.), 404; Vogel v. Ripper, 34 DIL, 100; Matteson v. Ellsworth, 33 Wis., 488; Wallace v. Wallace, 8 Brad. (IL), 69; Morrison v. Higgins, 53 Ia., 76; Clough v. Seay, 49 Ta., 111; Sullivan v. Budisall, 63 Ig., 158; Gillett v. Smith, 16 Hun. (N.Y.), 10. And this is so whether the alteration is made by the party himself or by an authorized agent if it was done honestly and in good faith: Nickels v. Johnson, 10 Conn., 192; Langdon v. Paul, 20 Vt., 217; Union Bank v. Cook, 2 Cranch (U.S. C.C.), 218; Park v. Blover, 23 Tex., 469; Reed v. Kemp, 16 I., 445; Major v. Hanson, 2 Biss. (U.S. C.C.), 195; Brownell v. Winnie, 29 N.Y., 400; Pequawket Bridge v. Mathes, 8 N.H., 139; Brennan v. Ayer, 35 N.H., 351; Rhedes v. Castner, 12 Allen (Mass.), 130; Shelton v. Dearing, 10 B. Mon. (Ky.), 405; Broughton v. West, 8 Ga., 248; Montgomery R.R. Co. v. Hurst, 9 Ala., 513; State v. Dean, 40 Mo., 463; Huntington v. Finch, 3 Ohio St., 445. The question as to what presumption is raised from an alteration or interlineation upon the face of a written instrument is one in reference to which the authorities are conflicting and irreconcilable, but the better rule would seem to be that the presumption is that it is a legitimate part of the instrument and was prior to its execution, imposing the burden upon the maker to show that it was altered after delivery: Wilson v. Hayes, Minn. Where a note, bill, or check has been fraudulently raised, the holder who has paid full value for the same is precluded from recovering even the original amount named therein from the maker: Flanagan v. Nat'l Bank of Dover, 13 St. Rep. (N.Y.), 826; Mayer v. Huneke, 55 N.Y., 412; Booth v. Powers, 56 N.Y., 31.

The rule being that when a negotiable instrument constitutes in itself the only obligation existing against its maker, all remedies are lost by its fraudulent alteration and the law will not create a new contract to supply the place of the one destroyed. Ruger, C.J., in Crawford v. West Side Bank, 100 N.Y., 58. See also bearing upon this question: Whitney v. Schneider, 2 Lans. (N.Y.), 478; St. Nicholas Bk. v. Nat'l Bk. of N.Y., 3 Weekly Dig. (Penn.), 583; Goodman v. Eastman, 4 N.H., 455; Holmes v. Trumper, 22 Mich., 427; Greenfield Bk. v. Stowell, 123 Mass., 196; Knoxville Bk. v. Clark, 51 Iowa, 264; Weyerhauser v. Dunn, 100 N.Y., 150; Me. Nat. Bk. v. Nat. City Bk., 59 N.Y., 61; Harris v. Burger, 15 St. Rep. (N.Y.), 339.

As to alteration generally, see Woolfolk v. Bank, 10 Bush (Ky.), 504; Draper v. Wood, 112 Mass., 315; Booth v. Powers, 50 N.Y., 22; Greenfield Bank v. Stowell, 123 Mass., 196; Hewins v. Cargill, 67 Me., 554; Aldrich v. Smith, 37 Mich., 468; Brown v. Straw, 6 Neb., 536; Taddiken v. Cantrell, 69 N.Y., 597; McGuinness v. Bligh, 11 R.I., 1; Page v. Danaker, 43 Wis., 221; Coburn v. Webb, 56 Md., 96; Lemay v. Williams, 32 Ark., 166; Robinson v. Reed, 46 Iowa, 219; Hamilton v. Hooper,

473

<br>

### --- Page 0465 ---

OOT ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE,

Thus, where a bill was drawn payable to A. B., and whilst in his possession the date was altered, and the bill was subsequently indorsed to the plaintiffs for value, it was held that they could not recover against the acceptor... "It seems admitted," says Ash-

Id., 515; Lamb v. Paine, Id., 550; Paramore v. Lindsey, 63 Mo., 63; Townsend v. Wason Co., 10 Neb., 610.

1 An alteration by the payee or holder of a bill or note in any material respect avoids the instrument as to the maker and all parties except the person making the alteration, even in the hands of an innocent indorsee for value: Stephens v. Graham, 7 S. & R. (Penn.), 503; Cloud v. Stout, 5 Litt. (Ky.), 205; Pankey v. Mitchell, L. Breeze (Ill.), 301; Mitchell v. Ringgold, 3 Har. & Johns., 159; Trigg v. Taylor, 27 Mo., 245; Judge v. Gilman, 20 Ill., 237. Even though it could not be detected by the most careful scrutiny: Wade v. Withington, 1 Allen (Mass.), 561. Though by the alteration the day of payment is protracted, the note is nevertheless void: United States Bank v. Russell, 3 Yeates (Penn.), 391; Miller v. Gilleland, 19 Penn. St., 119; Owings v. Arnot, 33 Mo., 406; Lisle v. Rogers, 18 B. Mon. (Ky.), 52. If the note was dated wrong by mistake, yet an alteration without the maker's consent, express or implied, renders it void: Bowers v. Jewell, 2 N. H., 543. See Hooker v. Jamison, 2 W. & S. (Penn.), 433; Henderson v. Wilson, 6 How. (Miss.), 65. Writing in the margin "payable at the Bank of America," held to be a material alteration: Woodworth v. Bank of America, 19 John. (N. Y.), 391; Simpson v. Stackhouse, 9 Penn. St., 166; Hill v. Cooley, 46 Penn. St., 259; Sturgess v. Williams, 9 Ohio St., 248; Southwark Bank v. Gross, 35 Penn. St., 80. An accommodation bill was drawn for the purpose of being discounted at a bank, and at the foot of it was a memorandum signed by the last indorser, directing the proceeds of the bill to be credited to the drawer. On the trial of a suit on the bill by the last against a prior indorser, it appeared that this memorandum had been cut off. It was held that the memorandum was no part of the bill, and that its being taken off in no way affected the rights of the parties to the bill: Hubbard v. Williamson, 29 Tred. (Penn.), 397.

If blank spaces be left to be filled after execution, the consent of the party executing that they shall be afterwards filled is to be implied: Wiley v. Mount, 17 S. & R. (Penn.), 438; Smith v. Crooker, 8 Mass., 633; Jordan v. Neilson, 2 Wash. (U.S.), 164; Boardman v. Gore, 1 Stew. (Ala), 517; Bank v. Curry, 2 Dana (Ky.), 142; Stahl v. Berger, 10 S. & R. (Penn.), 10; Commonwealth Bank v. McChord, 4 Dana (Ky.), 191; Douglas v. Scott, 8 Leigh (Va.), 43; Richmond Mfg. Co. v. Davis, 7 Blackf., 412; Ives v. Farmers’ Bank, 2 Allen (Mass.), 236. In the case of the printed form of the note, filled up by the maker and then indorsed for his accommodation by another, and then altered by the maker to a larger sum, by taking advantage of some vacant space in the form, held that the indorser was only liable for the smaller sum: Werrall v. Gheeve, 39 Penn. St., 388. An alteration which does not vary the meaning of an instrument does not avoid it, though made by the party claiming under it: Nicholls v. Johnson, 10 Conn., 192; Granite R.R. Co. v. Bacon, 15 Pick. (Mass.), 239; Morrill v. Otis, 12 N.H., 466; Pequawket Bridge v. Mathes, G.N. U., 139; Mathis v. Mathis, 6 D. & B. (N.C.), 60; Harris v. Bradford, 4 Ala., 214; Gardner v. Sisk, 3 Penn., 326; Burnham v. Ayer, 35 N. H., 351; Moye v. Herndon, 80 Miss., 110; Humphreys v. Crane, 5 Cal., 173. A promissory note was made payable to a partnership under one name and indorsed by a surety, and was afterwards altered by the maker and payee, without the knowledge of the surety, so as to be payable to the same partnership by a different name; held, in an action by the

<br>

### --- Page 0466 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE. 394

Hurst, J., "that if this had been a deed, the alteration would have 
vitiated it. Now, I cannot see any reason why the principle on 

payee against the surety, that the alteration was immaterial and did not affect the 
validity of the note: Arnold v. Jones, 2 R.B. I., d-to.

In Massachusetts it has been held that the addition of the name of a witness to 
a note, after its delivery, if made without the knowledge or consent of the maker, and 
by one who did not see him sign the note, avoids the note: Homer v. Wallis, 11 
Mass., 209; but an exception is made, when the act is innocently done, when the 
note had been witnessed before: Smith v. Dunham, 8 Pick. (Mass.), 246. In Ford 
v. Ford, 17 Pick. (Mass.), 418, a bond, which required attestation, but which was not 
attested when delivered, was held not to be avoided because the obligee procured a 
person who knew the signature of the obligor, but was not present when it was 
signed, was not thereby avoided.

In Maine Millbery v. Storer, 75 Me., 69, it was held that the putting of the name 
of a witness on the note without the maker's knowledge is not a material alteration, 
when the act was not fraudulent, and was honestly done.

See also Rollins v. Bartlett, 20 Me. 319; Thornton v. Appleton, 29 Me., 293.

But see Brackett v. Mountford, 11 Me., 15, where such an act was held to be such 
an unauthorized alteration as avoided the note.

To say the least, this is a dangerous doctrine, and one which should be very care- 
fully applied. In some of the States, a witnessed note stands upon a different ground 
from one which is not witnessed, particularly so far as the running of the Statute 
of Limitations is concerned. The addition of a name of a party as maker after 
its delivery avoids the note as to the original maker: Nicholson v. Combs, 90 Ind., 
515; Henry v. Coats, 17 Ind., 161; Dowers v. Briggs, 20 Ind., 139; Harper v. State, 
™ Blackf. (Ind), 61; see Marshall v. Gougher, 10 S. & R. (Penn.), 164; Ravises v. 
Alsten, 5 Ala., 297; Stewart v. Preston, 1 Branch (Fla.), 10; Blackwell v. Lane, 4 D. 
& B. O. (?), 118; Adams v. Frye, 5 Met. (Mass.), 103; Hennings v. Werkheiser, 8 
Penn. St., 518; Thornton v. Appleton, 29 Maine, 298; State v. Gherkin, 7 Tred. 
(N.C.), 206. Where the payee wrote his name under the names of the makers, and 
added after his own name the word “security,” it was held to be a material 
alteration: Chappell v. Spencer, 23 Barb. (N.Y.), 584. Alteration by adding "with 
interest," though without fraudulent intent, vitiates the note: Fay v. Smith, 1 
Allen (Mass), 477. If the maker, after it is indorsed, adds "with interest," it avoids 
the note even though the alteration was made before delivery to the payee: Water- 
man v. Vose, 43 Me., 501. A note made by two, subsequently altered by one, is no 
longer binding on the other: Crockett v. Thomason, 5 Sneed. (Tenn.), 42. If the 
payee makes a material alteration, he can neither recover on the note nor on the 
contract for which the note was given: White v. Hass, 32 Alabama, 430. The 
payee of a note given for the price of goods sold, having altered it without fraud, 
may recover on the common counts: Merrick v. Boury, 4 Ohio St., 60.

For cases in which an alteration has been held to be material, see McVean v. 
Scott, 46 Barb. (N.Y.), 379; Hall’s Adm’x v. McHenry, 19 Iowa, 521; Grimes v. 
Piersol, 25 Ind., 246; Hill v. Cooley, 66 Penn. St., 209; Miller v. Reed, 3 Grant, 51; 
Mahaiwe Bank v. Douglass, 31 Conn., 170; Cole v. Hills, 44 N.H., 227; Chadwick v. 
Eastman, 53 Me., 12; Wood v. Steele, 6 Wall. (U.S.), (S.C.), 80; Low v. Argrove, 30 
Ga., 129; Elbert v. McClelland, 8 Bush, (Ky.), 577; Wallace v. Jewell, 21 Ohio St., 
163; Holmes v. Trumper, 22 Mich., 427; Kennedy v. Crandell, 3 Lans. (N.Y.), 1; 
Flint v. Craig, 59 Barb. (N.Y.), 319; Wait v. Pomeroy, 20 Mich., 425; Lincoln v. 
Lincoln, 12 Gray, 45; Kountz v. Hart, 17 Ind., 329; Chism v. Turner, 27 Ark., 108; 
Washington Bank v. Ecky, 51 Mo., 272; Major v. Hanson, 2 Biss. (Ky.), 195; Espy  
475

<br>

### --- Page 0467 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE.

Which a deed would have been avoided should not extend to a case of a bill of exchange. There is no magic in parchment or wax, and the principle to be extracted from the cases is that any alteration avoids the contract. If A. B had brought this action, he could not have recovered, because he must suffer from any alteration of the bill whilst in his custody; the same objection must hold against the plaintiffs who derive title from him. So, where the drawer, without the consent of the acceptor, added to the acceptance the words “Payable at Mr. B.’s, Chiswell Street,” it was held that this was a material alteration, discharging the acceptor. And the same point has been repeatedly decided since the 1 & 2 Geo. 4, c. 78. “Suppose,” says Abbott, C. J., “a bill so altered to be indorsed to a person ignorant of the alteration; his right to sue his indorser would, as the bill appears, be complete upon default made where the bill is payable; whereas, in truth, the acceptor, not having in reality undertaken to pay there, would have committed no default by such non-payment. I am of opinion, therefore, that the alteration is in a material part of the bill, and the acceptor is, in consequence, discharged.”

By, Bank, 18 Wall. (U. S.), 605; Bradley v. Mann, 37 Mich., 1; Cochran v. Nebeker, 48 Ind., 459; Bank v. Dunn, 62 Mo., 79; Bank v. Gay, 63 Mo., 33; Swift v. Barber, 28 Mich., 503; Morehead v. Bank, 5 W. Va., 74; Benedict v. Cowden, 49 N. Y., 396.

Taking the name of additional joint maker of a note, without consent of the previous signers, discharges them; the new maker is bound for the whole note: Hamilton v. Hooper, 46 Iowa, 515.

For cases of immaterial alteration, see McCramer v. Thompson, 21 Iowa, 244; Terry v. Hazlewood, 1 Dav. (Ky.), 104; Levi v. Mendall, Id., 77; Holland v. Hatch, 15 Ohio St., 464; Corcoran v. Doll, 32 Cal., 82; Jones v. Berryhill, 25 Iowa, 239; Todd v. Bank of Kentucky, 3 Bush (Ky.), 626; Smith v. Lockridge, 8 Bush (Ky.), 423; Derby v. Thrall, 44 Vt., 413; Bridges v. Winter, 42 Miss., 135; Houghton v. Francis, 29 Ill., 244; McCaughey v. Smith, 27 N. Y., 39; McRaven v. Crisler, 53 Miss., 542; Hirst v. Wagner, 43 Iowa, 373; Goodenow v. Curtis, 33 Mich., 505; Herrick v. Baldwin, 17 Minn., 209; Hunt v. Gray, 35 N. J. L., 227; Brughaw v. Reddy, 5 Ben. (U. S. D. C.), 266.

1 Mastor v. Miller, 4 T. R., 320; in error, 2 H.BI, 110; Hirschman v. Budd, L. R., 8 Ex., 171; Vance v. Lowther, L. B., 1 Ex. Div., 126. The defence was capable of being raised by a plea traversing the acceptance; but see now Ord. ALX.

2 Cowie v. Halsall, 4 B. & Al., 197 (6 E. C. L. B.); 3 Stark., 36 (36 E. C. L. B.), 8. c.

3 M’Intosh v. Haydon, R. K. & M., 362; Desbrowe v. Weatherby, 1 M. & Rob., 438; 6 C. & P., 758 (25 E. C. L. B.), 3. c.; Taylor v. Moseley, 1 M. & Rob., 439, n.; Semple v. Cole, 8 L. J., Exch., 155. These decisions have been recently under review and confirmed by the Court of Queen’s Bench in Burchfield v. Moore, 23 L. J., Q. B., 261; 3 E. & B., 683 (77 E. C. L. B.), 4 c.; Gardner v. Walsh, 5 E. & B., 83 (85 E. C. L. B.), 476.

<br>

### --- Page 0468 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE. 325

But it has been held by the same learned judge, and by the Court of Exchequer, that a similar addition with the consent of the acceptor would not invalidate the instrument, either at common law or under the Stamp Act. Where a bill was addressed to A. B. & Co., and the acceptance was by A. and B., and the address was afterwards altered to correspond with the acceptance, as the acceptors would be liable either way, the alteration was held to be immaterial. An alteration of a foreign bill, by adding either on the face of the bill or to the indorsements the rate of exchange, according to which the bill is to be paid, is fatal.

The addition of the words “interest to be paid at six per cent. per annum,” written at the corner of the note and not in the body, is a material alteration avoiding the note.

But secondly, even if the consent of all parties have been obtained to an alteration in a material part, such alteration, nevertheless, avoids the bill under the stamp laws; for it is become a new and different instrument, and therefore requires a new stamp; which stamp cannot, as we have seen, then be affixed. Any alteration in the date, sum, or time of payment, the insertion of words rendering negotiable an instrument which before was not so, altering the words “value received” into an expression of the particular consideration which passed, are respectively material alterations, avoiding the bill under the Stamp Acts. But the addition of another name to a joint and several note on a different part of the face of the note,

1 Stevens v. Lloyd, M. & M., 292; and see Jacobs v. Hart, 6 M. & G., 142; Walter v. Cabley, 2 C. & M., 151; but in Walter v. Cubley the attention of the court was not drawn to Gibb v. Mather, 8 Bing., 221 (21 E.C. L. R.); 1 Moore & G., 387; 20 & 5., 244, s.c. Would not the alteration have been material in an action against the drawer? Stevens v. Lloyd, M. & M., 292; and if so, was not the legal effect of the instrument altered?

2 Farquhar v. Southey, M. & M., 17; 2 C. & P., 497 (12 E.C. L. B.), s.c.; Hamelin v. Bruck, 15 L. J., Q. B., 343; 9 Q. B., 306 (43 E.C. L. B.), s.c.

3 Hirshfield v. Smith, 35 L. J., C. P., 177; L. B. & C. P., 340, s.c., though the additions were in red ink.

4 Warrington v. Early, 23 L. J., Q. B., 47.

5 Wilson v. Justice, Bayley, 6th ed., 118; Bowman v. Nichol, 5 T. R., 537; 1 Esp., 81, s.c.

6 Hamelin v. Bruck, 15 L. J., Q. B., 343; 9 Q. B., 306 (38 E. C. L. B.), s.c.

7 Bathe v. Taylor, 15 East, 412; Walton v. Hastings, 4 Camp., 223; 1 Stark., 214 (2 ELC. L. B.), s.c.; Guthwaite v. Luntley, 4 Camp., 179; Knill v. Williams, 10 East, 431. The words “not negotiable” may be added at any time to a crossed check:

<br>

### --- Page 0469 ---

325 ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE,

with the assent of all parties, has been held, ut res magis valeat, to operate as an indorsement.

There are, however, two cases in which an alteration, though in a material part, will not vacate the instrument; first, where such an alteration is made before the bill is issued or becomes an available instrument; and secondly, where the bill is altered to correct a mistake or supply an omission, and in furtherance of the original intention of the parties.

Thus where the drawer of a bill payable to his own order sent it to the drawee for acceptance, and the drawee requested that a longer time might be allowed for payment, and an alteration to that effect was accordingly made with the consent of the drawer, and the bill was afterwards accepted, it was held that the alteration being made

1 Ex parte Yates, 27 L. J., Bank., 9; 2 DeG. & J., 191, &c. But see Gardner v. Walsh, 5 S. E. & B., 83 (85 E. C. L. R.).

41 Catton v. Simpson, 8 Ad. & E., 136 (35 E. C. L. R.); overruled by Gardner v. Walsh, 5 S. E. & B., 83 (85 E.C. L. R.); but see Ex parte Yates, supra, and Dodge v. Pringle, 29 L. J., Exch., 115; Aldous v. Cornwall, L. B., 3 Q. B., 573; 37 L. T., 201; 9 B. & S., 607.

Retracing the name of obligor which has faded or been blotted with ink and obscured does not avoid the obligation: Duan v. Clements, 7 Jones (N.C.) L., 58. To write over with ink words written in pencil is no material alteration: Reed v. Roark, 14 Tex., 29. When a third person who had written the note, and with whom it was left, in good faith changed the date, but on its being disapproved by the maker restored the original date, it was held that he was liable on the note: Collingre v. Makepeace, 13 Ind., 448. Noting the residences of indorsers after their names does not affect its identity, nor avoid it as to any of the parties to it: Struthers v. Kendall, 61 Penn. St., 214. The holder of a bill indorsed “A. B., Cashier,” may add the name of the bank: Bank of Genesee v. Patchin Bank, 13 N. Y., 308. The introduction of a dollar mark, followed by numerals, which may be omitted without affecting its sense, does not impair the validity of a note: Houghton v. Francis, 29 Ill., 244. The alteration of a note in a material respect by one of several makers, assuming to have authority so to do, and for the honest purpose of making it conform to the original intention of the parties, without the express though with the implied assent of the holder, will not prevent a recovery by the latter against all the makers declaring upon the note, as though in the form originally delivered: Murray v. Graham, 29 Iowa, 520. In the absence of evidence of fraudulent intent, a note is not void which has been altered after indorsement without the knowledge of the indorser, if again restored to its original condition: Kountz v. Kennedy, 69 Penn. St., 187.

An error in dating a note may be corrected by the holder, so as to make the note mature at the time in fact intended by the parties: Jessup v. Dennison, 2 Dis. (Ohio), 150; Dreker v. Franz, 7 Bush (Ky.), 273. Both parties intended to make a note payable to B. C., but accidentally it was written B. R. C.; held that the erasure of the R. by the payee after delivery was immaterial: Cole v. Hills, 44 N. H., 227; Ames v. Colburn, 11 Gray (Mass.), 390; Shepherd v. Whetstone, 51 Iowa, 457; Warpol v. Ellison, 4 Houst. (Del.) 322.

<br>

### --- Page 0470 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE. 326

Before the bill was an available instrument against any party, a new stamp was unnecessary. Upon the same principle, where three persons joined as drawer, acceptor and indorser, in the fabrication of an accommodation bill, and the date was altered before it came into the hands of a holder for value, it was held that as the accommodation parties could not sue upon it inter se, it was not, till it came into the hands of a holder for value, an available instrument, and therefore that an alteration before that time did not vitiate it. "The question," says Abbott, C. J., "is whether this alteration made it a new bill? Now, undoubtedly, when an accommodation bill has the different parties written upon it, it is, in some sense of the word, a bill of exchange; but it is utterly unavailable as a security for money until it is issued to some real holder for a valuable consideration. It first became a bill of exchange when it was issued to the indorsee for a valuable consideration." "Here," adds Best, J., "at the time when the alteration was made, the bill was a perfect bill in form, but it did not constitute a valid contract between the parties. A bond is a perfect instrument before delivery; but still an alteration made before delivery will not vitiate it." But if either payee or indorsee have given value for it, so that the drawer is liable, an alteration, though before acceptance, vacates the bill. "In such a case," says Lord Ellenborough, "it does not remain in fieri till acceptance. As to the drawer, it was before then a *perfect instrument.* When [327] the date was altered, a new bill was drawn, and that could not be done without a new stamp." So if a promissory note be signed by A., and subsequently by B. as surety for A., whilst the note is in the hands of the payee, it will be void, unless the signature of B. is in pursuance of a previous agreement at the time of making the note. And an altered bill will be void in the hands of an innocent indorsee, as well as in the hands of parties cognizant of the alteration.

1 Kennerley v. Nash, 1 Stark., 452 (2 E.C. L. B.).

4 Downs v. Richardson, 5 B. & Ald., 674 (7 E.C. L. B.): 1 D. & R., 332, &c.; Tarleton v. Shingler, 7 C. B., 812 (62 E.C.L. B.). As to the alteration of a deed after execution by one party, see Jones v. Jones, 1 C. & M., 721; before complete delivery, Spicer v. Burgess, 1 C., M. & R., 129; 4 Tyr. 598, &c.

* Walton v. Hastings, 4 Camp., 223; 1 Stark., 215 (2 E.C. L. B.), &c.

† Outhwaite v. Luntley, 4 Camp., 179.

° Clerk v. Blackstock, Holt N. P. C., 474. See Ex parte White, 2 Deac. & Chit., odd. Outhwaite v. Luntley, 4 Camp., 179.

<br>

### --- Page 0471 ---

327 ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE.

If again, the alterations were merely to correct a mistake, or to make a bill what it was originally intended to be, it will not avoid it under the Stamp Act. Thus where the drawee intended to make the bill negotiable, and indorsed it over, but had omitted the words “or order,” their subsequent insertion in pursuance of the original intention was held not to vacate the bill. So where a bill having been dated by mistake 1822, instead of 1823, the agent of the drawer and acceptor to whom it had been given to be delivered to the indorsee without their knowledge or consent corrected the mistake, it was held that such alteration did not vacate the bill.

So again, a man who has agreed beforehand to be a surety may, after the advance to another maker, sign the note.* A bona fide holder of a bill of exchange accepted payable to or order may insert his own name as payee, and indorse it, and the bill may be declared on as payable to the party who has inserted his name. “One,” says Best, C. J., “who accepts a bill in this form, undertakes to be answerable for it in the shape of a bill. That being so, he undertakes to be answerable for it in the form which a bona fide holder has a right to give it, and the description in the declaration is made out against him. No new stamp is necessary; the first stamp gives authority for the insertion.”* Whether the intent of the alteration were to vary the original contract, or merely to correct a mistake, is a question of fact for the jury.*

1 Kershaw v. Cox, 3 Esp., 246; 10 East, 437; Jacobs v. Hart, 2 Stark., 45 (3 E. C.L. R.); 6 M. & Sel., 142, &c.; Byron v. Thompson, 11 Ad. & ELL, 31 (39 ELC, L. B.), 3 P. & D., 71, &c.

2 Brutt v. Picard, R. & M., 37.

3 Dodge v. Pringle, 29 L. J., Exch., 115.

4 Atwood v. Griffin, R. & M., 425; 2 C. & P., 368 (12 B.C. L. B.), 80.

5 Ibid. The acceptor of a bill may alter the date before he accepts, and if this be concurred in by the other parties, it will bind them; Batciiff v. Planters’ Bank, 2 Sneed (Tenn.), 425. Where a party to a negotiable bill or note containing blanks, whether in the date or amount, intrusts it to another, especially if indorsed in blank or payable to bearer, such a transaction carries on its face an implied authority in the person to whom the bill or note is intrusted to fill up the blanks at his discretion; Michigan Bank v. Eldred, 9 Wall. (U.S.), 544; Smith v. Luckridge, 8 Bush (Ky.), 423. The innocent holder of a note forged over an autograph, given in bank for mere purposes of identification, held not entitled to recover: Caulkin v. Whisler, 29 Iowa, 495. Where a person signed an instrument to be filled up as an order for a certain article, and intrusted it to another for such purpose, who filled it up as a promissory note, the maker is liable to a bona fide holder, who had paid value before maturity: McDonald v. Muscatine National Bank, 27 Iowa, 319. One who signs a note written partly in ink, but containing a material condition in pencil, is

480

<br>

### --- Page 0472 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE. 328

*An alteration by the drawer and payee of the bill or the payee of a note, though it avoids the instrument, does not extinguish the debt; but an alteration by an indorsee not only avoids the security as against all parties, but also extinguishes the debt due to the indorsee from the indorser.* For it would be unjust that the indorsee should compel the indorser to pay his debt, when the indorsee has destroyed the instrument on which alone, in some cases, and on which preferably in all cases, the indorser should be liable to a holder in good faith, who takes it after the pencil marks have been erased: Harvey v. Smith, 55 Ill., 224. Where the form of a bill of exchange is signed in blank, and the holder, without the knowledge or consent of the signer, has filled the blanks in such a manner as to make a promissory note, and thus change its legal tenor and effect, the signer will be released from responsibility upon such note while in the hands of the person who has made the alteration: Luellen v. Hare, 32 Ind., 211. Garrard signed a printed note, in the blank of which was written “one hundred,” leaving a blank space between that and “dollars,” which was in print; this, after delivery was filled with “fifty” in the same hand, and nothing in the appearance to raise a suspicion that it was not all right; held that Garrard was liable for the face of the note to a bona fide holder for value: Garrard v. Huddah, 73 Penn. St., 82. 

For cases in which the note has been so negligently drawn as to induce fraudulent alterations, see Nebcker v. Cutringer, 43 Md., 436; Zimmerman v. Rote, 75 Penn. St.,188; Brown v. Reed, 70 Penn. St., 370; Cornell v. Nebcker, 58 Md., 455; Seibel v. Vaughan, 69 Ill., 257. When maker's signature is obtained by fraud: Chapman v. Rose, 44 How. (N. Y.), 364; Peiffer v. Smith, 57 Ill., 527; Kellogg v. Steins, 29 Wis. 626; Walker v. Ebert, Id., 194; Reidell v. Dillman, 73 Ind., 568. The maker whose signature is obtained without his consent or knowledge will not be liable even to an innocent holder: Briggs v. Ewart, 51 Mo., 245. Where maker signed without knowing that it was a note: Cline v. Guthrie, 42 Md., 227; Martin v. Smyler, 55 Mo., 577; Sims v. Brie, 67 Ill., 88; Wilson v. Miller, 72 Ill., 616; Anderson v. Walter, 34 Mich., 113. When maker supposed it was a different instrument: Kimble v. Christie, 55 Md., 140; Bank v. Lierman, 5 Neb., 217; De Camp v. Hamina, 29 Ohio St., 467; Rass v. Doland, Id., 473; Horner v. Hale, 71 Ill., 552; Swannell v. Watson, Id., 456.

As to filling up blanks: Page v. Morris, 3 Abb. (N. Y.), 433; Schryon v. Hawkes, 22 Ohio St., 303; Peyton v. Harman, 22 Gratt. (Va.), 643; Guthrupt v. Williamson, 81 Ind., 599; Armstrong v. Harshman, 61 Ind., 52; Waldron v. Young, 9 Heisk. (Tenn.), 777; Frazier v. Gains, 68 Tenn., 92; Harding v. State, 54 Md., 359; Emmons v. Mecker, 55 Md., 321; Angle v. N. Y. Mn. Ins. Co., 92 U.S., 330; Rich v. Starbuck, 81 Ind., 67; Hardy v. Norton, 60 Barb. (N. Y.), 527; Abbott v. Rose, 62 Me., 194; McGrath v. Clark, 56 N. Y., 34; Rainbelt v. Eddey, 34 Iowa, 440; Gillaspie v. Kelly, 41 Md., 158; Settig v. Berkestack, 38 Me., 158; Redlick v. Doll, 54 N. Y., 234; Yocum v. Smith, 63 Ill., 321; Schooler v. Tilden, 71 Mo., 580.

Sutton v. Toomer, 7 B. & C., 416 (14 E. C. L. R.); 1 M. & R., 125, s. c.; Atkinson v. Hawdon, 2 Ad. & E., 628 (29 E.C.L.R.); 4 N. & M., 409; 1 H. & W., 77, s. c.; see Sloman v. Cox, 1 C., M. & R., 171; 5 Tyr., 174, s. c. Unless the bill or note were taken in satisfaction of the debt: McDowall v. Boyd, 17 L. J., Q. B., 295.

Alderson v. Langdale, 3 B. & Ad., 660 (23 E. C. L. R.).

§1 481

<br>

### --- Page 0473 ---

328 ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE,

sue. To make the indorser liable on the consideration, and give him a cross action against the indorsee for the alteration, would be to oblige him to rely on the indorsee instead of the antecedent parties and to prove a fact of which he might have no evidence. It would besides introduce a needless circuity of action. A bona fide transferee for value of an altered bill is in no better position as to his remedy on the bill than his transferor.

If a bill be altered so that a man otherwise liable on it is discharged, he is not liable on a bill given in renewal of the altered bill, unless he were actually apprised of the alteration at the time he gave the substituted bill.

It is conceived, notwithstanding some recent cases, that the alteration of a bill or note need not, when the plaintiff declared on the instrument in its altered state, have been specially pleaded. When altered, it is no longer the same instrument that the defendant signed, and, moreover, there is no stamp applicable to the altered instrument, so that it cannot be looked at by the jury to prove the new contract. Therefore the defendant might, under the plea that he did not make, accept or indorse the instrument set forth in the declaration, show the alteration, and thereby prove that he executed another instrument, and not that in question, or, if there be no fresh stamp, that there is no instrument which the jury can look at.

1 Burchfield v. Moore, 23 L. J., Q. B., 261; 3 E & B, 683 (77 E.C.L. &.), s.c. Where an agreement is reduced to writing, whether under seal or not, so as to merge the original promise, and the written agreement is so altered as to avoid it, the party cannot resort to the original contract: Newall v. Mayberry, 3 Leigh (Va.), 250; Wheelock v. Freeman, 13 Pick. (Mass.), 165; Mills v. Starr, 2 Bailey (S.C.), 359.

The payee of a note fraudulently altered by him cannot recover on the original consideration: Smith v. Mace, 44 N. H., 553; Bigelow v. Stilphen, 35 Vt., 521. An alteration made by the payee in good faith, supposing himself authorized by the maker, will not preclude him from a recovery on the original consideration: Krause v. Meyer, 32 Iowa, 566. An alteration of an instrument by the holder, although such as to destroy its identity, will not cancel a debt of which the instrument was evidence, unless made fraudulently: Vogle v. Ripper, 34 N.J., 100. An unauthorized and material alteration of a note, made without fraudulent intent, but under a mistake of facts, avoids the note but leaves the original debt unpaid: Lewis v. Schenck, 3 Green (Iowa), 409.

2 Means of knowledge are not equivalent to actual knowledge: Bell v. Gardiner, L.J., C.P., 195; 4 M. & G., 11 (43 E.C. L. B.), s.c.

3 Cock v. Coxwell, 2 C., M. & R., 291; 4 Dowl., 187; 1 Gale, 177, s.c.; Calvert v. Baker, 4 M. & W., 417; 7 Dowl. 17, s.c.; Langton v. Lazarus, 5 M. & W., 629;

<br>

### --- Page 0474 ---

ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE, 329

But where the declaration was on the instrument in its original condition, the alteration must have been specially pleaded.

The plea, where it merely relied on the absence of a proper stamp on the altered instrument, must have shown that the bill or note could not be made good by being stamped before the trial.

Where an alteration appears on the face of a bill or note, it lies on the plaintiff to show that it was made under such circumstances as not to vitiate the instrument. And this rule is most reasonable; for if it lay on the defendant, on an acceptor for example, sued by an indorsee, to show that the alteration was improperly made it might be a great hardship; for he may have no means of proving that the bill went unaltered from his hands, or of showing the circumstances of a subsequent alteration. But the burden of explaining an alteration imposes no hardship on the plaintiff, for if the bill was altered while in his hands, he may and ought to account for it; if before, then he took it with a mark of suspicion on its face, which ought to have induced him either to refuse it or to require evidence of the circumstance under which the alteration was made.

Knight v. Clements, 8 Ad. & E., 215 (35 E.C. L. B.); Field v. Woods, 7 Ad. & E. 114 (E.C. L. K.); Crotty v. Hedges, 4 M. & G., 563 (43 E.C. L. B.); and Clifford v. Parker, 2 M. & G., 909 (40 E. C. L. R.). But see now Ord. XIX., r. 23.

Hemming v. Trenery, 9 Ad. & E., 996 (34 E.C.L. B.); 1 Per. & Dav., 661, s. c.; Bridgman v. Sheehan, cor. Parke, B., at nisi prius, T. T., 1842; Magon v. Bradley, L. J., Exch., 425; 11 M. & W., 590, s.c. This distinction does not appear to have been recognized in some of the cases. See Parry v. Nicholson, 13 M. & W., 778. But the most recent decision is in accordance with the view of the law taken in the text: Hirshman v. Budd, L. B., Ex., 171. See now Order XIX., r. 23.

Bradley v. Bardsley, 15 L. J., Exch., 115; 3 D. & L., 476; 14 M. & W., 873, s.c.

Johnson v. Duke of Marlborough, 2 Stark., 313 (3 E. C. L. R.); Henman v. Dickinson, 5 Bing., 183 (15 E. C. L. R.); 2 M. & P., 281, s.c.; Knight v. Clements, 3 N. & P., 375; 8 Ad. & E., 215 (35 E. C. L. R.), s.c.; Bishop v. Chambre, 1 M. & M., 116; 3 C. & P., 55 (14 E.C. L. R.), s.c. In Sibley v. Fisher, 7 Ad. & E., 444 (34 E.C. L. R.); 2 N. & P., 420, s.c., the making of the bill as described in the declaration was admitted on the record. See Earl of Falmouth v. Roberts, 9 M. & W., 471; Disbrowe v. Weatherly, 6 C. & P., 758 (25 E. C. L. R.); Semple v. Cole, 8 L. J., Exch., 155; 3 Jurist 263, s.c. And whether the alteration were before or after the completion of the bill has been left as a question of fact for the jury: Taylor v. Moseley, 6 C. & P., 273 (25 E. C. L. B.); and see Leykrieff v. Ashford, 12 Moore, 281.

It is said that the presumption against the legality of an alteration is confined to the cases of a bill of exchange or promissory note and a will. See Doe v. Catamore, 16 Q. B., 745 (71 E. C. L. B.), and Doe v. Palmer, 16 Q. B., 747 (71 E. C. L. B.), and the note to Master v. Miller, in 2 Smith’s Leading Cases.

The American cases on this subject are not harmonious. The weight of author-

<br>

### --- Page 0475 ---

B00 ALTERATION OF BILL OR NOTE,

ity, however, sustains the position in the text. There are several cases which leave the question as a presumption of fact to be determined by the jury. In some instances it has been held that the law presumes an erasure or interlineation to have been made before the instrument was signed. In other cases, however, it has been decided that if an erasure or interlineation appears on the face of a negotiable instrument, some explanation must be given in evidence before it can be allowed to go to the jury. It is then a question of fact for the jury when, by whom and by whose consent, it was altered; but the materiality of the alteration is in all cases a pure question of law for the court. See Jackson v. Osbourne, 2 Wend. (N.Y.) 595; Cumberland Bank v. Hall, 1 Halsted (N.J.), 215; Bailey v. Taylor, 11 Conn., 531; Haffelfinger v. Shute, 16 S. & R. (Penn.), 44; Chesley v. Frost, 1 N.H., 145; Jackson v. Jacoby, 9 Cow. (N.Y.), 125; Prevost v. Gratz, Pet. (U.S.C.C.), 369; Stephens v. Graham, 78 S. & R. (Penn.), 503; Bowers v. Jewell, 2 N.H., 543; Steele v. Spencer, 1 Pet. (U.S.), 552; Rankin v. Blackwell, 2 Johns. Cas. (N.Y.), 198; Hills v. Barns, 11 N.H., 395; Goche v. Bryant, 13 Mo., 386; Crabtree v. Clark, 7 Shepl., 357; Davis v. Carlisle, 8 Ala., 707; Warren v. Layton, 3 Mar. (Md.), 404; Bank v. Lum, 7 How. (Miss.), 414; Wilson v. Henderson, 9 S. & M. (Miss.), 375; Matthews v. Coalter, 9 Missouri, 705; Beaman v. Russell, 20 Vt., 205; Tillou v. Clinton and Essex Ins. Co., 7 Barb. (N.Y.), 564; Pain v. Esdeli, 19 Penn St., 178; Clarke v. Eckstein, 22 Penn St., 507; Burnham v. Ayer, 30 N.H., 351; Maybee v. Sniffen, 2 E.D. Smith (N.Y.), 1; Abbe v. Rood, 6 McLean (U.S.C.C.), 106; Hill v. Cooley, 46 Penn St., 259; White v. Hass, 32 Alabama, 430.

When an instrument has been altered, and there is no evidence when the alteration was made, it will be presumed to have been done at or before the execution: Stoner v. Ellis, 6 Indiana, 152. The court will not presume that a contract of guaranty has been fraudulently written over a simple indorsement from the mere fact that it is not in the handwriting of the indorser: Small v. Sloan, 1 Bosw. (N.Y.), 352. When the alteration consists in striking out a word in the printed form to make room for the name of one of the payees, and to change “order” to “bearer,” and it is apparent that it was done at the making of the note, it will have no effect: Williams v. Starr, 5 Wis., 531. An alteration of date, shown only by the note itself, was held insufficient to show that it was without consent: Harlan v. Berry, 4 Green (Iowa), 219. That material words were written at the end of a note, with different ink and in a different handwriting from the rest, does not throw the burden of proving that there was no alteration or explaining it on the plaintiff: Ault v. Fleming, 7 Clark (Iowa), 143.

The question of the burden of proof in such cases arose in the Supreme Court of Pennsylvania, in Simpson v. Stackhouse, 9 Penn St., 186. And it was held that the onus of showing that an alteration in a material part of a negotiable instrument was lawfully made is on the holder; and that where the place of payment is in a different handwriting from the body of the instrument there is a presumption of alteration.

*CHAPTER XXV. [*331]

OF THE FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES.

Alteration, . . . 335  
Uttering, . . . . 335  
Procuring to utter, . . 336  

Forgery is the counterfeit making or altering of any writing with intent to defraud. It is a misdemeanor at common law, punishable by fine and imprisonment, and a conviction of it, as of any other species of the crimen falsi, makes a man infamous, and formerly rendered him incompetent as a witness.

The forgery of bills or notes, or of any part of them, and the uttering of them knowing them to be forged, are respectively felonies, punishable by penal servitude for life.

Fraudulently obliterating or altering the crossing of a check is felony.

*The fraudulent signing of a bill or note for any other person by procuration or otherwise, without lawful authority, ...

See Reg. o. White, 1 Den. C. C., 208.

Ame’s Case, 2 Me., 365; Commonwealth v. Ladd, 15 Mass., 526; People v. Schull, 9 Cow, (N. Y.), 778; Barnum v. State, 15 Ohio, 717.

Bl. Cor., 248.

Com. Dig. Tostm., A., 5; Rex v. Davis, 5 Mod., 74. He is now capacitated by 6 & 7 Viet., c. 85,

24 & 25 Vict., c. 98, s. 22,

6 Thid., 8, 25.

<br>

### --- Page 0477 ---

S02 FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES.

Uttering a forged bill or note, or knowingly uttering the same, has, by the recent act 24 & 25 Vict., c. 98, s. 24, for the first time been erected into a felony.

Inducing a person by violence or threats to execute a bill or note or other valuable security is now also for the first time made a felony.

Fraudulently to obtain by false pretences a signature to a bill or note, or the destruction of the instrument in whole or in part, is now a misdemeanor.

Forging or uttering such a bill or note as the legislature has declared void is not within the statutes, as, for example, a bill or note for less than 20s., or a bill or note for less than £2, which did not comply with the requisites of 17 Geo. 3, c. 30, in the former state of the law.

Where there is no payee, or no maker’s name, it has been held that the offence is not within the act.

A mere informality, as the omission of the word POUNDS in the body, where the letter £ precedes the figures 50 in the margin, does not prevent the crime amounting to forgery.

In order to constitute forgery, it is not necessary that the instrument should be duly stamped, or stamped at all.

[*333] The most common species of forgery is fraudulently writing the names of an existing person. But a fraudu-

1 See as to the law before this statute, Maddock’s case, 3 Russ., C. & M., 499; Rex v. White, 1 Den, C. C., 208; 2 C. & K., 404.

2 24 & 25 Vict., c. 96, s. 48. See Rex v. Pipos, 2 Leach, 673; Rex v. Edwards, 6 C. & P., 521 (25 E. C. L. R.).

3 24 & 25 Vict., c. 96, s. 90. See Rex v. Danger, 1 D. & B. C. C., 307.

4 Rex v. Moffatt, 1 Leach, 431; 2 East, P. C., 945, c.

5 Rex v. Richards, R. & R., C.C., 193; Rex v. Randall, R. & R., C. C., 195; and see as to other fatal defects, Rex v. Jones, Doug., 287; Rex v. Pateman, R. & R., 455, where there was no maker’s name; Rex v. Burke, R. & R., 496; Rex v. Wilcox, Barley, 6th ed., 11. To constitute the forgery of a bill of exchange within 1 Will. 4, c. 66, s. 4, the instrument must be complete: Rex v. Turpin, 2 C. & K., 820 (61 E. C. L. R.). Forging an acceptance to an instrument in the form of a bill, but without the drawer’s name, is not within the statute: Rex v. Butterwick, 2 Moo. & R., 196.

6 Rex v. Peet, B. & B., 101, and Bayley, 11; and see Collinson’s case, 2 Leach, 1048.

7 Teague’s case, 2 East, P.C., 979; B. & R., 33, s. c.; Rex v. Hawkeswood, 1 Leach, 57; 2 East, P.C., 935, s. c.; Rex v. Lee, 1 Leach, 258, n.; Merton’s case, 2 East, P.C., 955; and see 33 & 34 Vict., c. 97, s. 17.

8 Making a mark and suffering the assumed name to be written against it is forgery: Rex v. Dunn, 1 Leach, 57; 2 East, P.C., 962. Putting the address of an

456

<br>

### --- Page 0478 ---

FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES. 333

lent misapplication of the genuine signature of another man is as much forgery as counterfeiting his signature. Thus, where the prisoner, having in his possession the genuine signature of one Thomas Gibson, wrote over it a promissory note for 6400/., he was indicted and convicted of having forged the note. And where the same prisoner, having the genuine signature of Samuel Edwards, wrote on the other side of the paper a promissory note, payable to Samuel Edwards, and so turned the genuine signature into an indorsement, he was convicted of forging the indorsement. If a clerk be intrusted to fill up a blank check signed by his master with a particular sum, and he fraudulently inserts a larger sum, it is a forgery of the check.

There may be an innocent misapplication of his own genuine signature by the party himself. Thus, where a man was induced to sign his name to a bill by a fraudulent misrepresentation of the nature of the instrument, it was held that, if not guilty of negligence, he was not liable even to an innocent holder, any more than if he had been blind or illiterate, and the instrument had been falsely and fraudulently read over to him.

To sign the name of a fictitious or non-existing person is forgery. Where the prisoner was convicted of forging an order for payment of money, and it appeared that he had bought goods from the presenter and paid for them with a draft signed in the fictitious name

Existing person to a name, being the name of another person, is forgery: Reg. v. Dixon Bishop, 1 Den. C. C., 276.

Rex v. Hales, 17 St. Tr., 161.

Ibid., 209, 229.

Reg. v. Wilson, 17 L. J., M.C., 82; 1 Den. C. C., 284, &c.; Rex v. Hart, 1 Moo. C.C., 436; 7 C. & P., 692 (32 E. C. L. R.), &c.

Foster v. Mackinnon, L. R., 4 C. P., 704; and the English and American authorities there cited. Bank v. Weckerly, 67 Ind., 345; Auten v. Gruner, 90 Ill., 300.

If maker had opportunity, but relied on the representation of the payee, he cannot defend against a bona fide holder that he did not know what he was signing: Shirts v. Overjohn, 60 Mo., 303; Fredericks v. Clemens, Id., 313; Bank v. Smith, 55 N. H., 503. Fraud in procuring signature to a different note not good even in the hands of innocent holder: Griffiths v. Kellogg, 39 Wis., 260.

A party can acquire no title, legal or equitable, to a note originally obtained by fraud and passed by means of forgery in fraud of the maker: Carpenter v. Bank, 123 Mass., 66; Welch v. Goodwin, Id., 71.

Rex v. Francis, Bayley, 6th ed., 572; Russ. & Ry., 209; Lockett’s case, 1 Leach, 96; East, P. C., 940; Taft’s case, 1 Leach, 172; East, P. C., 959; or in the prisoner’s own name to represent a fictitious firm: Reg. v. Rogers, 8 C. & P., 629 (34 E. C. L. B.), 487

<br>

### --- Page 0479 ---

ms 25 $5" FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES,

of H. Turner, although the prosecutor had sworn that he gave credit to the prisoner and not to the draft, it was held that the prisoner was rightly convicted. The judges said that it was a false instrument, not drawn by any such person as it purported to be, and that the using a fictitious name was only for the purpose of deceiving. But the signing a fictitious name will not amount to forgery if it were used on other occasions as well as for that very fraud, or system of fraud, of which the forgery forms a part. Where proof is given of the prisoner’s real name, and no proof of any change of name until the time of the fraud committed, it lies on the prisoner to show that he has before assumed the false name on other occasions and for other purposes unconnected with forgery.

It is a forgery, also, to sign a man’s own name with intention that the signature should pass for the signature of another person of the same name. And where a person, whose name was Thomas Brown, was indicted for forging a promissory note signed Thomas Brown, and it appeared that he had uttered the note as a note of Captain Brown, a fictitious person, and the prisoner was convicted, the judges held the conviction right. But the adoption of a false description and addition where a false name is not assumed is not forgery. Thus, where the prisoner drew a bill and directed it to ‘Mr. Thomas Bowden, baize manufacturer, Romford, Essex,’ and it was accepted by one Thomas Bowden, but there was no Thomas Bowden of Romford, it was held by a majority of the judges that the giving a false description of Bowden on the bill with intent to defraud was not forgery.

Where the signature on the bill is genuine, an uttering by another person with a representation that he is the person whose signature is on the bill is not forgery nor a felonious uttering. The prisoner uttered a bill purporting to be payable to Bernard M’Carthy, or order, and having the indorsement B. M’Carthy thereon: he was indicted for forging that indorsement and uttering it knowing it to

7 Rex v. Bontien, R. & R., 200.

§ Peacock’s case, R. & R., 278.

Mead v. Young, 4 T. R., 28.

6 Rex v. Parkes, 2 Leach, 773; 2 East, P. C., 963, 5, &c.

© Webb's case, R. & B., 405; 3 B. & B., 229 (7 E.C.L. R.), &c.; Rex v. Watts, R. & R., 436; 6 Moore, 442; 3 B. & B., 197 (E.C.L. R.), &c.

FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES, 394

he forged; the jury found that there was such a man as B. M’Carthy and that the indorsement was his handwriting, but that the prisoner passed himself off as that B. M’Carthy when he uttered the bill. The judges were unanimous *that as the indorsement was [#335] not forged the prisoner was not liable to be convicted."

Writing a principal’s name “per procuration,” but without authority, was not until the recent statute forgery;* nor, as it should seem, writing merely another man’s name under a false pretence of authority, without any intention of imitating his handwriting.

Every fraudulent alteration, whether by subtraction, addition or substitution, is forgery, and would be so within the statute, even did they not contain the word alter, as was decided on 2 Geo. 2, c. 15, which did not contain that word. The statute 11 Geo. 4 & 1 Will. 4, c. 66, contain the word “alter” as well as “forge.” Nevertheless, an alteration may be described in the indictment as forgery. So, e converso, the discharging one indorsement and the insertion of another may be described as alteration of an indorsement.

Procuring a man to forge is an offence within the statute.

It has been decided that, in order to constitute an uttering, the instrument must be parted with, or tendered, or offered, or used in some way to get money or credit upon it. Therefore, where the defendant, in order to persuade an inn-keeper that he was a man of substance, pulled out of his pocket-book a 500l. and 50l. note, and saying that he did not like to carry so much property about him, delivered them to the inn-keeper to take charge of them for him, it was held that this did not amount to an uttering.

2 Vide ante, p. 332.

3 Reg. v. White, 1 Den., C. C., 208; 2 C. & K., 404, 61 E.C. L. R., s. c.

4 Ibid.; but see Awde v. Dixon, 6 Exch., 869.

5 Rex v. Elsworth, Bayley, 6th ed., 574; 2 East, P. C., 986; Reg. v. Blenkinsop, 1 Den. C. C., 276.

6 Rex v. Teague, K.R. & R., 33; 2 East, P. C., 979, s. c.; Rex v. Post, R.B. & R., 101; Rex v. Treble, 2 Taunt., 228; 2 Leach, 1040; R. & R., 164, s. c.

7 Rex v. Birkett, B.R. & R., 251.

10 Ibid.; and see Holden’s case, R. & R., 154; 2 Leach, 1019, s. c.; Palmer’s case, R.K. & B., 72; 2 Leach, 978, s. c.; Rex v. Morris, R. & B., 270; Reg. v. Hill, 2 M.C.C., 489

### --- Page 0481 ---

330 FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES.

[336] *Procuring to utter has been held a common law felony only.

But procuring to utter, if the person procured were innocent of the felony, is a statutory felony in the procurer.

Before certain recent acts of Parliament it was necessary to get out the forged instrument in the indictment in words and figures correctly: the slightest variance would have entitled the defendant to an acquittal. But the 14 & 15 Vict., c. 100, s. 5, in order to prevent justice from being defeated by clerical or verbal inaccuracies, enacted that in all indictments for forging or uttering any instrument, it shall not be necessary to set forth any copy or facsimile thereof, but it shall be sufficient to describe it by any name by which it would be usually known.*

An indictment for the larceny, and therefore now for the forgery, of a bill or note, may describe it generally as a bill of exchange or promissory note for the payment of the sum therein mentioned without setting out the instrument. But if it be alleged in the indictment to have been signed or made by any person, the signature must be proved.

If several make distinct parts of the instrument, they are each chargeable with the forgery of the entire instrument. Those who knowingly prepare the paper or plates for the purpose are forgers.

Before the 9 Geo. 4, c. 32, s. 2, a rule of evidence existed equally

30. If a merchant write his name on blank pieces of paper, and intrust them to his clerk for the purpose of having promissory notes filled out, and a person by false pretences obtains possession of one of them, and fills up a note for his own use, he does not thereby commit the crime of forgery: Putnam v. Sullivan, 4 Mass., 40.

1 Rex v. Morris, Bayley, 6th ed., 580; BR. & B., 270; 2 Leach, 1096, s. c. But see now 24 & 25 Vict., c. 94, ss. 1, 2, 49.

2 Bayley, 6th ed., 582.

3 And see now 24 & 25 Vict., c. 98, ss. 42, 43.

4 Milne’s case, Worcester Summer Assizes, 1800, decided by all the Judges; East’s P.C., 692, s. cc. Before this act it was held that, in an indictment for forgery, a bank post bill could not be described as a bill of exchange, but might be described as a bank bill of exchange: Rex v. Birkett, R. & R., 251.

5 Rex v. Craven, R. & R., 14; 2 East, P.C., 601, s. c. The statute 14 & 15 Vict., c. 100, gives most extensive powers of amendment; and as to the form of the indictment, see 24 & 25 Vict., c. 98, ss. 42, 43, 44; see also 16 & 17 Vict., c. 2.

6 Rex v. Bingley, R. & R., 446; Rex v. Kirkwood, 1 Mood. C. C., 304; vide Reg. v. Cook, 8 C. & P., 582 (34 E. C. L. R.).

7 Rex v. Dade, 1 Mood. C. C., 307.

490

### --- Page 0482 ---

FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES, 336

Anomalous and inconvenient, that in a criminal prosecution for forgery, the party whose name was forged was incompetent as a witness; but now he is made competent by that statute.337 All indictments or informations for forgery or uttering, either against principals or accessories, by common law or statute.

A doubt also formerly existed whether the making or uttering of an instrument payable abroad was an offence within some of the repealed statutes. But the statute 11 Geo. 4 & 1 Will. 4, c. 66, s. 30, brought within the operation of the acts against forgery instruments made, or purporting to be made, or payable, or purporting to be so, out of England. The statute now in force is 24 & 25 Vict. c. 98, s. 40.

Where the prisoner is indicted for using a fictitious name, some evidence must be given by the prosecutor that it is not his real name. But where the prisoner’s real name is proved, it lies on him to show that he has before assumed the false name for other purposes.

Upon an indictment for uttering forged notes, evidence that the prisoner has uttered other forged notes is admissible as evidence of his knowledge of the forgery. But such notes must be produced and proved to be forgeries. The admissibility of evidence as to uttering forged bills of a different kind has been doubted.

Where a title to a bill or note is necessarily made through a forgery, even a bona fide holder for value has in general no right to sue upon it, or even retain it; and, therefore, as a general rule, if the acceptor or maker pay one who derives his title through a

Rex v. Dick, 16 Leach, 8; Rex v. M’Kay, R. B. & R., 71.

The 16th section of 11 Geo., 4 & 1 Will., 4, c. 66, applies to notes of persons carrying on the business of bankers in the province of Upper Canada. This act is repealed now, 24 & 25 Vict., c. 98, s. 16.

Rex v. Peacock, Bayley, 6th ed., 579; R. B. & B., 273; Bontien’s case, R. & R., 263; Rex v. Peacock, R. B. & R., 278.

Wylie’s case, 1 New B., 92; Hough’s case, R. & B., 120; Reg. v. Green, 3 C. & K., 209.

Rex v. Millard, R. & R., 245.

Tac, Russ. & Ry, 247. As to the prisoner’s admission relating to other bills, see Reg. v. Cook, 8 C. & P., 686 (34 E. C. L. B.); Reg. v. Oddy, 2 Den. C. C., 264; Reg. v. Green, 3 C. & K., 209.

Burchfield v. Moore, 23 L. J., Q. B., 261; S. E. & B, 88 (77 E. C. L. R.), &c. See ante, chapter on ACCEPTANCE.

Esdaile v. Lanauze, 1 You. & Col., 394; Johnson v. Windle, 3 Bing. N. C., 225 (32 E. C. L. B.); 3 Scott, 608, s. c.

### --- Page 0483 ---

33F FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES.

forgery, that will not discharge him.’ So, if a bill or check altered and made payable for a larger sum than that originally inserted, should the drawee, banker or acceptor pay it, he cannot charge the drawer for the difference.’

But in case any act of the drawer facilitated or gave occasion to the forgery, he must bear the loss himself. A customer of a banker, on leaving home, intrusted to his wife several blank forms of checks, signed by himself, and desired her to fill them up according to the exigency of his business. She filled up one with the words “fifty pounds two shillings,” beginning the word fifty with a small letter in the middle of a line. The figures 52:2 were also placed at a considerable distance to the right of the printed £. She gave the check, thus filled up, to her husband’s clerk to get the money. He, before presenting it, inserted the words “three hundred” before the word fifty and the figure 3 between the printed £ and the figures 52:2. It was presented and the bankers paid it. Held that the improper mode of filling up the check had invited the forgery, and, therefore, that the loss fell on the customer and not on the banker.

So, if the acceptor of a bill tear the bill in two animo cancellandi, and the pieces are picked up in his presence and afterwards joined together so as to convey no notice of the cancellation to a stranger, a bona fide indorsee for value may acquire a title.

But a banker who pays a draft on himself, payable to order on demand, need not prove the genuineness of the first or any subsequent indorsement: 16 & 17 Vict. c. 59, s. 19. But this section does not protect other parties, so that a transferee of a check that had been stolen and indorsed by a forger has no title to the proceeds as against the loser, unless the loser has been guilty of negligence in the transaction itself: Arnold v. Check Bank, L.R., 1 C.P. Div., 578; Baxendale v. Bennett, L.R., 3 Q.B. Div., 525; in which case he would be estopped from setting up the true facts.

Hall v. Faller, 5 B. & C., 750 (11 E.C.L.B.); 8 B. & Ry., 465, s.c.; Smith v. Mercer, 6 Taunt., 76 (1 E.C.L.R.); 1 Marsh., 453, s.c.

Young v. Grote, 4 Bing., 253 (13 E.C.L.B.); 12 Moore, 284, s.c. See Ingham v. Primrose, 23 L.J., C.P., 294; 7 C.B., N.S. 82 (97 E.C.L.R.), s.c.; Ex parte Swan, 30 L.J., C.P., 115; 7 C.B., N.S., 400 (97 E.C.L.B.), s.c.; Orr v. Union Bank of Scotland, 1 Macq. H. of L. Cases, 513; British Linen Company v. Caledonian Insurance Company, 4 Macq. H. of L. Cases, 107; Foster v. Green, 6 Hurlst. & Norm., 163. And it has been held that a principal who, through his own agent, sends money to his creditor which is misapplied by the agent, is not responsible any further to the creditor, if the creditor's conduct facilitated the agent's fraud: Horsfall v. Fauntleroy, 10 B. & C., 755 (21 E.C.L.R.).

Ingham v. Primrose, supra. This doctrine of estoppel has never been extended.

<br>

### --- Page 0484 ---

FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES. 309

It is a general rule of law that money paid under a mistake as to facts may be recovered back. On this principle, if a forged note be discounted, the transferee, on discovery of the forgery, may recover back the money paid, the imagined consideration totally failing. But any fault or negligence on the part of him who pays the money on the note will disable him from recovering. Thus, where two bills of exchange falling due at different times were drawn on a man, and he paid the first without acceptance, and accepted and paid the second, and the signature of the drawer was some time afterwards discovered to be a forgery, Lord Mansfield held that an acceptor is bound to know the handwriting of the drawer, and that it is rather by his fault or negligence than by mistake if he pays on a forged signature. So where a forged acceptance of the drawee was made payable at the plaintiff's, the drawee’s bankers, and they paid the amount to the defendant as a bona fide holder, but seven days afterwards, upon discovering the acceptance to be a forgery, informed the defendant of it, and demanded the money, it was held that they could not recover, for that a banker ought to know his customer’s handwriting. Part of the court held the defendant discharged on the ground that by the plaintiff’s delay in giving notice of the forgery he had lost his remedy against the antecedent parties. Where the fault is not entirely on the side of the party paying, he may still recover. Certain bills of exchange, purporting to bear amongst others the indorsement of A., were refused payment; the notary took them to the plaintiff, the London correspondent of A., and asked him to take up the bills for A.’s honor. The plaintiff accordingly paid the money to the defendants, holders of the bills, and struck out all the indorsements subsequent to A.’s. The same morning it was discovered that the respective signatures of A., the drawer and acceptor, were forged. Plaintiff immediately sent notice to the defendants.

Instruments under seal; such an extension was attempted in Ex parte Swan, 30 L.J., C.P., 113; 7 C.B., N.S. 400 (97 E.C.L.B.), etc. But the Court of Common Pleas being equally divided, the rule dropped. The Court of Exchequer Chamber held that there was no estoppel: Ibid., 32 L.J., Exch., 273.

Jones v. Ryde, 5 Taunt., 488 (1 E.C.L.B.); 1 Marsh., 157, etc.; Bruce v. Bruce, 6 Taunt., 495 (1 E.C.L.B.); 1 Marsh., 165, etc.; Grundy v. Womersley, 4 E. & B., 133 (62 E.C.L.B.).

Price v. Neal, 3 Burr., 1354; 1 W.B., 390, etc.

Smith v. Mercer, 6 Taunt., 76 (1 E.C.L.B.); 1 Marsh., 453, etc. See as to delay, Pooley v. Browne, 11 C.B., N.S., 566 (103 E.C.L.B.).

### --- Page 0485 ---

339 FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES.

in time for them to advise their indorser. The court held that the plaintiff was entitled to recover his money back, and said, “A bill is carried for payment to the person whose name appears as acceptor entirely as a matter of course. But it is by no means a matter of course to call upon a person to pay a bill [*340] for the honor of an indorser; and such a call, therefore, imports, on the part of the person making it, that the name of a correspondent for whose honor payment is asked is actually on the bill. The person thus called upon ought certainly to satisfy himself that the name of his correspondent is really on the bill: but still his attention may reasonably be lessened by the assertion that the call itself makes to him in fact, though no assertion may be made in words. And the fault if he pays on a forged signature is not wholly and entirely his own, but begins at least with the person who thus calls upon him. And though where all the negligence is on one side it may, perhaps, be unfit to inquire into the quantum; yet, where there is any fault in the other party, and that other party cannot be said to be wholly innocent, he ought not, in our opinion, to profit by the mistake into which he may, by his own prior mistake, have led the other; at least if the mistake be discovered before any alteration in the situation of any of the other parties; that is, whilst the remedies of all parties entitled to remedy are left entire, and no one is discharged by laches. We think the payment in this case was a payment by mistake and without consideration to a person not wholly free from blame. The striking out an indorsement by mistake cannot, in our opinion, discharge the indorser.'

Where bankers who have paid a forged bill gave notice of the forgery, and demanded the money by one o'clock in the afternoon of the following day, the court took time to consider, and at length unanimously held that the money could not be recovered back. “In this case,” they say, “we give no opinion upon the point whether the plaintiffs would have been entitled to recover if notice of forgery had been given to the defendants on the very day on which the bill was paid, so as to enable the defendants on that day to have sent notice to other parties on the bill. But we are all of opinion that the holder of a bill is entitled to know on the day when it became

1 Wilkinson v. Johnson, 3 B. & C., 428 (10 E. C. L. B.); 5 D. & Ry., 403, &c., 504

<br>

### --- Page 0486 ---

FORGERY OF BILLS AND NOTES. 840

due whether it is an honored or dishonored bill; and that if he receives the money, and is suffered to retain it during the whole of that day, the parties who paid it cannot recover it back. The holder, indeed, is not bound by law (if the bill be dishonored by the acceptor) to take any other steps against the other parties to the bill till the day after it is dishonored. But he is entitled to do so if he think fit; and the parties who pay the bill ought not by their negligence to deprive the holder of any right to take steps against the parties to the bill on the day when it becomes due.

In an action on the bill alleged to be forged, the defendant may at any time, by notice in writing, require the other party to produce it for his inspection; or the court or a judge may order production on oath of all material documents, to be dealt with as shall appear just.

1 But if a banker, on whom a check is drawn, be also the banker of the holder who pays in the check without any intimation of the character in which he desires the banker to receive it, whether as drawee, or as his, the holder’s, agent, it will be presumed that the banker took it as the agent of the holder, and therefore the banker may, in the course of the next day, inform the holder that there are no effects, and that the check will not be paid: Boyd v. Emmerson, 2 Ad. & El., 184 (29 ELC. LG. B.); 4N. & M., 99, & c.; and see Kilsby v. Williams, 5 B. & Ald., 815 (7 ECL R); 1 D. & C., 476, & c.; Pollard v. Ogden, 2 E. & B., 459 (75 E. C. L. R.).

> Cocks v. Masterman, 9 B. & C., 902 (17 E. C. L. R.); 4 M. & Ry., 676; Dans. & Ll., 329, & c.; and see Mather v. Lord Maidstone, 18 C. B., 273 (86 E. C. L. B.); 25 L. J., C. P., 311, & c.; Pollard v. Ogden, 2 E. & B., 459 (75 E. C. L. B.). Where a party became the holder of a forged draft before it had been accepted, and afterwards it was accepted and paid, and the acceptors, immediately upon ascertaining the fact of the forgery, gave notice of the fact to the holder, it was held that the acceptors were not estopped from proving the forgery and recovering back the money they had paid through error: McKleroy v. Southern Bank, 14 La. Ann., 453.

Without a limitation of actions no man can be secure in
the enjoyment of his property. Prescription is the original

496
