### --- Page 0488 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS, etc.

source of all title. After a lapse of years evidence is [*343] weakened or destroyed. And a claimant who has long slept on his demand has no right to complain if, for the public advantage, it is at length taken from him. In practice it is found that no statutes are so useful as those of limitation, compelling as they do investigation whilst the means of investigation subsist, and supplying the loss of those means by a general act of settlement applicable to each man’s case.

Though an act of limitation, in respect to real property was passed in this country in the year 1270, yet, partly from the comparatively inconsiderable amount of personal property, partly from the frequency of the sales in market overt, and partly from the circumstances that debts above 40s. were commonly secured by bond or single bill, and debts below that amount were not tried in the superior courts, no limitation to personal actions was introduced till the year 1623, when the present Statute of Limitations (the 21 Jac. 1, c. 16, supplemented by 3 & 4 Will. 4., c. 42) was passed.

The enactments of that statute, as far as they are applicable to our present purpose, are as follows:

By s. 3, all actions on the case (other than such accounts as concern the trade of merchandise between merchant and merchant, their factors and servants), and all actions of debt grounded on any lending or contract without specialty, must be brought within six years of the cause of such actions, and not after.

By s. 4, if judgment for the plaintiff be arrested or reversed, or the defendant be outlawed and afterwards reverse the outlawry, the plaintiff or his executors may commence a new action within a year.

Section 7 provides that if any person entitled to the action shall at the time of the cause of action accrued be, first, an infant; secondly, feme covert; thirdly, non compos mentis; fourthly, imprisoned; or fifthly, beyond the seas, then such person may bring the action within six years after their full age, discontinuance, sound memory, enlargement or return from beyond the seas.

In treating of the effects of this statute in its relation to bills and notes, we shall consider, 1, its general operation, and whether it destroys the debt or only bars the remedy; 2, what actions or

The exceptions of merchants’ accounts was repealed by 19 & 20 Vict., c. 97, s. 9.

<br>

### --- Page 0489 ---

344 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

(*344] legal proceeding on those instruments it *limits; 3, from what period the statute begins to run; 4, to what period the time of limitation is computed; 5, how the statute may be avoided by issuing a writ and continuing it down; 6, the proviso as to persons laboring under disabilities; 7, what promises, acknowledgments or payments will take a bill or note out of the statute; 8, how the statute is to be taken advantage of; and lastly, when, independently of the statute, lapse of time will be a bar to an action on a bill or note.

First, as to the general operation of the statute.

The Statute of Limitations is a good plea in equity as well as at law. It is also an answer to proof under a petition for adjudication in bankruptcy.¹ It was formerly a doubt whether the statute was a bar in the Admiralty Courts to a suit for seamen’s wages; but that doubt was removed by 4 Anne, c. 16, §§ 8, 12, which enacts that all suits and actions in the Court of Admiralty for seamen’s wages shall be commenced and sued within six years next after the cause of such suits or actions shall accrue, and not after.

The Statute of Limitations does not destroy a debt, but only bars the remedy. Therefore, it must in all cases be pleaded, and cannot be given in evidence, even under the plea of nil debet, or the replication of ni debet to a set-off.⁴ Therefore, also, a promissory note more than six years old, though not a good petitioning creditor’s debt as against the bankrupt (who may object that the remedy by a petition in bankruptcy as well as by action is taken

¹ Ex parte Dewdney, 15 Ves., 479.

² Ewer v. Jones, 6 Mod., 20.

³ As to an agreement not to rely on the statute, see East India Company v. Paul, Jur., 253; 7 Moo. P.C.C., 85; Lade v. Trill, 6 Jur., 272; Waters v. Thanes, 20 Q.B. (42 E. CL. B.).

These statutes bar the remedy simply, and therefore may be altered at any time without impairing the obligation of a contract: Oriental Bank v. Friese, 15 Me., 109; Curtis v. Hardee, 38 Ga., 350; Dale v. Frisbie, 59 Ind., 520; Button v. Guy, 12 S.C., 42; Pearsall v. Kenan, 79 N.C., 472; Cox v. Berry, 13 Ga., 306; Holland v. Dickerson, 41 Iowa, 367; Meek v. Meek, 41 Iowa, 294; Johnson v. R. R. Co., 54 N.Y., 416.

The operation of the statute was suspended during the civil war of 1861-5 so far as regards the enforcement of contracts between residents of both sections of the United States then in arms: Levy v. Stewart, 11 Wall. (U.S.), 244; Stewart v. Kahu, Id., 493, 503; United States v. Wiley, Id., 508, 513; Brown v. Hiatt, 1 Dill. (U.S.C.C.), 372; Coleman v. Holmes, 44 Ala., 124; Mixer v. Sibley, 53 Ill., 61; Braun v. Sauerwein, 10 Wall. (U.S.), 218; Stiles v. Easley, 57 Ill., 245.

⁴ Chapple v. Durston, 1 C. & J., 1, overruling the opinion of Lord Holt at Hertford Assizes, 1690; Anon, 1 Salk., 278; Draper v. Glossop, 2 Ld. Raym., 153.

<br>

### --- Page 0490 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS. 244

‘Is nevertheless a good petitioning creditor’s debt as against strangers! “It is settled,” says Lord Mansfield, “that the Statute of Limitations does not destroy the debt, it only takes away the remedy; the objection lies in the mouth of the bankrupt himself, but not in the mouth of a third person.” Therefore, again, a lien may be enforced where an action for its amount would be barred by the statute.

A foreign Statute of Limitations is no defence to an action on a foreign contract in the English courts, unless it have the effect of extinguishing the contract, and the parties are living in the foreign country at the time of the extinction. For a Statute of Limitations usually affects the remedy merely, and not the construction of the contract.

Secondly, as to the actions and legal proceedings which the statute limits. It will be sufficient for the present purpose to remark that actions of debt and of assumpsit are limited to six years. That though the statute does not in terms apply to proceeding in equity, courts of equity adopt its provisions as a rule. “With regard to that statute,” says Sir William Grant, “though it does not apply to any equitable demand, yet equity adopts it, or at least takes the same limitation in cases that are analogous to those in which it applies in law.” But the statute does not bar a trust nor a legacy. We have already seen that the statute is a bar to bankruptcy.

1 Swaine v. Wallinger, 2 Stra., 746.

2 Quantock v. England, 5 Burr., 2628; 2 W. Bl., 703, &c. See the same doctrine laid down by Lord Ellenborough and Bayley, J., in Williams v. Jones, 13 East, 450; and the Court of Exchequer, in Chapple v. Durston, 1 C. & J., 1; Mavor v. Pyne, 2 C. & P., 91 (12 E. C. L. R.).

3 Spears v. Harley, 3 Esp., 81.

4 Huber v. Steiner, 2 Bing., N. C., 202 (29 E. C. L. B.); 2 Scott, 304, s.c.; Harris v. Quine, L. B., 4 Q. B., 653. See the chapter on FOREIGN LAW. This is also the rule in this country, and the statutory bar of one State cannot be set up to defeat an action in another, unless, as is the case in several of the States, the statute expressly so provides, 5 Sec. 3.

6 Johnson v. Smith, 2 Burr., 961: Prince v. Heylin, 1 Atk., 493. Courts of Equity act in analogy to courts of law, and give effect to the statute in all cases of concurrent jurisdiction: Keaton v. McGuier, 24 Ga., 217; Callard v. Thrall, 4 Vt., 491; Thorp v. Thorp, 15 Vt., 105; Lewis v. Marshall, 5 Pet. (U. S.), 407; Hill v. Bayland, 40 Miss., 618; Gunn v. Brantley, 21 Ala., 633.

7 Starhouse v. Baruston, 10 Ves., 466.

8 Heath v. Hanley, 1 Cha. Ca., 20.

9 Anon., 2 Freem., 22.

<br>

### --- Page 0491 ---

345 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

The exception as to merchants’ accounts (which, as we have seen, is now repealed) applied only to an action of account, or perhaps also to an action on the case for not accounting, but not to an action of debt or assumpsit.

It is conceived that if the statute have run out against the holder of a bill or note payable at a day certain, and he then transfers it, the transferee’s right of action is barred. For he, as transferee of an overdue bill, can stand in no better situation than his transferor. He, like his transferor, has a debt due to him, but has lost the right of action, and has notice of the loss of it. And perhaps, as to the Statute of Limitations, the holder for the time being is a trustee of the action, so that prior or subsequent indorsees are, as between themselves and earlier parties, prejudiced by his laches.

Thirdly, as to the time from which the statute runs.

The Statute of Limitations begins to run on a bill or note, as well as on any other contract, from the time that the action first accrued to the party.

1 Inglis v. Haigh, 8 M. & W., 769; and see Cottam v. Partridge, 4 M. & G., 271; Sentt, N. R., 174, &c.; Phillips v. Cage, 12 S. & M. (Miss.), 141. Contra, Mandeville v. Wilson, 5 Cranch (U. S.), 15; Brackenridge v. Baltzell, 1 Smith, 217. As to what are merchants’ accounts: Slacambs v. Holmes, 1 How. (Miss.), 756; Fox v. Fish, 6 Id., 328; Bevan v. Cullen, 7 Penn. St., 281; Brackenridge v. Baltzell, 1 Smith, 217; Marseilles v. Kenton’s Exch., 17 Penn. St., 238. The exception is available in merchants’ accounts though none of the items come within six years: Bass v. Bass, 8 Pick. (Mass.), 187; Dyott v. Letcher, 6 J. J. Marsh. (Ky.), 544; McClellan v. Crofton, 6 Me., 308.

2 See Scarpelina v. Atcheson, 7 Q. B., 864 (53 E. C. L. B.).

3 See Webster v. Kirks, 17 Q. B., 947 (79 E. C. L. B.).

4 Though at that time an action and judgment would have been fruitless: Emery v. Day, 1 C., M. & B., 245; 4 Tyr., 695, &c.

5 It begins to run only from the time the right of action accrued: Richmond v. Richman, 5 Halsted, 114; Odlin v. Greenleaf, 3 N. H., 270; Banks v. Coyle, 2 Marsh. (Ky.), 564; Jones v. Conway, 4 Yeates (Penn.), 109; Bennett v. Herring, 1 Branch (Fla.), 337; Dobyns v. Schoolfield, 10 B. Mon. (Ky.), 311. Upon a note payable on demand the statute begins to run from its date: Wilks v. Robinson, 3 Rich. (S. C.), 182; Hirst v. Brook, 50 Barb. (N. Y.), 354; Larson v. Lambert, 12 N. Y. L. 47; Codman v. Rogers, 10 Pick. (Mass.), 488; Fells Point Sav. Ins. v. Weedon, 18 Md., 320. On a note payable “one day” after date the statute begins to run the next day: Davis v. Eppinger, 18 Col., 378; Raefle v. Moore, 58 Ga., 94; Smith v. Wilson, 15 Tex., 132; Ferris v. Williams, 1 Cr. (U. S. C. C.), 475. On notes payable “at sight” or “when called for” “when wanted,” or, in fact, upon all notes in which no definite time of payment is named, the statute begins to run from their date: Kingsbury v. Batter, 4 Vt., 458; Bowman v. McChesny, 22 Gratt. (Va.), 609; White v. Smith, 77 Ill., 351; Young v. Weston, 29 Me., 492. In Iowa, Creighton v. Rossean,

<br>

### --- Page 0492 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS, 3846

Therefore, on a bill payable at a certain period after date, the statute runs, not from the time the bill was drawn, but from the

Iowa, 133, it was held that upon a note payable “at any time within two years” the statute did not begin to run until the two years had elapsed, but in Young v. Weston, 39 Me., 492, it was held that a note payable “at any time within six years must be treated as payable on demand, and that the statute began to run from its date. Upon a note payable upon a condition, the statute begins to run when the condition is performed: Walters v. McBee, 1 Lea (Tenn.), 364; Jones v. Sisler, 3 Kan, 134. A note payable “on demand with interest after four months” is payable on demand, and the statute begins to run from its date: First Nat. Bank v. Price, 52 Iowa, 570; Loring v. Gurney, 5 Pick. (Mass.), 15. Upon bills of exchange payable “on presentation,” “at sight,” or “on demand and at sight” are payable on demand, and the statute runs from their date: Rodgers v. Rosser, 57 Ga., 319; Patterson v. Todd, 18 Penn. St., 428.

Where, however, a note in express terms contemplates that it shall not be payable until a demand is in fact made, then the statute does not begin to run until a demand is made, and the question in all such cases is whether a demand was made within a reasonable time, in view of the circumstances: Jameson v. Jameson, 72 Mo., 218.

Upon notes payable “after demand,” “after sight,” etc., it is held in England, and in some American cases, that the statute does not begin to run until demand is actually made: Wolfe v. Whiteman, 4 Harr. (N.J.), 248; Little v. Bland, 9 Pick. (Mass.), 458; Richman v. Richman, 8 N.J.L., 114; Codman v. Rogers, 10 Pick. (Mass.), 120; Wenman v. Ins. Co., 13 Wend. (N.Y.), 267; Wright v. Hamilton, 2 Bailey (S.C.), 51; Hibernia Bank v. O’Grady, 47 Cal., 579; Hathaway v. Patterson, 45 Cal., 234. But see Palmer v. Palmer, 36 Mich., 487, where it was held that upon a note payable “thirty days after demand” the statute began to run at the end of thirty days.

Upon bills payable at a particular place, they do not become due and payable so that an action can be maintained thereon until after demand at that place and its dishonor there: Picquet v. Curtis, 1 Sum. (U.S.C.C.), 473; Hunt v. Taylor, 108 Mass., 503.

If accepted after maturity the statute begins to run from the date of its acceptance. Spaulding v. Andrews, 48 Penn. St., 413; Williams v. Winans, 14 N.J.L., 339.

Upon bills or notes subject to grace, the statute begins to run from the last day of grace; Smith v. Aylesworth, 40 Barb. (N.Y.), 104; Wilcombe v. Dodge, 3 Cal., 250; Pickard v. Valentine, 13 Me., 512.

Upon checks, where the drawer had funds in the bank with which to meet it, the statute does not begin to run until it has been presented for payment: Bank v. Baxter, 31 Vt. 104; Girard Bank v. Bank of Penn Township, 39 Penn. St., 92; Mead v. Merchants’ Bank, 25 (N.Y.), 142; but where the drawer had no funds in the bank, the statute begins to run from the date of the check: Brush v. Barrett, 16 Hun. (N.Y.), 409; Fitch v. Redding, 4 Sandf. (N.Y.), 130.

Upon notes subject to assessment, the statute begins to run upon each assessment as soon as the assessment is made and notice thereof given as required by the statute: Hope Mut. Ins. Co. v. Perkins, 2 Abb. App. Dec. (N.Y.), 383; Hope Ins. Co. v. Weed, 28 Conn., 51; In re Slater v. Mut. F. Ins. Co., 10 R.I., 42; Lord v. Lilenthal, 46 N.Y., 541.

Upon a note payable by instalments the statute begins to run upon each instalment as it becomes due: Esterbrook v. Moulton, 9 Mass., 258; Bush v. Stowell, 71 Penn. St., 203; Burnham v. Brown, 23 Me., 400. But where interest upon a note

<br>

### --- Page 0493 ---

346 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

time when it fell due.’ And this is so also as to the account stated, of which the bill may be evidence."

So where the maker of a note gave it to a third person to be delivered to the payee after certain events should happen, the statute was held to run, not from the date of the note, but from the time of its delivery to the payee.®

It is conceived that if a note be payable by instalments, and contain a provision that, if default be made in payment of one instalment, the whole shall be due, the statute runs from the first default against the whole amount of the note.

And so in an action on a bill by an administrator, who had not taken out administration till after the bill became due, it was decided that the statute ran, not from the time the bill fell due, but from the time of granting letters of administration, for there can be no action till there is a party capable of suing.°

As upon a bill drawn payable after sight, there is no right of

“payable annually,” it is held that the statute does not begin to run upon the interest until the note matures: Ferry v. Ferry, 2 Cush. (Mass.), 92; Grafton Bank v. Dow, 19 Vt., 463; Henderson v. Hamilton, 1 Hall, (N. Y.), 314. But upon coupons and interest warrants, from the time they respectively become due, they being treated as negotiable notes by the law merchant, and being independent of the principal debt: Cooper v. Thompson, 13 Clatchf. (U.S.C.C.), 134; Bailey v. Lansing, 13 Id., 424; Evertsen v. Nat. Bank of Newport, 66 N.Y., 14.

1 Wittersheim v. Lady Carlisle, 1 H. Bl., 631,

2 Fryer v. Roe, 12 C. B., 437 (74 B.C. L. R.).

3 Savage v. Aldren, 3 Stark., 232 (3 E.C. L. B.).

4 See Hemp v. Garland, 4 Q. B., 419 (45 E. C. L. B.). See note 5, p. 346.

5 Murray v. East India Company, 5 B. & Ad., 204 (7 E.C.L. B.). But this interval is now to be computed where the administrator claims a chattel real: 3 & 4 Will. 4, c. 27, s. 6. The statute runs against an executor from the time the bill falls due, for he can commence an action before probate. Where an action does not accrue until after the death of the creditor, the statute does not begin to run until administration is granted, but if it accrues before his death the running is not thereby suspended: Beauchamp v. Mudd, 2 Bibb. (Ky.), 587; Hobart v. Connecticut Turnpike Co., 15 Conn., 145; Jackson v. Hitt, 12 Vt., 285; Abbott v. McElroy, 103 S. & M. (Miss.), 100. Where A. has a demand against B. which is not barred, and B. dies intestate, the statute will not begin to run until letters of administration are taken out: Burnet v. Bryan, 1 Halsted (N. J.), 377. The running of the statute against a claim on the estate of a person deceased is suspended during the time in which the administrator is not liable to an action thereon: Houpt v. Shields, 3 Port. (Ala.), 247.

Where a surety pays a note, his right of action against the maker accrues from the date of the payment, and the Statute of Limitations commences running from that time: Burton v. Rutherford, 49 Mo., 255; Bennett v. Cook, 45 N. Y., 263. So it runs against an indorser who is compelled to pay from the time of payment: Godfrey v. Rice, 59 Me., 308.

502

<br>

### --- Page 0494 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS, 347

action till presentment; without such presentment the statute does not begin to run. If a note be payable at a certain period after sight, the statute runs from the expiration of that period, after the exhibition of the note to the maker.

But we have seen that if a bill or note be payable at sight or “on demand,” those words are held not to constitute a condition precedent, but merely to import that the debt is due and payable immediately; or, at any rate, an action is sufficient demand. Therefore on a bill or note payable on demand, unless the note be accompanied by some writing restraining or postponing the right of action, the statute runs from the date of the instrument, and not from the time of the demand. Where a note payable on demand was given to a bank, accompanied by an agreement that the note should be held as a security for advances, the Court of Exchequer decided in a recent case that the statute did not begin to run against the note till after advances made, and a claim made as for a debt. The learned judge, however (Mr. Baron Martin), who tried the case, appears to have thought otherwise, or, at least, to have doubted. Where a loan was made by a check, the statute was held to run, not from the date of the check, but from the time the check was cashed.

If a note is made payable at a certain period after demand, it is like a note payable after sight; the demand and the lapse of the

1 Holmes v. Kerrison, 2 Taunt., 323.

2 Sturdy v. Henderson, 4 B. & Al., 592 (6 E.C. L. B.); Sutton v. Toomer, 7 B. & C., 416 (14 B.C. L. B.); M. & Ry., 123, &c.; Holmes v. Kerrison, 2 Taunt., 323; and see Dixon v. Nuttall, 1 C., M. & R., 307; 6 C. & P., 320 (25 E.C.L.R.), &c.

3 Capper. Lancaster, Cro. Eliz., 548; Rumball v. Ball, 10 Mod., 38; Collins v. Benning, 12 Mod., 444; M’Intosh v. Haydon, R. & M., 363.

4 Christier, Forsick, Sel. N. B., 9th ed., 351. This case is said to have been overruled in K. B., sed quere. If, indeed, a bond is conditioned to be void on payment on demand, a demand must be proved, or the bond is not forfeited: Carter v. Ring, 3 Camp., 459. In Megginson v. Harper, 2 C. & M., 322; 4 Tyr., 948, &c., it was assumed that the statute ran from the date of the note, which was payable on demand. Quare tamen, if the note be a reissuable one, and reissued, or if it be payable at a particular place; and see pp. 182, 211, 219. In general the statute begins to run from the date of a note payable on demand; Easton v. McAllister, 1 Mo., 662; Larson v. Lambert, 7 Halsted (N. J.), 247; Newman v. Kettle, 13 Pick. (Mass.), 418; Wenman v. Mohawk Ins. Co., 13 Wend. (N. Y.), 267; Wilks v. Robinson, 3 Rich. (S.C.), 182; Hill v. Henry, 17 Ohio, 9. But see Wolfe v. Whiteman, 4 Harr. (Md.), 216. Where reasonable presentment of a draft on demand is made, the statute does not commence to run as against the drawer until payment is refused by the drawee: Wood v. McMeans, 23 Tex., 481.

5 Garden v. Bruce, L. B., 3 Ex., 300; 37 L. J., Ex., 508.

<br>

### --- Page 0495 ---

347 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

Specified time after the demand are conditions precedent, and the statute runs from the time when the note falls due. And if a bill be made payable twelve months after notice, the statute does not begin to run till after notice and the twelve months subsequent.

*348* It has been suggested that where the plaintiff has been the subject of fraud, he may by a special replication avoid a plea of the statute, and postpone its application. It is now, however, settled that such a replication is bad. But possibly the fraudulent concealing of a cause of action on the part of a defendant till the plaintiff’s remedy is gone may constitute a substantive ground of action.

Upon the contract which the law implies to indemnify an accommodation acceptor, it has been held that the statute begins to run from the time at which the plaintiff is damnified by actual payment.

1 Thorpe v. Booth, R. & M., 388.

2 Clayton v. Gosling, 5 B. & C., 360 (11 B.C. L. R.); 8 D. & Ry., 110, 8 c. In case of a note payable at a given day after demand, it commences to run only from the time of the demand; Wenman v. Mohawk Ins. Co., 13 Wend. (N.Y.), 267; Little v. Blunt, 9 Pick. (Mass.), 438; Wright v. Hamilton, 2 Bailey (S.C.), 51.

3 South Sea Company v. Wymondsell, 3 P. Wms., 143; Bree v. Holbech, Doug., 630; Clark v. Hougham, 2 B. & C., 149 (10 E. C. L. B.); 3 D. & Ry., 322, 5 c; Ex parte Bolton, 1 Mont. & Ayr., 60; Granger v. George, 5 B. & C., 149 (1d E. C. L. R.); Browne v. Howard, 2 B. & B., 73 (6 E.C. L. R.). In a contract tainted with fraud, the statute runs from the time of its discovery: Pennock v. Freeman, 1 Watts (Penn.), 401; Sherwood v. Sutton, 5 Mason, 143; Turnpike v. Field, 3 Mass., 201; Miles v. Berry, 1 Hill (S.C.), 296; Frankfort v. Markley, 1 Dana (Ky.), 373; Cole v. McGluthry, 9 Me., 131. A fraudulent concealment of the plaintiff’s cause of action will not protect him against the operation of the statute: Smith v. Bishop, 9 Vt., 110; Fee v. Fee, 10 Ohio, 469; Allen v. Mille, 17 Wend. (N.Y.), 202; Baines v. Williams, 3 Ired. (N.C.), 481. 4 Imperial Gas Company v. London Gas Company, 10 Exch., 39.

5 Reynolds v. Doyle, 1 M. & G., 753 (39 E. C. L. R.); Collinge v. Heywood, 9 Ad. & E., 633 (36 E. C. L. R.); but see Webster v. Kirk, 17 Q. B., 944 (79 E. C. L. R.) When a surety on a promissory note pays it before maturity, his cause of action accrues against the principal for indemnity only when the note becomes payable: Tillotson v. Rose, 11 Met. (Mass.), 299; Farmers’ Bank v. Gibson, 6 Penn. St., 57; Jackson v. Adamson, 7 Blackf. (Ind.), 97. Where one not a party to a note divides with the maker the consideration for which it was given, promising the maker to pay his half of the amount when the note becomes due, the statute will begin to run in bar of a suit for a breach of this promise as soon as the note becomes due and unpaid; nor will its subsequent payment in full by the maker raise an implied assumpsit to him by the party who made such promise for money paid and advanced: Joiner v. Perry, 1 Strobh. (S.C.), 76. Interest is never barred till the principal is. Thus, if interest is payable yearly on a note having several years to run, the statute does not begin against the interest until the principal is due: Grafton Bank v. Doe, 19 Vt., 463.

<br>

### --- Page 0496 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS, 848

If a bill be dishonored by non-acceptance, and afterwards by non-payment, the statute runs from the refusal to accept.

Fourthly, as to the period up to which the time of limitation is computed.

The words of the statute 21 Jac. 1, c. 16, s. 3, are all actions of trespass, etc.: shall be commenced and sued within six years, etc.

Therefore, when, according to the old practice, writs bore teste of a day before the day of issuing them, it was held that the time within which the action should be brought must be computed not to the teste but to the issuing of the writ.

At present no difficulty on this subject can exist, as the date and teste of a writ are the same.

Where an action is commenced in an inferior court and removed into a superior court, the time of limitation is to be computed only to the commencement of the action in the inferior court.

To bar a set-off, the six years must have expired before action is brought.

When the statute once begins to run, it never stops, except in the cases mentioned in the fourth section, although circumstances should arise in which it is impossible to sue, as if for example, the debtor die before action, and no executor be appointed.

But where an action has been commenced in time, and then the plaintiff dies, and the period of limitation has expired, the courts, by a strained construction of the statute, have allowed the personal representative to commence another action within a year from the plaintiff’s death.

And where the defendant dies, a year is also given, and a year from the grant of administration where there is no executor. In the case of the defendant’s death, the allowance of a year rests not

1 Whitehead v. Walker, 9 M. & W., 506.

2 The day on which the cause of action accrued is to be included, as an action might have been commenced on that day: Presbrey v. Williams, 15 Mass., 193. In computing time under the statute, the first day is to be excluded, and the last to be included: Smith v. Cassity, 9 B. Mon. (Ky.), 496.

3 Johnson v. Smith, 2 Burr., 950.

4 2 Will 4, c. 49, s. 12.

5 Bevin v. Chapman, 1 Sid., 228; Matthews v. Phillips, 2 Salk., 244.

6 Walker v. Clements, 15 Q. B., 1046 (69 E. C. L. R.).

7 Rhodes v. Smethurst, 4 M. & W., 42; affirmed in error, 6 M. & W., 505.

<br>

### --- Page 0497 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS,

only on the analogy to the case of a plaintiff, but also upon the general rule that where an action abates by the act of God, the same plaintiff may have a new writ by journey's accounts. Now the action is not abated by marriage, death or bankruptcy of any party, if the cause of action survives, but the successor in interest, or such other parties as the court or a judge think fit, may be joined.

Fifthly, as to the mode in which the operation of the statute may be obviated by issuing a writ and continuing it down.

According to the old practice, the plaintiff might issue a writ, and without serving it on the defendant keep it in his pocket, and get it returned at any time within the six years, then file it (for it must have been filed), and enter continuances at any time down to the writ on which the appearance was, and, by replying the writ with the continuances, obviate the effect of the statute.

But this practice was abolished by the Uniformity of Process Act. By that act no first writ affects the operation of the statute, unless the defendant has been arrested or served with it, or proceedings of outlawry have been had upon it, or unless the writ and every continuing writ has been returned non est inventus, and entered of record within one calendar month from its expiration; and each succeeding writ must issue within a month of the expiration of the preceding, and contain a memorandum specifying the date of the first writ. The return of bailable process was to be made by the sheriff; of non-bailable, by the plaintiff or his attorney.

1 Curlewis v. Lord Mornington, in error, 27 L. J., Q. B., 439.

2 Ord. L., rules, 1-4; Taylor v. Hipkins, 5 B. & Al., 489 (7 E. C. L. B.).

3 Harris v. Woolford, 6 T. R., 617.

4 The first instance of a statute replied against is Coles v. Sybsye, Style’s R., 156, a.p., 16149; and see Dacy v. Clinch, 1 Sid., 53. As the form of the plea now is that the action did not accrue within six years before the commencement of the suit, it is not proper to reply the writ, but to traverse the plea and give the writ in evidence, producing by the roll: Dickenson v. Teague, C., M. & R., 241. The commencement of a suit to defeat the statute must be the same suit to which the plea is pleaded; Delaplain v. Crowninshield, 3 Mas. (U.S.), 329; Soulden v. Van Rensselaer, 3 Wend. (N. Y.), 743; Davis v. West, 5 Id., 63; Sherman v. Barnes, 8 Conn., 138; Callis v. Waddy, 2 Munf. (Va.), 511; Harris v. Dennis, 18 S. & B.R. (Penna.), 236; Ontario Bank v. Rathbun, 19 Wend. (N. Y.), 291; Ivins v. Schooley, 3 Har. (N. J.), 269; Cheney v. Archer, Riley (S. C.), 195; Connell v. Moulton, 3 Den. (N. Y.), 12. 2 Will. 4, c. 39, s. 10.

5 Of which the roll is no evidence: Walker v. Collick, 4 Exch., 171.

### --- Page 0498 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS. 350

By the 15 & 16 Vict. c. 76, ss. 11, 12, the writ was to be renewed every six months, and the original writ marked with a seal bearing the date of renewal; but by Ord. VELL vr Ia writ is to be in force for twelve months, and a renewed writ for six months from date of renewal.

A bill in equity, filed by one creditor on behalf of himself and the other creditors, will prevent the Statute of Limitations from running against any of the creditors who come in under the decree.

Sixthly, as to the saving clause in favor of infants, married women, lunatics, persons imprisoned or beyond seas.

An infant would have been bound had he not been expressly excepted? For infants may, during the six years, sue by their guardians. An infant cestui que trust is bound by the laches of his trustee, even in equity.

The plaintiff’s imprisonment now no longer postpones the running of the statute.

In the old Statute of Limitations, passed before the union with Scotland, the saving clause in favor of absent claimants protected claimants “out of the realm;” but the statute 24 Jac., 1, c. 16, being after the unions of the crowns, changed the expression “out of the realm” to the expression “beyond the seas.” Scotland, therefore, is not within the saving, but Dublin, or any other place in Ireland, India or the colonies was. By the 3 & 4 Will. 4, c. 42, s. 7, no part of the British Isles is to be deemed beyond the seas. And now, from the meaning of the expression “beyond seas,” whether applied to plaintiff or defendant, are excluded by the 19 & 20 Vict., c. 27, s. 12, all Great Britain and Ireland, the islands of Man, Jersey, Guernsey, Alderney and Sark, and the islands adjacent. Foreigners were within the benefit of this saving. “If the plaintiff,” says the Court of C. P., “is a foreigner, and doth not come to England in fifty years, he still hath

1 Sterndale v. Hankinson, 1 Sim., 393.

2 Pridcaux v. Webber, 1 Lev., 31.

3 Chandler v. Villett, 2 Saund., 121, a.

4 Wynch v. East India Company, 3 P. Wms., 309.

5 19 & 20 Vict., c. 97, s. 10.

6 King v. Walker, 1 W. Bl., 487.

7 Panther v. Gaitskell, 13 East, 432.

8 See Nightingale v. Adams, 1 Show., 91.

<br>

### --- Page 0499 ---

561 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS,

Six years after his coming into England to bring his action, 
if he never comes into England himself, he has always a right of 
action while he lives abroad, and so have his executors or administrators 
after his death. If one only of several plaintiffs were abroad, the case was not within the exception.

But now the plaintiff’s absence beyond the seas is no disability and gives no further time.

This statute is not retrospective.

The defendant’s absence beyond seas is not a case within the 94 Jac. 1, c. 16, though it is one in which the saving is much more necessary than when the plaintiff himself is absent, as an absent plaintiff may sue a defendant in England, but a defendant beyond seas could not formerly have been sued in England at all. To remedy this hardship, the statute 4 & 5 Anne, c. 16, s. 19, enacts that if at the accruing of the action the defendant be beyond the seas, the plaintiff may bring his action within six years after the defendant’s return. A mere setting foot on English ground is not a return within the statutes. If one of several co-defendants in an action ex contractu were abroad, the Statute of Limitations did not begin to run against any of them. But the statute 19 & 20 Vict., c. 97, s. 11, preserves the protection of the statute to such of the defendants as were within seas at the time of action accrued.

1 Strithorst v. Greme, 3 Wils., 145; 2 W. Bl., 723, s.c.; Le Veux v. Berkeley, 5 Q. B., 835 (48 E. C. L. R.); Townsend v. Deacon, 18 L. J., Exch., 298; 3 Exch., 706, s.c.; Lafond v. Ruddock, 13 C. B., 813 (76 E. C. L. B.). Query, Whether the executors are limited to six years after the testator’s death: Townsend v. Deacon, supra.

2 Perry v. Jackson, 4 T. B., 516; secus, of one of several defendants: Fannin v. Anderson, 7 Q. B., 811 (53 E. C. L. B.). The words “beyond seas” in the Statute of Limitations of a State mean out of the limits of that state: Murray v. Baker, 3 Wheat. (U.S.), 541; Shelby v. Gray, 11 Wheat. (U.S.), 361; Bank of Alexandria v. Dyer, 14 Pet. (U.S.), 141; Pancoast v. Addison, 1 H. & J. (Md.), 350; Richardson v.

Richardson, 6 Haus. (Ohio), 125; Field v. Dickenson, 3 Pike (Ark.), 409. Contra, Whitlocke v. Walton, 2 Murph., 23; Earle v. McDowell, 1 Dev. (N.C.), 16; Thurston v. Dawes, 9 S. & B. R. (Penn.), 288.

3 19 & 20 Vic., c. 97, s. 10.

4 Floud v. Patterson, 30 L. J., Chan., 486; but see Cornill v. Hudson, 8 E. & B., 429 (92 E. C. L. B.).

5 Hull v. Wyborn, 1 Show., 98; Swayn v. Stephens, Cro. Car., 333.

6 Gregory v. Hurrell, 1 Bing., 24 (8 E. C. L. B.); 8 Moore, 199, s.c.

7 Fannin v. Anderson, 7 Q. B., 811 (53 E. C. L. B.); Towns v. Mead, 16 C.B., 128 (111 E. C. L. R.); Forbes v. Smith, 24 L. J., Exch., 299; 10 Exch., 717, s.c.; and see Forbes v. Smith, 11 Exch., 161. As to what is evidence for the jury of a person not having been in England, see Koch v. Shepherd, 18 C. B., 191 (86 E. C. L. B.).

### --- Page 0500 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS. 352

When a disability is removed and the statute once begins to run, no supervening disability will stop it.

Seventhly, as to the promises, acknowledgments, or payments, which take a bill or note out of the statute.

It was at first held that nothing short of an express promise would take a debt out of the statute; then that a mere acknowledgment would, as evidence of a promise; and that a part payment of principal or interest amounted to an acknowledgment. The effect of these decisions was nearly to repeal the statute. Their consequences were somewhat restrained by the case of Tanner v. Smart, in which it was decided that a new promise or acknowledgment did not operate by drawing down the original promise to a subsequent date, but by giving a new cause of action; and that the promise stated in the replication is to be considered as the promise laid in the declaration, and must be consistent with it.

1 Toe d. Duroure v. Jones, 4 T. B., 310; Smith v. Hill, 1 Wils., 134; Gray v. Mendez, 1 Stra., 553; Rhodes v. Smethurst, 4 M. & W., 42; 6 M. & W., 351, in error. The disability which entitles a party to the benefit of the proviso must exist when the right of action first accrues, and if several disabilities exist together the statute does not begin to run until the whole are removed: Jackson v. Johnson, 5 Cow. (N. Y.), 74; Jackson v. Wheat, 18 Johns. (N. Y.), 40; Butler v. Howe, 13 Me., 397; Starke v. Starke, 3 Rich. (S. C.), 438. Where the statute has commenced to run no subsequent disability can stop it: Peck v. Randall, 1 John. (N. Y.), 165; Rogers v. Hillhouse, 3 Conn, 398; Crosier v. Gana, 1 Bibb. (Ky.), 297; Mercer v. Selden, 1 How. (U.S.), 37; South v. Thomas, 7 Mon. (Ky.), 59; McDonald v. Johns, 4 Yeager, 238; Den v. Richards, 3 Greenl., 347; Dillard v. Philson, 5 Strobhart, 213.

2 Dickson v. Thomson, 2 Show., 126.

3 Hollis v. Palmer, 2 Bing. N. C., 713 (29 E. C. L. B.); 3 Scott, 265, s. c.

4 6 B. & C., 603 (13 E. C. L. R.).

5 Without stopping to review the theory upon which courts formerly proceeded relative to acknowledgments it may be said that it is now the universal rule that, in order to remove the statute bar, there must be either an express promise to pay, or an acknowledgment of such a character that a promise to pay can be fairly implied: Sensewan v. Hirshmann, 82 Penn St., 83; Croman v. Stull, 119 Penn St., 21; Miller v. Baschone, 83 Id., 356; Wachter v. Albee, 80 IN., 47; Faison v. Bowden, 76 N.C., 425; Carpenter v. State, 41 Wis., 36; Gover v. Chamberlain, 83 Va., 286; Bell v. Crawford, 8 Gratt. (Va.), 110; Ross v. Ross, 20 Ala., 105; Bryn v. Ware, 20 Id., 687; Ten Eyck v. Wing, 1 Mich., 74; Johnson v. Evans, 8 Gill (Md.), 155; Grant v. Ashley, _ Ark., 762; Bailey v. Crane, 21 Pick. (Mass.), 323; Mumford v. Freeman, 8 Met. (Mass.), 432. Except where the statute otherwise provides, an express promise is not necessary: Black v. Reybold, 3 Harr. (Del.), 528; Lee v. Polk, 4 McCord (S. C.), 215, the acknowledgment must be so explicit as to be equivalent to a promise: Fellows v. Guimarin, Dudley (Ga.), 100; Brewster v. Hardeman, Id., 138; Bradie v. Johnson, 1 Sneed (Tenn.), 464; Bell v. Morrison, 1 Pet. (U.S.), 351; Strickland v. Walker, 37 Ala., 385; Ash v. Patton, 38 & R. (Penn.), 300; Yaw v. Kerr, 47 Penn. St., 333; Evans v. Carey, 29 Ala., 99; Gaucher v. Gondrau, 20 La. Ann., 156; Conover

### --- Page 0501 ---

352 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS,

At length, further to restrain the mischief, the late learned Lon}

v. Conover, 1 N. J. Eq., 403; Waples v. Layton, 3 Harr. (Del.), 508; Bangs v. Hall, 2
Pick. (Mass.), 368; Belles v. Belles, 12 N. J. L., 339; French v. Frazier, 7 J. J. Mar.
(Ky.), 425; Oliver v. Gray, 4 Uh. & G. (Md.), 204; Phelps v. Sleeper, 17 N. H.,
332; Hunter v. Kittredge, 41 Vt., 359; Steele v. Towne, 23 Id., 771. Such promise
may be express or implied, and a jury will be authorized and bound to infer such promise from a clear, unconditional, and unqualified admission of the existence of
the debt at the time of such admission, if unaccompanied with any refusal to pay,
or declaration indicative of any intention to insist on the statute of limitations as a
bar. This language necessarily implies that the most unqualified admission of
the existence of a debt will be insufficient to sustain recovery if accompanied by
expressions showing an intention not to pay it, or to rely on the statute for protection.
The same rule prevails in the Supreme Court of the United States, where it
has been repeatedly determined that evidence of the confessions of the defendant
that the debt still subsists will not render him liable when more than six years
have elapsed since the cause of action accrued, unless they are unqualified by any
expressions inconsistent with an intent of payment. This doctrine was held by
Marshall, C. J., in Wetzell v. Bussard, 11 Wheat. (U.S.), 315, and still more strongly
laid down in the subsequent case of Moore v. Bank of Columbia, 6 Pet. (U.S.), 99.
It was there said that to take a case out of the statute, “where there is no express
promise, there must be an unqualified and direct admission of a subsisting debt
which the party is willing to pay,” and that if there are “accompanying circumstances
which repel the intention to pay,” the plaintiff cannot recover.

A new promise is held necessary, and on the maxim, expressum facit cessare tacitum,
the fullest acknowledgment of a debt is not permitted to raise a legal promise of
payment, when accompanied with expressions inconsistent with an intention to revive the obligation which the statute has extinguished: Fries v. Boisselet, 98, & R. (Penn.), 128; Church v. Feterow, 2 Penn., 305; Hogan v. Bear, 5 Watts (Penn.),
111; Berghaus v. Calhoun, 6 Id., 220; Allison v. James, 9 Id., 381; Hay v. Cramer, 2
W. & S. (Penn.), 138; Gilkyson v. Larue, 6 Id., 217. The same rule prevails in the
other States, and there can be no recovery in cases barred by the statute, without
such an acknowledgment of the obligation of the defendant as to constitute a new
cause of action when the suit is brought in debt, or raise a new promise by implication when it is in assumpsit. Bromwell v. Buckman, 7 Blackf. (Ind.), 537; Robbins
v. Farley, 2 Strobh. (S. C.), 348; Dickinson v. Conway, 5 Ga., 486; M’Lellan v. Albee,
17 Me., 184; Pray v. Garcelon, Id., 145; Porter v. Hill, 4 Id., 41; Perley v. Little, 3
Id., 97; Ventris v. Shaw, 14 N. H., 422; Shaw v. Newell, 2 B. J., 483; Fry v. Kerk,
4 G. & J. (Md.), 509; Ten Eyck v. Wing, 1 Mann. (Mich.), 40; Taylor v. Stedman,
11 Ired. (N. C.), 440; Sherrod v. Bennett, 8 Id., 309; Cross v. Connor, 14 Vt., 398; Phelps
v. Stewart, 12 Id., 263; White v. Dow, 23 Id., 300; Brainerd v. Buck, 25 Id., 573;
Ayres v. Richards, 12 Ill., 146; Exeter Bank v. Sullivan, 6 N. H., 132; Tillett v.
Linsey, 6 J. J. Mar. (Ky.), 337; Head v. Manners, 5 Id., 255; Harrison v. Handley,
1 Bibb. (Ky.), 443; Gray v. Lawridge, 2 Id., 285.

The acknowledgment must be clear and explicit, not inconsistent with an immediate promise to pay absolutely or unconditionally: Harrison v. Handley, 1 Bibb.
(Ky.), 443; Ash v. Patton, 3 S. & R. (Penn.), 200; Head v. Manners, 5 J. J. Marsh.
(Ky.), 255; Bell v. Morrison, 1 Pet. (U.S.), 351; Bradley v. Field, 3 Wend. (N. Y.),
272; Lawrence v. Hopkins, 13 Johns., 288; Atwood v. Coburn, 4 N. H., 315; Tichenor
v. Colfax, 1 South. (N. J.), 153; Bank v. Sullivan, 6 N. E., 124; Russell v. Cobb, 8 N.
W., 154; Marshall v. Dalliber, 5 Conn., 480; Fries v. Boisselet, 98, & R. (Penn.), 123;
Hudson v. Carey, 11 Id., 10; Bailey v. Bailey, 14 Id., 195; Brackett v. Mountford, ¢

Chief Justice of the King’s Bench introduced the act 9 Geo. 4, by

First. (Mc.), 72; Reger v. Waters, 2 G. & J. (Md.), 64; Hancock v. Bliss, 7 Wend.
(N. Y.), 267; Allison v. Pennington, 7 W. & S8. (Penn.), 180; Fellows v. Guiman,

conditional promise accompanied by proof of performance of the condition, or an

is willing to pay: Horlbeck v. Horlbeck, 1 McMullan (S. C.), 197; Waples v. Layton,
3 Har. (Del.), 508; Brown v. Joynes, 1 Rich. (S. C.), 210; Kelly v. Sanborn, 9 N. H.,

French v. Frazier, 7 J. J. Marsh, (Ky.), 425; Wetzell v. Bussard, 11 Wheat. (U. S.),
310; Oliver v. Gray, 1 H. & G. (Md.), 201; Ferguson v. Taylor, 1 Hayw. (N. C.), 20;
Belles v. Belles, 7 Halst. (N. J.), 339; Purdy v. Austin, 3 Wend. (N. Y.), 187; Bar-
law v. Bellamy, 7 Vt., 4.

It has been held in some cases that a general acknowledgment of a subsisting
indebtedness, without specifying the amount of the debt or the balance due, is suf-
ficient: Lord v. Harvey, 3 Conn., 379; Whitney v. Bigelow, 4 Pick. (Mass.), 110.
But the weight of authority undoubtedly is that the acknowledgment must clearly
refer to the very debt in dispute between the parties: Clarke v. Dutcher, 9 Cow.
(N. Y.), 674; Bell v. Morrison, 1 Peters, 351; Lockhart v. Eaves, Dudley (S. C.),
331; Pray v. Garcolon, 17 Me., 145; Dinsmore v. Dinsmore, 8 Id., 433; Davis v.
Herring, 6 Mo., 21; Thompson v. French, 10 Yerg. (Tenn.), 453; Robbins v. Farley,
2 Strobh. (S. C.), 348; Martin v. Broach, 6 Ga., 21; Davis v. Steiner, 14 Penn. St.,
275; Arcy v. Stephenson, 11 Ired. (N.C.), 86. An admission by an individual that
there is something due from him on account, with a promise of payment, will take
a case out of the statute, but will justify a recovery only for a nominal sum: Kit-
tridge v. Brown, 9 N. H., 377. When part of an account is barred, an admission of
indebtedness and a general promise to settle and pay is not such a new promise as
will take the case out of the statute, for it may refer to that part unaffected by
the statute: Morgan v. Walton, 4 Barr, 321. A conditional promise to pay a speci-
fied demand, where the other party refuses to accede to the condition annexed, is
not sufficient to take the demand out of the operation of the statute, either as a
promise to pay or an admission of present indebtedness; McLean v. Allbee, 17
Me., 184; Didier v. Davidson, 2 Sandf. Ch. (N. Y.), 61. Where the promise is con-
ditional the plaintiff must show that the terms have been fulfilled so as to make
the promise absolute: Brown v. Joyner, 1 Rich. (S. C.), 210; Tompkins v. Brown,
1 Den. (N. Y.), 247; Farmers’ Bank v. Clark, 4 Leigh. (Va.), 603; Shaw v. Newall,
1 K. I., 4838. Where the acknowledgment is accompanied with circumstances or
declarations showing an intention to insist on the benefit of the statute, no
promise to pay can be implied: Bangs v. Hall, 2 Pick. (Mass.), 368; Danforth v.
Culver, 11 Johns. (N. Y.), 146; Hay v. Kramor, 2 W. & S. (Penn.), 137; McGlensey
v. Fleming, 4 D. & B. (N.C.), 129. A promise not to plead the Statute of Limita-
tions will take a case out of the operation of the statute: Lowry v. Dubon, 2
Bailey, (S. C.), 425; Warren v. Walker, 22 Me., 453. Such a promise must itself, of

O11

<br>

### --- Page 0503 ---

352 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

which it is enacted that no acknowledgment or promise by word only shall take a case out of the statute, unless in writing and signed by the party chargeable.

That where there are several joint contractors or executors, one shall not lose the benefit of the statute through a written acknowledgment signed by the other, but the plaintiff shall recover against the acknowledging party only.

That the effect of payment of principal or interest by any person shall remain as before the statute.*

In considering the operation of this and other parts of the act 9 Geo. 4, c. 14, on the 21 Jac. 1, c. 16, in respect of acknowledgments, promises, or payments, as to bills or notes otherwise barred by the statute of James, we shall inquire, first, what sort of an acknowledgment, promise or payment it must be to take a

[*353] debt out of the statute; secondly, at what time it must be made; thirdly, by whom; fourthly, to whom; and, lastly, by what evidence it must be proved.

First, as to the sort of acknowledgment, promise or payment which will save the statute,

course, be within the time. The acknowledgment revives the old debt, and does not create a new obligation: Newlin v. Duncan, 1 Marr. (Md.), 201; Love v. Hackett, 6 Ga., 486. But see Sims v. Badcliffe, 3 Rich. (S. C.), 237; Austin v. Rostwick, 9 Conn., 496. Under a general replication to a plea of the statute, evidence of an acknowledgment or promise to pay within the time is admissible: Hunter v. Starkes, 8 Humph. (Tenn.), 654. An acknowledgment or promise to pay a debt, sufficient to take it out of the statute, may be made after the commencement of the suit: Danforth v. Culver, 11 John. (N. Y.), 116.

A promise made by the maker will not take the case out of the statute as against the indorser: Dean v. Munroe, 32 Ga., 28. Acknowledgment or promise must be express, must clearly identify the debt, and must be made to the party in interest; Ringo v. Brooks, 26 Ark., 540; Green v. Goble, 7 Kan., 247; Wooters v. Bing, 54 Ill., 313; Johns v. Lantz, 69 Penn. St., 321; Chambers v. Farey, 47 Mo., 99; Simonton v. Clark, 63 N. C., 525; Norton v. Colby, 52 Ill., 198; Gray v. McDowell, 6 Bush (Ky.), 475; Lee v. Wyse, 35 Conn., 384; McClelland v. West, 65 Penn. St., 487; Yaw v. Kerr, 47 Penn. St., 333; Wolfensberger v. Young, Id., 816; Crease v. Defeganiere, 10 Bos. (N. Y.), 122; Brayton v. Rockwell, 41 Vt., 621; Hunter v. Kittridge’s Estate, Id., 359; Knight v. House, 29 Md., 194; McCormick v. Brown, 36 Cal., 180; McDonald v. Grey, 29 Tex., 60; Conway’s Ex’rs v. Reyburn’s Ex’rs, 22 Ark., 200; Dawson v. King, 20 Md., 442.

Where several claims against a party are barred, a general acknowledgment of indebtedness will not take any of them out of the operation of the statute: Walker v. Griggs, 32 Ga., 119; Smith v. Moulton, 12 Minn., 352; Boyd v. Hurlbut, 41 Mo., 264. An acknowledgment or new promise made on Sunday is admissible in evidence to remove the bar of the statute: Thomas v. Hunter, 29 Md., 496.

* Cap. 14, § 1. 2 See 19 & 20 Vict., c. 97, § 14.

512

<br>

### --- Page 0504 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS. 353

An acknowledgment, before the 9 Geo. 4, c. 14, must have been such an acknowledgment as implies a promise to pay, and must be consistent still. “The statute,” says Tindal, C. J., “did not intend, as it appeared to us, to make any alteration in the legal construction to be put upon acknowledgments or promises made by defendants, but merely to require a different mode of proof, substituting the certain evidence of a writing signed by the party chargeable instead of the insecure and precarious testimony to be derived from the memory of witnesses.” Therefore, the acknowledgment must not be accompanied with expressions repelling the inference of a promise to pay; accordingly a letter written without prejudice will not suffice; and if a payment be made, accompanied by expressions which render the intention of the payment doubtful, then the meaning of any such expressions is a question of fact for a jury. If the promise be conditional, the condition must be shown to have been performed. “There must,” says Rolfe, B., “be a promise to pay; but from a simple acknowledgment the law implies a promise.” It is sufficient if the acknowledgment or [*354] promise ascertain, either expressly or by reference, the

Haydon v. Williams, 6 Bing., 166 (20 E. C. L. R.); 4 M. & P., 811, s. c.; Fern v. Lewis, 6 Bing., 349 (19 E. C. L. R.); 4 M. & P., 1 ac; Seales v. Jacob, 3 Bing, 685 (1 E. C. L. R.); 11 Moore, 533, s.c.; Ayton v. Bolt, 4 Bing., 105; 12 Moore, 005, s.c.; Kennett v. Milbank, 8 Bing., 33 (21 E. C. L. R.); 1 M. & Scott, 102, s.c.; Brigsteck v. Smith, 1 C. & M., 483; Spong v. Wright, 9 M. & W., 629; Cawley v. Tarnell, 120 B., 291 (74 E.C. L. R.); Smith v. Thorne, 18 Q. B., 134 (83 E. C. G. L. R.); Rackham v. Marriott, 25 L. J., Exch., 324; 1 H. & N., 234, s.c.; Goate v. Goate, 1 H. & N., 29; Cornforth v. Smithard, 5 H. & N., 13; 29 L. J., Exch., 223; Everett v. Robertson, 1 E. & E., 16 (102 E. C. L. R.); Collinson v. Margesson, 29 L. J., Exch. 305; Godwin v. Culley, 4 H. & N., 373.

Ex parte Mitchell, L. R., 6 Chan. Ap., 823.

Wainman v. Ryman, 1 Exch., 118.

Tanner v. Smart, 6 B. & C., 603 (13 E. C. L. R.); 9 D. & R., 049, s.c.; Kennett v. Milbank, 8 Bing., 08 (21 E. C.L. R.); 1 M. & Scott, 102, s.c.; Linsell v. Bonsor, 2 Bing. N. C., 241 (29 E. C. L. R.); 2 Scott, 399, s.c.; Humphreys v. Jones, 14 M. & W., 5; see Howcutt v. Bonser, 3 Exch., 499. It does not appear necessary to declare on the conditional promise: Irving v. Veitch, 3 M. & W., 90; Edmunds v. Downes, 2 C. & M., 259; 4 Tyr. 173, s.c.; Haydon v. Williams, 7 Bing., 163 (20 E. C. L. R.); 4 M. & P., 811, s.c.; Gardner v. McMahon, 3 Q. B., 581 (43 E. C. L. R.); Chasemore v. Turner, L. R., 10 Q. B., 500.

Hart v. Prendergast, 14 M. & W., 741; Williams v. Griffith, 18 L.-J. Exch., 210; 3 Exch, 335, s.c.; Phillips v. Phillips, 3 Hare, 299; Buckmaster v. Russell, 4 L. T. (N.S.), 552; Lee v. Wilmot, 35 L. J., Ex., 175; L. R., 1 Ex., 364. But the acknowledgment must be made for the purpose of recognizing the debt. An acknowledgment made in other affairs and alio intuitu is not sufficient: Cockerill v. Sparke, 1 H. & Colt., 649; Everett v. Robertson, 1 E. & E., 16 (102 E. C. L. R.); Rowe v. Hopwood,

<br>

### --- Page 0505 ---

304 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS,

amount due, or if it leave the amount to be supplied by parol evidence. Where, in an action against the acceptor of a bill of exchange, to take the case out of the statute, a letter by the defendant promising “to pay the balance” was produced, but the letter did not specify its amount, the plaintiff was held entitled to recover nominal damages.

The date of a letter acknowledging a debt may be supplied by parol evidence.

The construction of an ambiguous written document given in evidence, to save the statute, is for the court and not for the jury.

Where there was a mutual and running account between the plaintiff and the defendant, any item on either side within six years would formerly have taken the whole account out of the statute, but an item in an account not mutual would not. But since Lord Tenterden’s act there must be either payment by the defendant or a signed acknowledgment.

An account once stated is within the statute.

[*355] A devise in trust to pay a particular creditor will take a debt out of the statute in equity. But a devise for the payment of debts in general will not revive a debt if the statute has run out, but will, in equity, prevent the statute from running.

38 L.J., Q.B. 1; L.R., 4 Q.B., 1. The debt is the consideration; but other good considerations, as forbearance to sue, will support the promise: Wilby v. Elgee, L.R., 10 P., 497.

1 Lechmere v. Fletcher, 1 C. & M., 623. The amount may be ascertained by extrinsic evidence: Bird v. Gammon, 3 Bing., N.C., 583 (82 E.C.L.B.); 5 Scott, 213, s.c.; Waller v. Lacy, 1 M. & Gr., 54 (89 E.C.L.B.). A letter from the debtor asking for an account bars the statute: Quincey v. Sharp, L.R., 1 Ex. D., 72; Skeet v. Lindsay, 2 Ex. D., 314.

2 Dickinson v. Hatfield, 1 M. & Rob., 141; 5 C. & P., 46 (24 E.C.L.B.), s.c.; see Kennett v. Milbank, 8 Bing., 38; 1 M. & Scott, 102, s.c.

3 Edmunds v. Downes, 2 C. & M., 459.

4 Morrell v. Frith, 3 M. & W., 402. But it is a general rule that parol evidence is admissible to explain technical terms in mercantile instruments, though the construction of the instrument is for the court: Ibid; Bowman v. Horsey, 2 M. & Rob., 85; see, too, Bourdier v. Greenwood, L.R., 13 Eq., 281; 41 L.J., 73.

5 Rothery v. Munnings, 1 B. & Ad., 15 (20 E.C.L.R.); Cotes v. Harris, B.N.P., 149; Cranch v. Kirkman, Peake, 121; Catling v. Skoulding, 6 T.R., 193.

6 Williams v. Griffiths, 2 C., M. & R., 45. The exception of merchant's accounts applied only to an action of account, or to an action on the case for not accounting: Inglis v. Haigh, 8 M. & W., 769.

7 Farrington v. Leo, 1 Mod., 268; Renew v. Axton, Carth., 3; Chievly v. Bond, 4 Mod., 105; Tickell v. Short, 2 Ves., Sr., 259.

8 Burke v. Jones, 2 Ves. & B., 275; Gulliver v. Gulliver, 1 H. & N., 174.

### --- Page 0506 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS. 300

Out2 In a recent case, Lord Brougham held, reversing a contrary decision of Sir John Leach, M. R., that a bequest of personal estate for the payment of debts will have the same effect.

As a debt due from a testator’s estate may exist, and yet the executor not be liable to pay, a mere acknowledgment of a debt by an executor is not sufficient to take a debt out of the statute; there must be an express promise. And it seems that a part payment by one executor will not take the case out of the statute as against his co-executor.

It seems that a notice in the newspaper, by a personal representative, that he will pay all debts justly due from his testator, will prevent a debt from being barred by the Statute of Limitations.

A payment must appear to be the payment of a debt, of the debt for which the action is brought and a part payment of a larger sum. “The principle,” says Parke, B., “upon which part pay-

1 Hogheav. Wynn, 1 Turn. & R., 307; Hargreaves v. Mitchell, 6 Madd., 326; Moore v. Petchell, 22 Beav., 172; Jacquet v. Jacquet, 27 Beav., 333.

2 Jones v. Scott, 1 Russ. & M., 255. But see Spong v. Wright, 9 M.& W., 629; Campbell v. Sullivan, Hardin, 17; Mann v. Warner, 4 Whart. (Penn.), 455; Agnew v. Fetterman, 4 Penn. St. 56; Murray v. Mechanics’ Bank, 4 Edw. Ch., 567; Smith v. Porter, 1 Binn. (Penn.), 209. The Statute of Limitations does not operate as between a trustee and his cestui que trust to bar a trust claim: Everts v. Nason, 11 Vt. 122. Trusts which are not barred are those continuing trusts which are not cognizable at law, but fall within the proper, peculiar and exclusive jurisdiction of a court of equity: Finney v. Cochran, 1 W. & S. (Penn.), 112.

4 Tullock v. Dunn, R. & Moo., 416; and see Atkins v. Tredgold, 2 B. & C., 23; 2 D. & Ry., 200, &c.; Fordham v. Wallis, 22 L.J., Chan., 548.

5 Scholey v. Walton, 12 M. & W., 510. An acknowledgment by an executor or administrator will not avail: Fritz v. Thomas, 1 Whart. (Penn.), 66; Thompson v. Peter, 12 Wheat. (U.S.), 565; Parkins v. Bennington, 1 Harr. (Md.), 209; Gailey v. Washington, 2 Id., 204; Peck v. Botsford, 7 Conn., 172; Oakes v. Mitchell, 15 Me., 360; Forney v. Benedict, 5 Penn. St., 225. Contra, Baxter v. Penniman, 8 Mass., 133; Emerson v. Thompson, 16 Id., 421; Larason v. Lambert, 7 Halsted (N.J.), 247; Newhouse v. Redwood, 7 Ala., 598; Hord v. Lee, 4 Mon. (Ky.), 36; Niemcewictz v. Bartlett, 13 Ohio, 271. The promise of an executor or administrator to pay a debt barred in the lifetime of the testator or intestate will not be binding; aliter, when the debt is not barred: Reigne v. Despartes, Dudley (S.C.), 118; McKee v. Ferguson, Riley (S.C.), 159; Pearce v. Zimmerman, Harper (S.C.), 305; Forney v. Benedict, 5 Penn. St., 225; Seig v. Accord, 21 Gratt. (Va.), 365; Huntington v. Babbitt, 46 Miss., 528.

Part payment by executor or administrator of claim already barred is not sufficient: McLaren v. McMartin, 36 N.Y., 88; but aliter while the statute is running: Heath v. Grenell, 61 Barb. (N.Y.), 190.

6 Jones v. Scott, 1 Russ. & M., 253.

7 Tippetts v. Heane, 1 C., M. & R., 252; 4 Tyr., 772, &c. But the sum need not then be ascertained; Walker v. Butler, 25 L. J., Q. B., 377; 6 E. & B., 506 (88 E.C., 515).

### --- Page 0507 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

The argument that a part payment states a debt out of the statute is that it admits a greater debt to be due at the time of the part payment. Unless it amounts to an admission that more is due, it cannot operate as an admission of any still existing debt.

Where a debtor owes his creditor some debts from a period longer than six years, and others from a period within six years, and pays a sum without appropriating it to any particular debt, such payment is not a payment on account to take out of the Statute of Limitations the debts due longer than six years, but the creditor may at any time apply such payments to the debts due longer than six years.

Giving a bill is sufficient as a payment or acknowledgment to. 

In Burn v. Boulton, 15 L. J. C. P., 97; 2 C. B., 476 (52 E.C.L.B.), it was held that there was a difference between a debt on a promissory note and a debt on a quantum meruit; that, therefore, if a payment is made less than the amount of the note, it need not be proved by any expressions at the time of payment to be a part payment; and see Worthington v. Grimsditch, 7 Q.B., 479 (53 E.C.L.B.).

Worthington v. Grimsditch, 7 Q.B., 479 (53 E.C.L.B.). See Gowan v. Forster, 3 B. & Ad., 510 (23 E.C.L.B.). Part payment of an account, barred by the statute, removes the bar as to the remainder: Strong v. McConnell, 5 Ab. 333; Carshorey, Huyck, 6 Barb. (N.Y.), 583; State Bank v. Woody, Enz., Ark. 638; contra, Smith v. Westmoreland, 12 S. & M. (Miss.), 663. An indorsement made on the back of a note in the handwriting of the payee is not such evidence of a part payment as to take the note out of the operation of the statute: Clapp v. Ingersoll, 2 Fairfield, 83; see Coffin v. Buchanan, 3 Fairfield (Me.), 471; McGehee v. Grue, 7 Port. (Ala.), 537; Connelly v. Pierson, 9 Ill., 108. Contra, if proved to have been made before the bar had attached: Addams v. Seitzinger, 1 W. & S. (Penn.), 243; Concklin v. Pearson, 1 Rich. (S.C.), 391; Alston v. State Bank, 4 Eng. (Ark.), 455. As to the effect of part payment, see Egery v. Decrea, 53 Me., 392; Dyer v. Walker, 54 Me., 18; Noble v. Edes, 51 Me., 34; Holmes v. Durell, Id., 201; Galpin v. Barney, 37 Vt., 627; Eaton v. Gillet, 17 Wis., 435; Hopkins v. Stout, 6 Bush (Ky.), 375.

Where a debtor admits a certain sum to be due by him, and denies that a larger sum claimed is due, a payment of the exact amount admitted is not a payment on account to take the debt out of the statute: United States v. Wilder, 13 Wall. (U.S.), 204. Part payment by indorser as assignee of maker does not take the case out of the statute as to such indorser: Merchants’ Bank v. Watson, 46 Penn. St., 310.

A payment by the principal or maker does not avail as against the indorser or surety, unless he was a party to such payment: Hunter v. Robertson, 30 Ga., 479. Payment of interest by one of two joint makers, without the knowledge and consent of the other, takes the debt out of the statute: Corlies v. Fleming, 30 N.J.L., 349. Indorsements of partial payments made upon promissory notes in the handwriting of the payee do not of themselves furnish any competent evidence of such payments to take the notes out of the Statute of Limitations; Davidson v. Delano, 11 Allen (Mass.), 523.

Mills v. Fowkes, 5 Bing., N.C., 455 (35 E.C.L.B.); Scott, 444, a.c.; Walter v. Macy, 9 L.J., C.P., 217; Scott, 156; ALw Gr, 54 (389 E.C.L.R.), 50; Nash v.

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS, 356

obviate the statute. But drawing the bill is payment or acknowledgment at the time of the drawing, and not at the time of the payment by the drawee.?

Goods treated as money are a sufficient payment.

An acknowledgment required by the 9 Geo. 4, c. 14, is by the eighth section of that act exempted from stamp duty, to which it would otherwise have been subject, as an agreement. But if it amount to a promissory note, the exempting clause does not apply, and a stamp is necessary.

A mere parol statement of an antecedent debt, without any new contract or consideration made within six years before action brought, does not constitute a sufficient cause of action to prevent the operation of the Statute of Limitations. But where there are cross demands of which there is a mutual settlement by the statement of a balance, the case is taken out of the statute, because, as [#357] observed by Mr. Baron Alderson, “The truth is that the going through an account with items on both sides converts the set-off into payments,”

Gowan v. Forster, 3 B. & Ad., 507 (23 E.C. L.R.). A negotiable note given by a debtor for part of a debt is payment of such part, and takes the debt out of the statute: Usley v. Jewett, 2 Met. (Mass.), 168,

Hart v. Nash, 2C., M. & R., 337; Hooper v. Stevens, 7 C. & P., 260 (32 E.C. L. R.); 4 Ad. & B., 71 (31 E.C. L. R.); SN. & M., 635; 1 Har. & W., 480, 6. c.; and see ag. to the evidence, Moore v. Strong, 1 Bing., N. C., 441 (27 E.C. L. R.); Bodyer v. Archer, 10 Exch., 333.

Morris v. Dixon, 4 Ad. & E., 845 (31 E. GC. L. R.); ON. & M., 433, 8. c.

Bodyer v. Archer, 10 Exch., 333; Amos v. Smith, 31 L. J., Exch., 423; Worthington v. Grimsditch, 7 Q. B., 479 (53 E. C. L. R.). See, however, Clark v. Alexander, 12 L. J. CP. 133. One item only is enough: Knowles v. Mitchell, 13 East, 219; Highmore v. Primrose, 5 M. & S. 65. See Lemero v. Elhott, 6 H. & N., 656. If there be mutual running accounts between others than merchants, and any of the items have accrued within the time of the statute this amounts to an acknowledgment of the previous account, and prevents the operation of the statute: Fitch v. Hilliary, 1 Hill (S. C.), 292; Belles v. Belles, 7 Halst. (N.J.), 339; Burnett v. Bryant, 1 Halst. (N.J.), 387; Chamberlain v. Cuyler, 9 Wend. (N.Y.), 126; Wood

<br>

### --- Page 0509 ---

OF STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS

Payment of interest is in general sufficient to take the principal out of the statute, but a payment of principal (except in the case of bills or notes) will not revive a claim for interest.

Secondly, as to the time when the acknowledgment must be made.

Except in the cases which have been mentioned of devises and bequests for the payment of debts, it makes no difference whether the promise, acknowledgment or payment were made before or after the expiration of six years. An acknowledgment which prevents the running out of the statute will also revive a debt already barred.

It was formerly held that the acknowledgment might be after action brought. But as the acknowledgment is now considered as the ground of action and the subject of the declaration, the promise, acknowledgment or payment must clearly be before action brought.

v. Pfarney, 2 Vt., 399; Davis v. Smith, 4 Me., 337; Abbott v. Reith, 11 Vt., 525; Van Swearingen v. Harris, 1 W. & S. (Penn.), 306; Thomson v. Hopper, Id., 407. Bug items in an account charged within six years do not take items charged more than six years before suit out of the statute, unless there are mutual accounts between the parties: Bennett v. Davis, 1 N. H., 19; Kimball v. Brown, 7 Wend. (N.Y.), 322; Miller v. Colwell, 2 Southard, 577; Luntin v. Logow, 1 Blackf. (Ind.), 373; Tucker v. Ivers, 6 Cow. (N.Y.), 193; Chipman v. Bates, 5 Vt., 143; Gold v. Whitehead, 14 Pick. (Mass.), 163; Blair v. Drew, 6 N. H., 235; Smith v. Ruecastle, 2 Halst. N. J., 357. To constitute mutual accounts there must be items, within the period limited by statute, on both sides of the account: Gulick v. Turnpike Co., 2 Green (Iowa), 545. Where there was an account of hats sold, and a credit of cash paid and one hat returned, it was held that this was not a mutual account: Kay v. Cramer, 2 W. & S. (Penn.), 187; Lowber v. Smith, 7 Penn. St., 381. Where all the items of an account between the plaintiff and defendant as merchants bore date more than twenty years antecedent to the commencement of the action, it was held that a small item to the debit of the defendant, dated within twenty years, but at a time when the defendant had ceased to be a merchant, such item not being of a mercantile character, would not revive the whole account against the defendant: Hancock v. Cook, 13 Pick. (Mass.), 30.

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS, 357

Payment of money into court will not take a bill or note out of the statute, except as to the amount paid in.

Thirdly, as to the person by whom the promise, acknowledgment, or payment may be made.

“It may be made by an agent,” and therefore by a wife acting as agent, and by one partner even after dissolution.

1 Reid v. Dickons, 5 B. & Ad., 499 (27 E.C.L.R.); 2 N. & M., 309, s. c.; and see Long v. Greville, 3 R. & C., 10 (10 E.C.L.R.); 4 D. & R.B., 632, s. c. It was formerly held that the acknowledgment need not be made to the party himself or his agents: Oliver v. Gray, 1 H. & G. (Md.), 204; Whitney v. Bigelow, 4 Pick. (Mass.), 110; St. John v. Garraway, 4 Port. (Ala.), 223; Minkler v. Minkler, 16 Vt., 194; Watkins v. Stevens, 4 Barb. (N.Y.), 163; Curshore v. Huyck, 6 Id., 583. Contra, Kyle v. Well, 17 Penn. St., 286; Gillingham v. Gillingham, Id., 302; but the rule as now held, requires that it should be made to the creditor or to some person having authority to act for him in that regard, so that a new contract, resting upon the old one for its consideration, may be set up in reply to a plea of the statute; Ringo v. Brooks, 26 Ark., 9; Fleming v. Stanton, 74 N.C., 203; Faison v. Bowdoin, 78 N.C., 215; McGreer v. Forsythe, 80 Ill., 98; Niblock v. Goodman, 67 Ind., 174; Zacharias v. Zacharias, 23 Penn. St., 452; Sibert v. Shuford, 16 Kan., 176; Trousdale v. Anderson, 9 Bush (Ky.), 276; Cape Girardeau Co. v. Harkinson, 58 Mo., 90; Pearson v. Darlington, 32 Ala., 227; Bachman v. Roller, 9 Baxt. (Tenn.), 409; McKinney v. Snyder, 73 Penn. St., 497; Bloodgood v. Bruen, 8 N.Y., 362.

2 Burt v. Palmer, 5 Esp., 145. But an acknowledgment in writing, signed by an agent, has been held insufficient: Hyde v. Johnson, 4 Bing., N.C., 776 (29 E.C.L.R.); 3 Scott, 289, s. c. Sed quere. This case, however, has been several times recognized, and a question has ever been made whether a written acknowledgment, signed by one of several partners in trade, has any other effect than an acknowledgment by one of several ordinary joint contractors: Clark v. Alexander, 13 L.J., C.P., 133. But now by 19 & 20 Vict, c. 97, s. 13, the signature of an agent suffices.

3 Evidence of admissions by an agent may be admissible without calling the agent: Palethorpe v. Furnish, 2 Esp., 511; Anderson v. Sanderson, 2 Stark, 204 (3 E.C.L.B.); Holt, N.P.C., 591, s. c.; Gregory v. Parker, 1 Camp., 394; but see Gibson v. Haghott, 5 C. & P., 211 (24 E.C.L.B.).

4 The acknowledgment by one of several joint debtors is sufficient to take the case out of the statute as to them all: Getchell v. Heald, 7 Me., 26; White v. Hale, 3 Pick. (Mass.), 291; Bound v. Lathrop, 4 Conn., 336; Shepley v. Waterhouse, 9 Shepl., 497; Clark v. Sigourney, 17 Conn., 511. Contra: Coit v. Tracey, 8 Conn., 263; Id., 1. An acknowledgment by one partner after the dissolution binds the other partner: Patterson v. Choate, 7 Wend. (N.Y.), 441; Smith v. Ludlow, 6 John. (N.Y.), 267; Austin v. Bostwick, 9 Conn., 403; Wheelock v. Doolittle, 18 Vt., 440. Contra: Bell v. Morrison, 1 Pet. (U.S.), 351; Searight v. Craighead, 1 Penn. St., 135; Brewster v. Hardeman, Dudley, Ga., 138; Steele v. Jennings, 1 McMullan, 297; Mun v. Donaldson, 2 Humph. (Tenn.), 166; Van Kewen v. Parmelee, 2 N.Y., 523. If a promise or acknowledgment by one partner after dissolution is made before the bar of the statute has attached, it will keep the debt alive as to all, but such promise or acknowledgment will not revive a debt once barred: Brewster v. Hardeman, Dudley, Ga., 138; Fellows v. Guimon, Id., 100; McIntire v. Oliver, 2 Hawk. (N.C.), 209; Walton v. Robinson, 5 Ired. (N.C.), 341; Beardsley v. Hall, 36 Conn., 270. Contra: Graham v. Selover, 59 Barb. (N.Y.), 313. One partner, after dissolu-

<br>

### --- Page 0511 ---

368 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

tion of the partnership, if he makes a payment. But if an agent exceed his authority in making the payment, it will not take the debt out of the statute. It may be made by an infant for necessaries. Payment of interest by an indorser of a promissory note does not take the note out of the statute as against the maker.

The 9 Geo. 4, c. 14, introduced, as we have seen, a distinction between acknowledgments and promises by words only and payments. The former, in the case of joint contracts, affected only the party acknowledging; the latter retained their former effect.

Where there is a joint contract, the parties were, under the old Statute of Limitations, respectively agents for each other in respect of that contract till the joint liability had determined. In joint action, therefore, against the makers of a joint and several promissory note, a payment by one would have revived the debt against the others. So, if the action had been brought against one alone, payment by his companion would have bound the defendant, though made fraudulently. And it made no difference that

tion, cannot by a payment in part, before the statute has run, affect the bar as to the other partners: Myatts v. Bell, 41 Ala., 222. An acknowledgment by principal is good as against the surety; Eyre v. Barker, 4 Pick., 582; Zent v. Hart, 8 Penn. St., 337. Contra: Lowther v. Chappel, 8 Ala. 353. A partial payment by one of two joint makers does not take the case out of the statute as to the other: Hathaway v. Haskell, 9 Pick. (Mass.), 42; Coleman v. Forbes, 22 Penn. St., 156. Contra: Joslyn v. Smith, 13 Vt., 353; Real Estate Bank v. Hartfield, 5 Pike, 551; Davis v. Coleman, 7 Iredell, 124; Patch v. King, 29 Me., 449; Caldwell v. Sigourney, 19 Conn. 37; Turner v. Ross, 1 R. I., 83. A partial payment by one of two obligors of a joint and several bond will not take the case out of the statute as against the other obligor, unless such payment be made while their joint liability continues: Disborough v. Bideman, 1 Spencer, 275; Zent v. Hart, 8 Penn. St., 337; Lane v. Doty, 4 Barb. (N. Y.), 530; Biscoe v. Jenkins, 5 Eng. (Ark.), 108. Payment in part by indorser will not keep the claim alive against the maker: Bibb v. Peyton, 118 & M. (Miss.), 275.

1 Wood v. Braddick, 1 Taunt., 104.

2 Linsell v. Bonsor, 2 Bing., N. C., 241; 29 E. C. L. B.; 2 Scott, 399, s. c.

3 Willins v. Smith, 4 E. & B., 180 (82 E. C. C. R. B.)

4 Warding v. Edgecumbe, 23 L. J., Exch., 313.

5 As to the effect of an acknowledgement by an executor, see Fordham v. Wallis, 22 L. J., Chan., 548. See Emery v. Day, 1 C., M. & R., 249; 4 Tyr., 690, s. c.

6 Wood v. Braddick, 1 Taunt., 104.

7 Perham v. Raynal, 2 Bing., 306; 9 Moore, 5568, s. c. Though made by a partner after a dissolution of partnership: Goddard v. Ingram, 12 L. J., Q. B., 9; 3 Q. B., 839 (13 E. C. L. B.), s. c.

8 Whitcomb v. Whiting, Doug., 629, overruling Bland v. Haselrig, 2 Vent., 101; and see Burleigh v. Stott, 8 B. & C, 36 (15 E. C. L. R.); 2 M. & B., 93, s. c.

9 Goddard v. Ingram, 3 Q. B., 834 (13 E. C. L. B.).

### --- Page 0512 ---

STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS. 309

the statute had run out, when the payment by the other [#359] joint contractor was made. But after the joint liability had been determined by the death of one of the parties, payment by the survivor would not have taken the note out of the statute against the executors of the deceased;* nor would a payment by the executor of the deceased have affected the survivor. And it has been held that nothing short of an express promise will take a debt out of the statute against an executor. And if the plaintiff rely on a payment, it must distinctly appear that the payment was made by the executor in his representative and not in his personal capacity.2 And it seems that payment by one executor would not of itself have taken the case out of the statute as against his co-executor.£ When one of two makers of a joint and several note made his companion his executor, and died, and the survivor afterwards paid interest on the note out of his own pocket, this, being an acknowledgment in his personal and not in his representative capacity, was held not to revive the debt as against the executors. But the executors of the deceased were bound, if the payment were made by the survivor before the death of their testator.®

So, where a joint note was made by a man and a woman, and the woman afterwards married, and a joint action was brought against husband and wife and the other maker, laying the promise by the other maker and the woman dum sola, and the defendants pleaded that the action did not accrue within six years, evidence of a promise by the other maker after the marriage was held to be out of the issue.

1 Channell 2. Ditchburn, 5 M. & W., 494.

2 Atkins v. Tredgold, 2 B. & C., 23 (9 E.C.L. R.); 3 D. & R., 200, 8. c.

* Slater v. Lawson, 1 B. & Ad., 396 (20 E.C.L.R.).

‘ Tullock v. Dunn, 1 R. & M., 416.

* Scholey v. Walton, 12 M. & W., 510; see, however, Griffin v. Ashby, 2 Car. & K., 139 (61 E.C.L. R.).

6 Ibid.

7 Atkins v. Tredgold, 2 B. & C., 23 (9 E.C.L. R.); 3 D. & Ry., 200, 8. c.

8 Burleigh v. Stott, 8 B. & C., 36 (15 E.C.L. R.); 2 M. & Ry., 93, 8. c.

9 Pittam v. Foster, 1 B. & C., 248 (8 E.C.L. R.); 2 D. & Ry., 363, 5 c. When a single woman gives a promissory note and marries, and the note is more than six years old, there are great difficulties in suing, although acknowledgments and payments have been made within the six years, but after marriage. A husband can only be sued for the debt of his wife dum sola during coverture: Com. Dig. Baron and Feme, C. 2; and therefore a promise by him to pay would extend his liability, and is void unless upon a new consideration: Mitchinson v. Hewson, 7

<br>

### --- Page 0513 ---

3060 STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS.

[#36 *The distinction, however, between the operation of payments and acknowledgments is abolished by the 19 & 20 Vict., c. 97, s. 14, which statute restrains the effect of the acknowledgment implied from payment and confines it to the party making it, as the 9 Geo. 4., c. 14, had restrained the effect of an express acknowledgment. But the statute is not retrospective.*

It has been held that payment of a dividend under a commission of bankruptcy against one of two makers of a joint and several note would take the note out of the statute against the solvent maker.* But that is doubtful, for it has since been more correctly held that payment of a dividend by the assignees of an insolvent will not take a note out of the statute as against his co-makers, for there is no acknowledgment of more being due.*

Fourthly, as to the person to whom the acknowledgment, promise or payment must be:

It has been held that the acknowledgment or promise need not, in point of fact, be made to the plaintiff, but may be made to a stranger.* Therefore, a letter by one joint and several maker of a promissory note to another has been decided to take the note out.

T. R., 348. An acknowledgment or payment, therefore, by the husband would not suffice. An acknowledgment or promise by the husband and wife, or by the wife alone, could have no operation, the wife being incompetent to contract: Morris v. Norfolk, 1 Taunt. 212. If the husband’s promise were considered as a promise to pay during coverture, it would still extend his liability, for an action for not paying during coverture would lie after the coverture. If as a promise to pay provided judgment be recovered during coverture (for the judgment fixes the husband with the debt, Com. Dig. Baron and Feme, B. 2), it would still be subject to these exceptions: first, there would be no cause of action till judgment recovered, which is absurd; secondly, the judgment in such an action, being against the husband alone, would charge him to a greater extent than a judgment against husband and wife; for, on a joint judgment, if the husband survive, real execution would be against his wife's lands as well as his; and if the wife survive, personal execution would, it is conceived, survive against her, and real execution would still be joint, whereas on a judgment against the husband alone he is subject, notwithstanding his predecease, to personal execution, and has no contribution in real execution. But see now p. 69, ante.

1 Jackson v. Woolley, 27 L. J., Q. B., 448. This statute has been held to be retrospective, and to take away the effect of a payment by a joint contractor as against his companion, though made before the statute: Thompson v. Walthman, 26 L. J., Chan., 1384; Jackson v. Woolley, 27 L. J., Q. B., 181.

2 Davies v. Edwards, 21 L. J., Exch., 4.

3 Jackson v. Fairbank, 2 W. H. Bl., 340, recognized in Perham v. Raynal, 2 Bing., 306 (69 E.C.L. #.); 9 Moore, 555, &c.; but see Brandram v. Wharton, 1 B. & Ad., 463.

4 Peters v. Brown, 4 Esp., 46. As to payment to an agent of the holder, see Megginson v. Harper, 2 C. & M. L., 322; 4 Tyr., 94, &c.
