### --- Page 0543 ---

[*386] CHAPTER XXIX.

HOW FAR A BILL OR NOTE IS CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT,

Suspends the Remedy on a Simple Contract, . . . 386;  
Bill given as Collateral Security, . . . 387;  
Form of Pleading, . . . 387;  
But not on a Contract under Seal, . . . 388;  
Does not suspend Distress, . . . 388;  
Payment of Attorney, . . . 388;  
Consequence of a Creditor taking Bills of a Third Person, . . . 388;  
Of the Creditor’s Agent taking the Debtor’s Bill, . . . 389;  
Earnest, . . . 389;  

What a Creditor who has paid by a dishonored Bill must prove, . . 399;  
Where the Transferor knew the Instrument to be of Value, . . . 399;  
A lost or destroyed Bill, when payment, . . . 399;  
Payment by Bank-notes or Bills or Notes payable to Bearer, . . . 399;  
Where a Bill is renewed, . . . 399;  
Taking a Bill determines a Lien, . . . 391;  
But not on Land, . . . 391.  

Though it be a general rule of law that one simple contract cannot be satisfied by another similar executory contract, for that is merely substituting one cause of action for another, yet the delivery of a valid bill or note suspends the creditor’s remedy for a debt, and if he either receives the money on the instrument, or be guilty of laches, it operates as a complete satisfaction.

4 But see Com. Dig. Accord, B.; Good v. Cheesman, 28 & Ad., 329 (22 E. L. C. L. R.); 40 & P., 513 (19 E. C. R.), 8. c.; Cartwright v. Cook, 38 & Ad., 701 (23 E. C. L. R.); Garrard v. Woolner, 8 Bing., 238 (21 E. C. L. R.); 1 M. & S., 327; Carter v. Wormald, 1 Exch., 81.

2364 Anne, &c. 9.8.7; Sibree v. Tripp, 15 L. J., Exch., 318; 15 M. & W., 93, 8. c. When the check of a third party is received by a creditor from his debtor, the presumption is that it was received as a conditional payment: McIntyre v. Kennedy, 29 Penn. St.; Kilpatrick v. Bldg. and Loan Ass’n, 119 Penn. St., 30. But this presumption may be overcome by proof that it was received in payment: Briggs v. Holmes, 118 Penn. St., 282. The payee who takes from the maker a bill as conditional payment must present and give due notice of the dishonor of the bill: Starr v. Kerr, 31 Miss., 199. The reception of a draft as conditional payment suspends the plaintiff's original right of action till the draft is properly presented for payment and such payment is refused: Phoenix Ins. Co. v. Allen, 11 Mich., 501. A note is prima facie evidence of a settlement of accounts to its date: Thornton v. Williams, 14 Ind., 518; Gaskins v. Wells, 15 Id., 253; Spencer v. Chrisman, Id., 215. Although a new note includes a new debt, it will not discharge the former note: Cotton v. Atlas Bank, 145 Mass., 43.

A new note given in renewal of an old one does not operate as a payment of the old note, but merely suspends the debt evidenced thereby, and is not a satisfaction.

<br>

### --- Page 0544 ---

BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT.

Law,” says Lord Kenyon, “is clear that if, in payment of a debt, the creditor is content to take a bill or note payable at a future day, he cannot legally commence an action on his original debt until such bill or note becomes payable, and default is made in the payment; but if a bill or note is of no value, as if, for example, drawn on a person who has no effects of the drawer in his hands, and who, therefore, refuses it, in such case he may consider it as waste paper, and resort to the original demand, and sue the debtor on it.”

The taking a bill or note from the original debtor, *or from [+387] a third person,’ amounts to an agreement to give the debtor credit for the time it has to run; but when that time has expired, and the bill or note is in the hands of the creditor unpaid, the liability of the debtor on the original debt revives.’

Until paid: Belleville Sav’g Bank v. Bornman, 124 Ill., 200; Fry v. Patterson, 49 N.

—, LL. GI,

° It may be shown that it was intended and accepted in payment: Granite National Bank v. Fitch, 145 Mass., 567.

In Maine it is held that taking a new note, prima facie, shows that the former note is paid: Snow v. Foster, 79 Me., 558; and in Colorado this is held to be the case when a note, check or bill is given for a previous indebtedness: First National Bank v. Newton, 10 Col., 161.

1 Stedman v. Gouch, 1 Esp., 3; Kearslake v. Morgan, 5 T. R., 513. An unsatisfied judgment on the bill alone will not destroy the original debt: Tarleton v. Alhusen, 2 Ad. & Ell, 32 (29 E. C. L. B.), ante, p. 240.

2 Bellshaw v. Bush, 11 C. B., 131 (73 E. C. L. R.); Bottomley v. Nuttall, 23 L. J., C.P, 110; 5 C. B. N. S., 122 (94 E. C. L. R.), 8. c.

$ Okie v. Spencer, 2 Whart. (U.S.), 253; contra Weakley v. Bell, 9 Watts (Penn.), 273. A bill or note is not satisfaction of a pre-existing debt unless so agreed, or the debtor is injured by the laches of the creditor who receives it: Hoar v. Clute, 15 Jolin. (N.Y.), 274; Woodcock v. Bennet, 1 Cow. (N.Y.), 711; Denniston v. Imbrie, 3 Wash. (U.S.C.C.), 396; Dougal v. Cowles, 5 Day (Conn.), 511; Hart v. Boller, 15 S.& R. (Penn.), 162; McGinn v. Holmes, 2 Watts, 121; Chartain v. Cox, 2 Bailey (S.C.), 574; Bill v. Porter, 9 Conn., 23; Gardner v. Gorham, 1 Douglass, 507; Weed v. Snow, 3 McLean (U.S.), 265; Hay v. Stone, 7 Hill, 128; Kelsey v. Rosborough, 2 Rich. (S.C.), 241; MeConnell v. Stettinius, 7 Ill., 707; Steamboat v. Hammond, 9 Mo., 09; Morgan v. Bitzenberger, 3 Gill (Md.), 350; Elwood v. Diefendorf, 5 Barbour (S.C.), 393; Gordon v. Price, 10 Iredell, 385; Smith v. Smith, 32 N.H., 244; Thompson v. Briggs, 32 N.H., 40; Citizens’ Bank v. Carson, 32 Mo., 191; Howard v. Jones, 33 Id., 683; Graham v. Sykes, 1 La. Ann., 49; Devlin v. Chamblin, 6 Minn., 463; Kough v. McNitt, Id., 513; Morrison v. Welty, 18 Md., 169; McCrary v. Carrington, 35 Ala., 693; Blunt v. Walker, 11 Wis., 334; Jobbill v. Goundry, 29 Barb. (N.Y.), 509; Terry v. Griffins, 10 Md., 273; Dickinson v. King, 28 Vt., 373; Sleevin v. Lurty, 1 Hemp. (Tenn.), 431; Jose v. Baker, 37 Me., 463; Barnett v. Smith, 34 N.H., 203; Coburn v. Odell, Id., 510; Caldwell v. Fifield, 20 N.J.L., 150; Bassett v. Sanborn, 9 Cush. (Mass.), 57; King v. Lowry, 20 Barb. (N.Y.), 532; Appleton v. Kennon, 19 Mo., 637; Merrick v. Bowry, 4 Ohio, 60. Aliter, if it is accepted as payment: Abercrombie v. Manly, 9 Port. (Ala.), 145; Slocumb v. Holmes, 1 How., 553

<br>

### --- Page 0545 ---

BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT,

But a creditor may agree to take for a debt already due a bill as collateral security, without affecting his present right to sue for it. (Miss.), 139; Cave v. Hall, 5 Mo., 59; Watson v. Owens, 1 Rich. (S.C.), 114; Mins v. McDowell, 3 Ga., 142. And not then, if it was the party's own note and not the note of a third person: Cole v. Lachett, 1 Hill (N.Y.), 162; Waydell v. Law, 6 Hill (N.Y.), dis; Elwood v. Deffendorf, 5 Barb. (N.Y.), 398. The rule is that a note given for a precedent debt does not operate as a payment of the original debt unless it was intended by both parties to have that effect: Hunter v. Maul, Penn. St., 13; McGuire v. Bidwell, 64 Tex., 45; Racine Bank v. Case, 63 Wis., 504; Paute Williams, 17 S.C., 396; Bertraux v. Dillon, 20 Mo. App., 603. And this is held to be the rule where the note of a third person is given, or where a surety signs with the principal debtor: Aikin v. Peters, 49 Ark., 315; Dryden v. Stephens, 19 W. Va., 11. A sealed note is held to operate as payment of an unsealed one, in place of which it was given: Chalmers v. Turnipseed, 21 S.C., 126, unless it is shown that it was not so intended: Pelzer v. Steadman, 22 S.C., 279. A worthless check or draft does not operate as payment: Fleig v. Sleet, 43 Ohio St., 53; Woodburn v. Woodburn, 115 Ill., 427. In Massachusetts, it is presumed that a negotiable note given for a debt was taken in payment, but this presumption may be overcome by proof that the parties did not intend it to have that effect: Green v. Russell, 182 Mass., 536. See also in Indiana: Weston v. Wiley, 78 Ind., 54. In New Jersey, a note given for a pre-existing debt does not operate as a payment unless it was agreed that it should have that effect: Swain v. Frazier, 35 N.J., Eq., 326. The giving of a note and mortgage is held not to extinguish a prior debt unless understood and agreed: Brown v. Dunckel, 46 Mich., 29. In Maine, Vermont, Massachusetts, and Colorado it is presumed to have been intended as payment; Descadililas v. Harris, 8 Me., 293; Wallace v. Agry, 4 Mass. U.S., 343; Cornwall v. Gould, 4 Pick. (Mass.), 444. See Hutchins v. Olent, 4 Vt., 555; Costar v. Davies, 3 Ene. (Ark.), 213; Arnold v. Sprague, 34 Vt., 402; Collamer v. Langdon, 29 Vt., 32; Page v. Hubbard, Sprague (U.S. D.C.), 335; First National Bank v. Newton, 10 Col., 161. If the debt was pre-existing, the presumption is against the acceptance of a bill or note in satisfaction; if contemporaneously contracted, the creditor must show that it was not so accepted: Noel v. Murray, 13 N.Y., 167; see Mooring v. Mobile M. Ins. Co., 27 Ala., 234. The onus is on the debtor to show that a bill or note was received as payment. It is sufficient if he shows that the parties so intended without an express agreement: Randlett v. Herren, 20 N.H., 102; Whitney v. Goin, Id., 34. Where there is an express agreement to sell goods for a particular note, delivery of the note is full payment of the debt: Ferdon v. Jones, 2 E.D. Smith (N.Y.), 106; Soffe v. Gallagher, 8 E.D. Smith (N.Y.), 507. A negotiable promissory note executed by a debtor, in settlement of his debt, to a third person at the instance of the creditor or to the creditor himself, is prima facie a payment of the original debt: Smalley v. Edey, 19 Ill., 207. An omission to require the debtor’s indorsement on the bill or note is prima facie evidence that it was received in payment: Union Bank v. Smiser, 5 Sneed (Tenn.), 501. Where the note of a third person is taken by the vendor of property at the time of sale, there is a presumption that it was in payment, but this presumption may be rebutted: Torry v. Hadley, 27 Barb. (N.Y.), 192. Where, upon receipt of a note, a receipt in full of all demands is given by the creditor, it is a question of fact for the jury: Seltzer v. Coleman, 32 Penn. St., 493. A promissory note may be satisfied by a new note. But if for any reason such new note is void, the party is remitted to his original claim: Sheppard v. Hamilton, 29 Barb. (N.Y.), 156; Central Bank v. Dana, 32 Vt., 296. A creditor taking a note which he indorses and gets discounted, but is afterwards obliged to pay, has not...

<br>

### --- Page 0546 ---

BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT. 387

that debt. A creditor who takes from his debtor as collateral security only a bill indorsed by his debtor, as he is trustee of the received payment thereby of an antecedent debt: Kean v. Dufresne, 38, & R. (Penn.), 233. If the bill or note of a third person is transferred for a debt contracted at the time, the presumption is that it is received in satisfaction; but if for a precedent debt, then it is received as collateral security merely; but in either case the presumption may be rebutted: Bayard v. Shunk, 1 Ww. & S. (Penn.), 92. The taking of such security raises a presumption that a settlement is then made of all outstanding accounts between the parties, but this is a presumption which may be rebutted by other presumptions, or by other facts and circumstances: Butts v. Dean, 2 Met. (Mass.), 76; Usley v. Jewett, Id., 168; Maynard v. Johnson, 4 Ala. 116.

The receipt by a creditor of a less sum of money and the promissory note of a third person for a portion of the balance in full of a demand is an extinguishment of the original indebtedness: Conkling v. King, 40 W. Y., 440; see Highby v. N.Y. R. R. Co., 3 Bos. (N.Y.), 497.

For other cases on payment by notes or checks, see Gover v. Littlefield, 13 Allen (Mass.), 1227; Lentell v. Getchell, 59 Me., 135; Stevens v. Anderson, 30 Ind., 301; Lawson v. Gudgell, 45 Mo., 480; Bates v. Rosekrans, 37 N.Y., 409; Dennis v. Williams, 40 Ala., 633; Meshke v. Van Doren, 16 Wis., 319; Lee v. Peckham, 17 Id., 33; Woodville v. Reed, 26 Md., 179; Milliken v. Whitehouse, 49 Me., 527; Farwell v. Supaugh, 32 Iowa, 582; Wright v. Lawton, 37 Conn., 167; Mosely v. Floyd, 31 Ga., 54; Bowe v. Still, 49 Penn St., 65; Schallenberger v. Seldonridge, Id., 83; Strong v. King, 35 Ill., 9; Crabtree v. Rewand, 33 Ill., 421; Fickling v. Brewer, 38 Ala., 633; White v. Jones, 33 Ill., 159; Burlington Co. v. Greene, 22 Iowa, 503; Lindsay v. McCleland, 18 Wis., 481; Middlesex v. Thomas, 20 N.J., 39; Gibson v. Toby, 53 Barb. (N.Y.), 191; Roberts v. Fisher, Id., 69; Myatts v. Bell, 41 Ala., 222; Hardin v. Brauner, 20 Iowa, 361; McLaren v. Hall, 26 Ill., 207; Crane v. McDonald, 45 Barb. (N.Y.), 476; Archibald v. Argall, 53 Ill., 307; Burlington Gas Light Co. v. Green, 28 Iowa, 289; Guion v. Doherty, 43 Miss., 633; Palmer v. Elliot, 1 Cliff. (U.S.C.C.), 63; Appleton v. Parker, 15 Gray (Mass.), 163; Baker v. Draper, 1 Cliff. (U.S.C.C.), 420; Paine v. Dwinel, 53 Maine, 52; Day v. Stickney, 14 Allen, 255; Thorn v. Wilson, 27 Ind., 370; Kimball v. The Anna Kimball, 2 Cliff. (U.S.C.C.), 4; Hudson v. Bradley, Id., 130; Ward v. Bourne, 56 Maine, 161; Mehlberg v. Fisher, 24 Wis., 607; Bingham v. Silly, 130 Mass., 485; Goodspeed v. S.B. Plow Co., 45 Mich., 237; Day v. Thompson, 65 Ala., 269; Theveatt v. State, 66 Ga., 673; Morriss v. Harvey, 15 Va., 426; In Re Hurst, 1 Cliff. (U.S.C.C.), 462; Case v. Seass, 44 Mich., 195; Ames, etc., Co. v. Tucker, 8 Mo. App., 95; Grant v. Monticello, 71 Ind., 68; Meehan v. Thompson, 11 Me., 492; Teal v. Spangler, 72 Ind., 330; Emerson v. O'Brien, 36 Ohio St., 491; Kell v. Larkin, 72 Ala., 493; Merchants' Bank v. Good, 21 W. Va., 455; Weaver v. Nison, 69 Ga., 699; Graham's Est., 14 Phila. (Penn.), 280; Kemmeren's App., 102 Penn. St., 508.

The giving of a check on the presentment of a draft to the drawee does not amount to the payment of the draft if the check is not paid: Kelly v. Second Bank, 62 Barb. (N.Y.), 323; Smith v. Miller, 6 Robt. (N.Y.), 413; Bradford v. Fox, 38 New York, 289; Johnson v. Bank of North America, 5 Robertson, 554; U.S. v. Thompson, 30 Ala., 575; Syracuse R.R.B. Co. v. Collins, 3 Lang. (N.Y.), 29; Burkholter v. Second Nat. Bank, 42 N.Y., 938; 40 How. Pr. (N.Y.), 324.

The holder of a check drawn upon a bank cannot avail himself of a custom by which banks belonging to an association called the clearing house, of which the drawee was one, are bound to return checks presented through the clearing house.

<br>

### --- Page 0547 ---

O87 BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT,

rights, so he is bound by the duties of a holder, and if he neglects to present or give notice of dishonor to his debtor, the debtor is discharged, for no one but the actual holder can perform these duties.

It is not essential to plead the taking of a negotiable instrument either as payment or as satisfaction. In answer to an action for debt, it is sufficient to allege that a bill or note, payable to order or bearer, was delivered for or on account of the sum due, and that the bill or note has been or is running, or that it is in the hands of a third person. But a plea was not double which alleged both that the bill was taken for and on account and also in payment. But the liberty of pleading that a bill or note was given or taken on account was confined to the case of such instruments. It must have appeared on the face of the plea that the bill or note was payable to order or to bearer, otherwise the plea would be bad, even after the verdict.

[#388] The payment of the substituted note, though given by a stranger, has been held, in an action on the first note, admissible under a plea of payment.

and which they have no funds to pay, upon the same day or before banking hours of the next day, under penalty of being liable for the same: Overman v. Hoboken City Bank, N. J. L., 61.

Where a party to whom a draft has been delivered for collection receives in payment therefore the check of the company on which the draft is drawn upon a bank and neglects to present the same during the business hours of the next day, and the company fail, he will be held liable to the party from whom he received the draft: Nunnemaker v. Larnier, 43 Barb. (N. Y.), 234.

Though the delivery of the note of a third party is not payment of a precedent debt, but merely suspends the remedy, yet if the holder is guilty of laches it operates as a complete satisfaction: Shipman v. Cook, 1 Green, 201. See Rephart v. Butcher, 17 Iowa 240; Middlesex v. Thomas, 20 N. J., 39; Smith v. Miller, 43 N. Y., 171; Lean v. Friedlander, 45 Miss., 559.

The effect of taking a note payable at a future day—though not in payment—is to extend the time: Happy v. Mosher, 45 N. Y., 313.

1 Peacock v. Pursell, 32 Vt. J., 256.

2 Kearslake v. Morgan, 5 T. R., 513; see Griffiths v. Owen, 13 M. & W., 3.

3 Price v. Price, 16 M. & W., 232; but see Mercer v. Cheese, 12 Q. B., 90; M. & G., 804 (43 E. C. L. R.), 8 c.; Crisp v. Griffiths, 20, M. & W., 159.

4 Maillard v. Duke of Argyle, 6 M. & G., 40 (46 F. C. L. R.). And an allegation that a bill was given “on account of and in payment and discharge” is not equivalent to an allegation that it was given in satisfaction: M’Dowall v. Boyd, 17 L. J., Q. B., 695; Kemp v. Watt, 15 M. & W., 672.

5 James v. Williams, 13 M. & W., 828.

6 Thorne v. Smith, 20 L. J., C. P., 71; 7 C. P., 659.

<br>

### --- Page 0548 ---

BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT. 388

The taking a bill or note from a party bound by a contract under seal does not extinguish or suspend the remedy on the specialty, unless the bill or note be actually paid. Thus, where one of three joint covenantors gave a bill of exchange for part of a debt secured by the covenant it was held that the bill only operated as a collateral security, not affecting the remedy on the covenant, and even though judgment had been obtained on the bill; Le Blanc, J., observing, “the giving of another security, which, in itself, would operate as an extinguishment of the original one, cannot operate as such by being pursued to judgment, unless it produce the fruit of a judgment.”

Where a tenant gave a note of hand for arrears of rent, it was held that the landlord might nevertheless distrain, for the note was no alteration of the debt till after payment.

The attorneys and solicitors act, 6 & 7 Vict., c. 73, s. 27, enacts that an application to tax an attorney’s or solicitor’s bill must be made within twelve months after payment. Where a promissory note is given for an attorney’s bill, payable at a future day, the twelve months run from the time the note was paid, and not from the time it was given, unless it were treated as payment at that time.

If the debtor, instead of paying the creditor, directs him to take a bill of a third person, which the creditor does, and the bill is dishonored, the liability of the original debtor revives; and it is not necessary to give the original debtor notice of the dishonor. The bill or note must be presented within a reasonable time.

1 In Bolter v. Dawkins, 16 S. C., 19, a contrary rule is adopted, and the acceptance of an unsealed note in payment of one which is sealed, is held to extinguish the former.

2 Drake v. Mitchell, 3 East, 251; and see Curtis v. Rush, 2 Ves, & B., 416.

3 Harris v. Shipway, 1744; Ewer v. Lady Clifton, C. B., Trin. T., 1735; Bull. N. P., 162; Palfrey v. Baker, 3 Price, 572; Davis v. Gyde, 2 Ad. & E.L., 623 (29 E.C. L R.); 2 N. & M., 462, 80. Even a bond given for rent does not extinguish it. Rent, though due on a parol lease, is of as high a nature as an obligation: 11 Vin., Ab., 289; Wolveramest v. Bruner, 4 H. & McH. (Md.), 89; Snyder v. Kunkleman, 3 Penn. St., 487.

4 Sayer v. Wastac*, 5 Beav., 415; In re Harries, 13 M. & W., 3; In re Wilton, Q.

5 Marsh v. Pedder, 4 Camp., 257; Holt, N. P. C., 72 (3 ELC. L. Rep), s. c.; Ex parte Dickson, cited 6 T. R., 142; Taylor v. Briggs, M. & M., 28; and see Robinson v. Read, 9 B & C, 410 (11 E. C. L. Rep); 4 Man. & Ry., 341, s. c.

6 Swinyard v. Bowes, 5 M. & S., 62; 7 Chamberlyn v. Delarive, 2 Wilson, 354.

<br>

### --- Page 0549 ---

889 BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT,

So if the creditor, not having the option of taking cash, takes of his own accord a bill of his debtor's agent, the debtor is not discharged. But if the debtor refers his creditor to a third person for payment generally, and the creditor, having the option of taking cash, elects to take a bill which is dishonored, the original debtor is discharged.

The consequence of giving a bill to an agent, an auctioneer for example, who has no authority to receive anything but cash, is that the party giving the bill is not discharged from the demand of the principal, although the bill falls due at the period when the debt ought to have been discharged, and be regularly paid to the holder.

The taking of his separate bill from one of several partners for a joint debt will, as we have seen, discharge the others. Such transaction imports an agreement between the creditor and the firm that the creditor shall rest on the liability of the one partner alone, and shall discharge the others—that is, an accord—and the separate bill is a satisfaction. For the separate liability of one partner may, in many cases, be more advantageous than his joint liability with others. It is not extinguished at law by his predecease; in the event of a separate adjudication of bankruptcy against him, it would be satisfied before joint debts, and it avoids difficulties which might arise in suing him with another defendant.

[*390] Where the creditor’s rights against an original debtor are reserved, whether by express agreement, or by the


1 Robinson v. Reade, 9 B. & C., 444 (17 E. C. L. R.); Mirsh v. Pedder, 10 Ex. N. P. C., 72 (81 E. C. L. R.); 4 Camp., 227, 8. c.

2 Sirens v. Hert, 6 B. S. & C., 243 (E. C. L. R.); 8 D. & B. R., 169; 2 C. & P., 55 (2 E. C. L. R.), s. c.; Smith v. Ferrand, 7 B. & C., 19; 9 D. & R., 803, s. c.; and see Baillie v. Moore, 15 L. J., Q. B., 169; 8 Q. B., 489 (56 E. C. L. R.).

3 Sykes v. Gilles, 5 M. & W., 645; Williams v. Evans, 1 Law Rep., Q. B., 352; Catterall v. Hindle, 1 Law Rep., C. P., 186. A check duly honored is equivalent to cash; thus, when the steward of a manor received payment from a copyholder by a crossed check, which was duly honored, the payment was held good as against the lord, although the bankers, through whose hands the check necessarily passed, being crossed, detained the proceeds on account of a debt due to them from the steward: Bridges v. Garrett, L. R. 5 C. P., 491; 39 L. J., 251.

4 6 Gen., 4. c. 16, s. 62; 12 & 13 Vict., c. 106, s. 140; 31 & 32 Vict. c. 71. A discharge on a separate bankruptcy frees from joint debts: Ex parte Hammond, L. R., 16 Eq., 614.

5 Evans v. Drummond, 4 Esp., 82; Reed v. White, 5 Esp., 122; Thompson v. Percival, 5 B. & Ad., 925 (27 E. C. L. R.); 3 N. & M. L., 667, s. c.

6 Bedford v. Deakin, 2 Stark., 178 (3 E. C. L. R.); 2 B. & Ald., 210, s. c.

<br>

### --- Page 0550 ---

BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT, 390°

nature of the transaction, or by the original debtor’s name being on the new bill, the taking of the bill of one of several, or of a stranger, does not discharge the original debtor.

Where a debtor indorses a bill to his creditor, the creditor cannot sue for his debt without proving presentment of the bill and notice of dishonor. But where he does not indorse it, it seems sufficient for the creditor, when suing for the original debt, to show that the bill still remains in his hands, without proving presentment or notice of dishonor; for that is presumptive evidence of dishonor sufficient to throw it on the defendant to show that the bill has been paid.

If the party who gave the bill in payment as a good bill knew at the time that it was of no value, or fraudulently misrepresented the solvency of parties to it, the holder, on discovering the fraud, may immediately sue such party on his original liability; or, if the bill were given for goods delivered at the time, he may disaffirm the contract, and sue in trover for the goods. Thus where a vendee, under terms to pay for goods on delivery, obtained possession of them by giving a check which was afterwards dishonored, Lord Tenterden said, “If the vendee had reasonable ground to expect that the check would be paid, the transaction was not fraudulent, and the property would pass to him; if he had not reasonable ground for so expecting, the transaction was fraudulent, and the vendors are entitled to recover their property in an action of trover.”

A negotiable bill or note given in discharge of a debt, and then lost or destroyed, is at common law payment; but the [*391] statute 17 & 18 Vict., c. 125, s. 87, may, as we have seen, enable the owner to recover.

1 Kearslake v. Morgan, 5 T. R., 513; Bridges v. Barry, 3 Taunt., 130.

2 Goodwin v. Coates, 1 M. & Rob., 221; Bishop v. Rowe, 3 M. & Sel., 362.

3 Hawse v. Crowe, 1 Z. & M., 414; Puckford v. Maxwell, 6 T. B., 52; Owenson v. Morse, 7 T. R., 64; Bishop v. Shillito, 2 B. & Ald., 229, n.; Taylor v. Flumer, 3 M. & Sel., 302; Brown v. Kewley, 2 B. & P., 513; Gladstone v. Hadwin, 1 M. & Sel., 517; Noble v. Adams, 7 Taunt., 89 (2 E. C. L. R.); Earl of Bristol v. Wilsmore, 1 B. & C., 400 (8 E. C. L. R.); 2 D. & B. R., 755, S. C.; Kilby v. Wilson, 1 R. & M., 173; Markle v. Hatfield, 2 John (N. Y.), 455; Ontario Bank v. Lightbody, 13 Wend. (N.Y.), 161; Lowrey v. Murrell, 2 Port (Ala.), 400; Bayard v. Shunk, 1 Watts & Serg., 91.

Acceptance of a counterfeit or forged note, in satisfaction, is not payment: Burrill v. Watertown Bank, 51 Barb. (N. Y.), 105; Offut v. Bank of Ky., 1 Bush (Ky.), 106; Goodrich v. Tracy, 43 Vt., 314; Belleville Sav’g Bk. v. Bornman, 124 Ill., 200.

4 Woodford v. Whitelaw, M. & M., 517; Crave v. Clay, 9 Exch., 504. N.B.—In this chapter the word PAYMENT is not always used in its strict legal sense.

909

<br>

### --- Page 0551 ---

391 BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT.

We have already seen that it has been held that where a bill or note payable to bearer is delivered without endorsement, not in payment of a pre-existing debt, but in payment or exchange for goods or other securities sold at the time, such a transaction amounts generally to a sale of such a bill or note, and to an election by the transferee to take it as money with all its risks, and, consequently, to complete payment by the transferor.

If, in payment of dishonored bills, other bills be given for the sum due, and the first bills remain in the hands of the holder, if the latter bills be not paid, the liability of parties on the first bills revives. And even if the new bills be duly paid, the holder may recover on the old bills if the amount of interest and principal due thereon be not covered by the amount of the new bills. The holder of an old bill for the full amount of which a new bill is given cannot sue on it till the new one is at maturity.

1 Chapter on TRANSFER.

2 Cambridge v. Allenby, 6 B. & C., 373; 13 B.C. T.L. R.; 9 D. & R., 391, & 0; Ward Evans, 2 Ld. Raym., 928; Brown v. Rewley, 2 B.L. & P., 498; Guardians of Lichfield Union v. Greene, 26 L.J., Exch., 149; 1 H.E. & N., 364, 80.; Smith v. Mercer, L.R., 3 Ex., 51. See the chapter on TRANSFER.

3 Atten v. Ash, 7 S. & B.R. (Penn.), 116; People v. Howell, 4 John. (N.Y.), 296; Dennis v. Hart, 2 Pick., 204; Bayard v. Shunk, 1 W. & S. (Penn.), 91. Where the vendor of goods is induced to take the note of a third person as payment by a fraudulent representation of the solvency of that person, the note is no satisfaction: Pierce v. Drake, 15 John. (N.Y.), 475; Martin v. Pennock, 2 Penn. St., 376. Where a man in payment of a debt to another gives him a counterfeit bill, if he has notice that it is counterfeit within a reasonable time, he is bound to take it back, and the question of reasonable time is one for the jury: Simms v. Clark, 11 Th., 17; Ramsdale v. Horton, 9 Penn. St., 330; Raymond v. Baner, 13 S. & B.R. (Penn. sts.).

4 Ex parte Barclay, 7 Ves., 507; Bishop v. Rowe, 3 M. & S., 362; Dillon v. Rimmer, 1 Bing., 100; 18 B.C.D.R.; 7 Moo., 425, s.c.

5 Lumley v. Musgrave, 4 Bing, N.C., 9 (33 E.C.L.R.); 5 Scott, 250, s.c.

6 Kendrick v. Lomax, 2 C. & J., 405; 2 Tyr., 435, s.c. A note or acceptance given for the price of goods at the time of purchase does not destroy the vendor's right of action on the original agreement, but only suspends the right until the expiration of the credit given: Hall v. Richardson, 16 Md., 396; McMurray v. Taylor, 9 Mo., 263; Higgins v. Wortell, 18 Cal., 330. Where a bill or note is taken for a prior debt, the party cannot recover upon the original consideration unless the bill or note is produced and cancelled at the trial, or it appears that it cannot be enforced by a third party: Miller v. Lumsden, 16 IHL, 161. The payee of a bill, acceptance of which has been refused, may surrender the bill and maintain an action on the debt for which the bill was given: Derickson v. Whitney, 6 Gray (Mass.), 248. An action may be brought on an account for which a note has been given, without surrendering the note, unless it has been negotiated, in which case it must be surrendered before execution can be taken out: Street v. Hall, 29 Vt., 165.

560

<br>

### --- Page 0552 ---

BILL OR NOTE CONSIDERED AS PAYMENT. 391

The taking of a bill or note in payment will in general determine a lien. Thus where the owner of a ship, having a lien on the goods until the delivery of goods and approved bills, took a bill of exchange in payment, and though he objected to it at the time, afterwards negotiated it, it was held that such a negotiation amounted to an approval of the bill by him, and to a relinquishment of his lien on the goods. So where, for goods sold, the vendor took the vendee’s promissory note and negotiated it with his banker, and it was subsequently dishonored, but continued outstanding in the banker’s hands, it was once held that the vendor, by taking the note and negotiating it, relinquished his lien, and that the lien did not revive on the dishonor of the note, the note continuing in the banker’s hands. But this doctrine has been overruled by a decision of the highest authority.

But if a bill or note is taken, and, remaining in the vendor’s hands, is dishonored, the goods not being delivered, it should seem that the lien revives.

On the sale of real property the taking and negotiating a note or bill by the vendor does not amount to a relinquishment of his lien on the land for the unpaid purchase-money.

A bill, check or promissory note in earnest, or part payment, within the seventeenth section of the Statute of Frauds, so as to obviate the necessity of a written contract.

A creditor cannot recover on the original debt, for which he has accepted another security, unless he can show the note to have been lost, or produces it at the trial to be cancelled: Matthews v. Dare, 20 Mad., 248.

If he only partly receives a note as collateral, he is bound to use ordinary diligence in collecting it: Roberts v. Thompson, 14 Ohio St., 1; Kilpatrick v. Home Bld Ass'n, 119 Penn. St., 20.

Horncastle v. Furnan, 3 B. & Ald., 497 (5 E. C. L. R.); 2 Stark., 590, 8. c.; Alsager v. St. Katherine’s Dock Company, 14 M. & W., 744; Tamvaco v. Simpson, 19 C. B., N.S., 473 (115 E.C. L. R.).

Burney v. Poyntz, 4 B. & Ad., 568 (24 F. C. L. R.); IN. & M., 229.

Gunn v. Bolchow, Vaughan & Co., L. J., 10 Chan. Ap., 490, where it was held that a vendor's lien was only conditionally discharged by taking vendee’s acceptances, and was capable of reviving on their dishonor, even though they had been negotiated.

New v. Swain, 1 Dans. & L., 193; Valpy v. Oakley, 16 Q. B., 941 (71 E. C. L. R.).

Ex parte Loring, 1 Rose, 19; Grant v. M. His, 2 V. & G. B., 306. See Macreth v. Simmons, 15 Ves., 329. See as to the effect of taking a void check, Bond v. Warden With. J., Chan., 154; 1 Coll., 533, 8. c.

As to the circumstances under which the transfer of a bill is payment in bankruptcy, see the chapter on BANKRUPTCY.

Chitty on Bits, 8th ed., 50, note (b), p. &f.

A covenant to pay in promissory notes implies and includes a covenant to pay the notes when due.*

An unstamped bill or check is not payment.

### --- Page 0554 ---

CHAPTER XXX. 308

OF SETS, PARTS AND COPIES OF BILLS.

What is a Bill drawn in parts is . . 393  
Effect of omitting to refer to the whole set but one Bill . . 301  
To whom the Bill belongs when the parts are in different hands . . 301  
Liability of Drawee, as . . 350  
Liability of Indorser, . . 395  
Substitutions, . . 396  

Foreign bills are often drawn in parts, all the parts together making what is called a set.

Exemplars or parts of the bill are made on separate pieces of paper, each part being numbered, and referring to the other parts. Each part contains a condition that it shall continue payable only so long as the others remain unpaid. These parts should circulate together; or one may be forwarded for acceptance while the other is delivered to the indorsee, thus relieving him from the necessity of forwarding his part for acceptance, but giving him the indorser’s security immediately, and diminishing the chances of losing the bill. Every transferor is bound to hand over to his transferee all the parts of the bill in his possession, and he

Il existe dans la négociation des lettres de change un usage qui la facilite et assure leur paiement rapide; c'est la faculté de tirer par première, seconde, et troisième, etc., c’est-à-dire de souscrire plusieurs exemplaires.

Cet usage remonte à des temps déjà reculés; il était en vigueur sous l’ancienne législation, et Cleirac en cite des exemples qui se rapportent au milieu de seizième siècle.

Il n’est pas sans intérêt de reproduire ses observations fort sensées :

“Et d’autant que les lettres de change sont des papiers volants, des petits poulets, ou billets, Polizza di Cambio, qui se peuvent facilement égarer et perdre, comme aussi le banquier correspondant à Paris peut manquer au paiement, c’est pourquoi, tant le bourgeois qui a tiré, que son commissionnaire résidant à Paris, ont chacun besoin d'une copie pour faire leurs diligences. A cette cause le banquier doit écrire, et fournir par précaution deux ou trois copies de la même lettre de semblable teneur.” Nouguier des Lettres de Change, I, 104.

The facility which drawing a bill in sets affords for its presentment has been held to accelerate the time within which a bill, payable after sight, ought to be presented for acceptance: Straker v. Graham, 4 M. & W., 721. The whole of a set of exchange constitutes but one bill, and payment or cancelling of either of the set extinguishes all: Durkin v. Cranston, 7 John. (N.Y.), 442; Ingraham v. Gibbs, 2

<br>

### --- Page 0555 ---

SETS, PARTS AND COPIES OF BILLS,

may even be liable to hand them over to a subsequent transferee if he have them still in his possession.

The whole set, of how many parts soever it be composed, constitutes but one bill, and the regular payment and cancellation of any one of the parts extinguishes all.

A firm, who were both payees and acceptors of a foreign bill in three parts, indorsed one part to a creditor to remain in his hands till some other security were given for it, and then indorsed another part of the same bill for value to a third person. They afterwards gave the first indorsee the proposed security and took back the first part of the bill from him. Held that the holder of the second part was not precluded from recovering against the firm: first, because the substitution of the security for the first part was not payment; and secondly, because the firm were, as between themselves and the second indorsee, estopped from disputing the regularity of their acceptance and indorsement of the second part.

But as between bona fide holders for value of different parts of the same bill, he who first obtains a title to his part is entitled to the other parts, and might, it has been said, maintain trover for them, even against a subsequent bona fide holder.

If a man be under an obligation to deliver a foreign bill, it seems he is bound to deliver as many parts as may be applied for.

An omission on one part to express the reference to the others.

Dalt (U. S.), 134; Miller v. Hackley, Anthon (N. Y.), 68. As to the different sets of bills, see Kenworthy v. Hopkins, 1 Johns. Cas. (N. Y.), 107.

In a suit by the indorsee of a bill against the acceptor, where the first part of the bill sued on has been accepted or protested, the production of that part is prima facie sufficient: Johnson v. Offut, 4 Met. (Ky.), 19.

1 Pinard v. Klockman, 32 L. J., Q. B., 82; 3 Best & Smith, 328 (13 EL. CL. RB.).

2 A contract to deliver up a bill drawn in parts is a contract to deliver up every part: Kearney v. West Granada Mining Company, 1 H. & N., 412.

3 Woldswoerth v. Hunter, 10 B. & C., 449 (21 ELC. 1. R.).

4 Ibid.: Perreira v. Jopp, 10 B. & C. 450 n. (21 E. C. L. RB.).

For it is the duty of a person taking one of the several parts to inquire after the others: Lang v. Smyth, 7 Bing., 281, 201 (20 F.C. LL. RB.), SOM. & PB. 13, 6, etc.; and he is advertised by the part which he does take that he takes it without the others at his peril.

6 1 Pard., 331. But since each part is now subject to a stamp, if issued or negotiated apart, 33 & 34 Vict., c. 97, s. 55, it may be doubtful whether he is so bound, unless the party applying will furnish the extra stamps.

### --- Page 0556 ---

SETS, PARTS AND COPIES OF BILLS.

and the condition relating to them, may have the effect of obliging the drawer to pay more than one part.

*The drawee should accept only one part. For if two accepted parts should come into the hands of different holders, and the acceptor should pay one, it is possible that he may be obliged to pay the other part also. And he should not pay without taking back the part which he has accepted, for, having paid the unaccepted part, he may be obliged afterwards to pay the accepted part also.

And if the indorser improperly circulates two parts to distinct holders, he may be liable on each. The forgery of the payee’s indorsement on one of the parts will of course pass no interest even to a bona fide holder. It is conceived that an indorser is not bound to pay any one part unless every part bearing his indorsement be delivered up to him.

Copies of bills are not, it is believed, much used in this country. A protest may be made on the copy of a bill in some cases. But abroad, when a bill is not drawn in sets, it is sometimes the practice to negotiate a copy, while the original is forwarded to a distance for acceptance.

In such a case the person who circulates the copy should transcribe the body of the bill, and all the indorsements, including his own, literally, and, after all, he should write “Copy:—the original being with such a person.” If he should omit to state that the bill is a

But not an inaccurate reference or an omission to name one part obviously by mistake: Bayley, 6th ed., 30.

See Holdsworth v. Hunter, 10 B. & C., 449 (21 E. C. L. R.).

See Holdsworth v. Hunter, supra.

Cheap v. Harley, 3 T. R., 127; see Smith v. Mercer, 6 Taunt., 80 (1 E. C. L. R.); Marsh, 453, s. c.; Fuller v. Smith, 1 C. & P., 197 (12 E. C. L. R.); Ry. & M., 49, B. C.

Lorsqu’une deuxième porte qu’elle ne sera payée qu’autant que la première ne l’aura pas été; l’endosseur qui endosse les deux exemplaires n'est point responsable envers le porteur de la seconde qui a reçu ce titre, tandis que la première était également en circulation.

Dans ce cas le porteur de la seconde est averti par les énonciations qu’elle contient. Pour se mettre à l'abri des fraudes de son cédant, il doit se faire remettre la première: Cour de Cassation, 4 Avril, 1832; Sircy, t. 32, 1, 29.

Dehers v. Harriot, 1 Show., 163.

The holder of such a substituted part may be deprived of his remedy against the acceptor by the intermediate act of the drawer:

### --- Page 0558 ---

*CHAPTER XXXI. [*397]  
OF FOREIGN BILLS AND NOTES.

What are Foreign and what Inland Bills . . . 397  
Stamp on an Inland Bill purporting to be a Foreign one . . . 398  
Statute 19 & 20 Vict., c. 97 . . . 397  
Sets of Bills . . . 398  
Presumption of being an Inland Bill . . . 395  
Presentment of Foreign Bills . . . 399  
Acceptance of Foreign Bills . . . 399  
PROTEST . . . 399

Bills of exchange are either foreign or inland. Inland bills of exchange at common law are such as are both drawn and payable within the limits of England, Wales and Berwick-on-Tweed.

Foreign bills, as distinguished from inland bills at common law, are such as are drawn or payable, or both, abroad, or drawn in one realm of the United Kingdom and payable in another.

Bills drawn in England and payable in Scotland or Ireland, or vice versa, were until recently foreign bills, for they were so before the union between the countries, and the union does not make them inland bills. But bills drawn and payable in Scotland, or drawn and payable in Ireland, were inland bills within 1 & 2 Geo. 4, c. 78, to which an acceptance in writing was necessary.

Holt, C. J.: “I remember when actions upon inland bills of exchange did first begin and there they laid a particular custom between London and Bristol, and it was an action against the acceptor. The defendant’s counsel would put them to prove the custom, at which Hale, who tried it, laughed, and said they had a hopeful case on ’t:” Butler v. Crisps, 6 Mod., 29; 1 Salk., 130; Holt, 119 (3 E.G. EB. Rots. ec).

A bill drawn in England on a person residing abroad, but drawn and accepted payable in England, has been held an inland bill within the stamp act: Ainner v. Clark, 2 C., Mo & N., 468.

As to the Isle of Man and the Channel Islands, see Com. Dig. Navigation, “3 and t; Godfrey v. Coulman, 13 Moo. P. C. C., 11; Haywood v. Pickering, LR, QB, 423.

Mahoney v. Ashlin, 2 B. & Ad., 478 (22 E. C. L. R.). A bill drawn in one of the United States upon a person in another is a foreign bill: Linville v. Welch, 29 Mo., 203; State Bank v. Hays, 3 Ind., 400. A bill of exchange drawn by a resident of Michigan, in favor of a Michigan payee, upon a person in Chicago, and accepted there, is a foreign bill: Mason v. Dousay, 35 Ill., 424. See also Commercial Bank v. Varnum, 49 N. Y., 269.

Mahoney v. Ashlin, 2 B. & Ad., 478 (22 E. C. L. R.).

### --- Page 0559 ---

398 OF FOREIGN BILLS.

[#398] 1. By the 19 & 20 Vict., c. 97, s. 7, bills or notes drawn in one part and payable in any other part of the British Islands are inland bills; and for the purposes of the present stamp act only bills or notes made (or purporting to be made) out of the United Kingdom are to be deemed foreign bills.

A bill of exchange is prima facie an inland bill. In an action brought on a foreign bill against a drawer or indorser, the declaration ought to have stated that it was a foreign bill. And if it did not, the defendant would be entitled to succeed on the ordinary traverses of the material allegations in the declaration.

Formerly the acceptor of a bill, purporting to be a foreign bill, but really made in England, and known by the acceptor at the time of acceptance to be so, was not precluded from objecting in an action by an innocent indorsee, that it was really an inland bill, and therefore void for want of a stamp. But there was implied warranty by transferror that a bill apparently drawn abroad really was so drawn. Now, however, every bill of exchange which shall purport to be drawn at any place out of the United Kingdom shall for all the purposes of the stamp act be deemed a foreign bill, and may, as we have seen, be stamped accordingly. By the 27 & 28 Vict., c. 56, s. 2 (now repealed), every bill of exchange payable on demand and purporting to be indorsed abroad, was, for the purposes of the stamp act, deemed to be a foreign bill.

Foreign bills are frequently drawn in sets; that is, exemplars or parts of the bill are made on separate pieces of paper, each part referring to the other parts, and containing a condition that it shall continue payable only so long as the others remain unpaid.

1. The Great Britain, Ireland, Man, Guernsey, Jersey, Alderney, Sark and the islands adjacent to any of them, s. 7.

2. Except so far as the late stamp act was concerned; and the new statute seems to be to the same effect: See Griffin v. Weathersby, L. R., 3 Q. B., 753; Heywood v. Pickering, L. R., 9 Q. B., 425. The new stamp act, 33 & 34 Vict., c. 97, s. 51, imposes ad valorem duties on foreign bills similar to those on inland bills not on demand.

3. 33 & 34 Vict., c. 97, ss. 61, 52.

4. Armani v. Castrique, 13 M. & W., 443.

5. Steadman v. Duhamel, 1 C. B., 888 (50 E. C. L. R.).

6. Gompertz v. Bartlett, 2 B. & B., 454 (75 E. C. L. R.).

7. 33 & 34 Vict., c. 57, ss. 52 & 52. See, too, the former enactment, 17 & 18 Vict., c. 83, s. 4 (repealed 1870), and Siordet v. Kuczyuski, 17 C. B., 251 (4 E. C. L. R.).

OF THE EFFECT OF FOREIGN LAW RELATING TO BILLS OF EXCHANGE AND PROMISSORY NOTES,

OF THE CONFLICT OF THE LAWS OF DIFFERENT COUNTRIES RELATING TO BILLS, 400; CONTRACTS, 405

General Principles of the English Law, . . . 401

Five Principal Rules, . . . 402

CASES WHERE THE LEX LOCI CONTRACTUS GOVERNS, . . 403

Statutes of Limitation, . . 407

Burden of Proof, . . 403

SOMETIMES bills drawn in England are payable in a foreign country, and bills drawn in a foreign country are payable in England. Sometimes English bills circulate abroad, and foreign bills circulate here; and frequently suits on foreign bills, or bills negotiated abroad, are brought in English courts of justice. The laws of foreign countries, as to bills of exchange, often differ widely from the law of England, and from each other. But natural justice, mutual convenience, and the practice of all civilized nations require that contracts, wherever enforced, should be regulated and interpreted according to the laws with reference to which they were made, otherwise the rights and liabilities of parties would entirely depend on the law of the country where the remedy might happen to be sought. Such a state of things would introduce uncertainty

* and confusion * infinitely greater than arises from that  
[401] measure of respect and comity which every tribunal now shows to the law of foreign nations.

570

<br>

### --- Page 0562 ---

OF FOREIGN LAW. 401

In determining how foreign laws are to regulate foreign contracts in English courts, a great variety of circumstances are often necessary to be considered. It may be essential to regard the domicile of one or both or all of the contracting parties, the place where the contract is made (which place it may not always be easy to determine, for the parties may live in different countries), the place where the contract is to be performed, the place where the subject-matter of the contract is locally situate, and the place where the remedy is sought.

Many nice questions, therefore, have already arisen, and many more will, no doubt, in future arise in our courts, from the conflict of English with foreign law as to bills of exchange.

The decisions of English courts of justice on the international law of contracts have not been very numerous, but nothing can exceed the discrepancy and irreconcilable contrariety of the doctrines and opinions of foreign writers, not only on the application of the principles of international law to foreign contracts, but on the very principles themselves. To enter into the discussion of such topics would be foreign to the object and exceed the limits of this little book.

But in the dearth of authoritative decisions on the degree to which foreign law is admissible here to govern the contracts arising on bills or notes made, negotiated, or payable abroad, it may not be altogether useless, with a view as well to the right understanding of such decisions as have already been pronounced as to the solution of such undecided questions on the same subject as may hereafter arise, first to enumerate some of the general principles which seem to have guided the English courts in determining the circumstances and the degree in which they will respect foreign laws in interpreting contracts either altogether or partially foreign, and then to adduce instances illustrating the application of those principles to the law of bills of exchange.

Among established principles in the law of this country, the five following rules appear to rank.

[402]

See the well-known and very learned work on the CONFLICT OF Laws, for which not only his own country and the United Kingdom, but Europe and the civilized world, are deeply indebted to the late Joseph Story.

That is to say, as to executory contracts and contracts relating to movables. But the transfer of real or immovable property is governed by another rule, the errei sitae. See Fenton v. Levington, Dor. Proc., 1859.

571

<br>

### --- Page 0563 ---

402 OF FOREIGN LAW.

First, every contract is, in general, to be regulated by the laws of the country in which it is made. For the laws of that country alone are there binding proprio vigore on aliens as well as on natural-born citizens or subjects, and the parties to the contract may generally be taken to have contemplated the legal consequence which those laws deduce from their stipulations.

Hence the formalities essential to the validity of the contract and the interpretation of that contract are to be governed by the laws of the country where it is made.

But secondly, where a contract is made in one country to be performed in another, the country where the contract is to be performed is deemed the country in which it was made. Such seems to be the general rule of the civil law. “Contraxisse unusquisque in eo loco intelligitur, in quo ut solveret se obligavit.” Some learned

According to some foreign writers, the domicile of persons entering into contracts while in a foreign country is to be considered in those contracts. Difficulties then arise where the domicile of two or more of the contracting parties is not the same. The common law does not, it should seem, regard these niceties: Copin v. Adamson, L. R., 9 Ex., 345 and 396, upheld on appeal; Jefferys v. Boosey, 4 H. of L. Cases, 814; 24 L. J., Exch., 81. See, as to a judgment in rem, Castrique v. Imrie, L. R., 4 H. of L., 414; 8 C. B. N. S., 1 (98 E. C. L. B.).

But query, how far the domicile of parties to bills of exchange regulates their personal capacity or incapacity to contract.

As to a purely personal contract which either expressly or impliedly contemplates performance in another state or country, it is presumed that the parties intended that, as to its validity, nature, obligation and interpretation, it should be governed by the law of the place of performance; Dickinson v. Edwards, 77 N. Y., 573; Stubbs v. Calt, 30 Fed. Rep., 417; Harris v. White, 81 N. Y., 532; Coates v. Bank, 15 J. & S. (N. Y.), 322, 334; Dunvigan v. Stevens, 122 Ill., 336; Curtis v. Del. Lack. & W. B. R. Co., 74 N. Y., 116. Bills drawn in one state and accepted in another, are governed by the law of the State where accepted: Hildreth v. Sheppard, 65 Barb. (N. Y.), 265; Dickinson v. Edwards, 77 N. Y., 573. Where no place of payment is named the note will be controlled by the law of the place where made: Sheldon v. Haxton, 24 Hun. (N. Y.), 196; N. E. Mortgage Security Co. v. Vader, 28 Fed. Rep., 295; Robinson v. Queen, 87 Tenn., 445; Sandheim v. Gilbert, 117 Ind. 71; Millard v. Truax, 41 N. W. (Mich.), 328. See Fessenden v. Laft, 17 Atl. (N. H.), 713; Holmes v. Manning, 19 N. East. Rep. (Mass.), 25; Winthrop v. Carlton, 12 Mass., 4; French v. French, 126 Mass., 336; Carnegie v. Morrison, 2 Met. (Mass.), 381; Cox v. U.S., 6 Pet. (U.S.), 172; Carnegie v. Morrison, 2 Met. (Mass.), 381, 397; Bulger v. Roche, 11 Pick. (Mass.), 36; Bruchett v. Norton, 4 Conn., 517; Sherrill v. Hopkins, 1 Cow. (N. Y.), 203; Allen v. Watson, 2 Hill (S. C.), 319; Loan Co. v. Towner, 13 Coun., 249; Watson v. Brewster, 1 Penn. St., 381; Watson v. Orr, 3 Dev. (N. C.), 161; Martin v. Martin, 1 S. & M. (Miss.), 176; Roe v. Jerome, 18 Conn., 138; Palmer v. Yarrington, 1 Ohio St., 253; Woodruff v. Hill, 116 Mass., 310; Evans v. Anderson, 78 Ill., 658; Overton v. Belton, 9 Heisk, (Tenn.), 762; Stox v. Mathews, 63 Mo., 371; Duerson v. Alsop, 27 Grat. (Va.), 229; Allen v. Bratton, 47 Miss. 61; National Bank v. Green, 33 Iowa, 140.

<br>

### --- Page 0564 ---

OF FOREIGN LAW, 402

Civilians have, indeed, entertained a different opinion, but such is unquestionably the general rule in the common law of England.  
“The law of the place,” says Lord Mansfield, “can never be the rule where the transaction is entered into with the express view to the law of another country as the rule by which it is to be governed.”  

Thirdly, contracts immoral, or contrary to the law of nations, or injurious to British public interests, though valid where made, will not be enforced on behalf of a guilty party in our courts.  
But, fourthly, one country will not regard the revenue laws of another country.  
Fifthly, the remedy is to be governed by the law of the country where that remedy is sought.  

Robinson v. Bland, 2 Burr., 1077; 1 W. Bl., 256, &c.; and see Rothschild v. Currie, 1 Q. B., 43 (41 B.C.L.B.); see Story's Conflict of Laws, 280 to 281; Allen v. Kemble, 6 Moore, P.C.C., 314; Smith v. Mead, 3 Conn., 253; Thompson v. Ketcham, 4 John. (N.Y.), 285; 8 Id., 189; Warren v. Lynch, 5 Id., 239; Pope v. Nickerson, 3 Story (U.S. C.C.), 465; Goddin v. Shippley, 7 B. Mon. (Ky.), 575; Broadhead v. Noyes, 9 Mo., 56; Dorsey v. Hardesty, Id., 157; Sherman v. Gasset, 9 Ill., 621; Tyler v. Trabur, 8 B. Mon. (Ky.), 306; Hunt v. Standart, 15 Ind., 33; Blodgett v. Durgin, 32 Vt., 361; Emanuel v. White, 34 Miss., 56; Cribbs v. Adams, 13 Gray (Mass.), 597; Everett v. Vendryes, 19 N.Y., 43; Brown v. Newell, 13 N.Y., 200; Davis v. Clemson, 6 McLean (U.S. C.C.), 622; Pomeroy v. Ainsworth, 22 Barb. (N.Y.), 118; Hawley v. Sloo, 12 La. Ann., 815; Vinson v. Platt, 21 Ga., 135; Able v. McMurray, 10 Tex., 350; Gaylord v. Howard, 5 McLean (U.S. C.C.), 448; Emerson v. Partridge, 27 Vt., 8; Barney v. Newcomb, 9 Cush. (Mass.), 46; Thorp v. Craig, 10 Iowa, 461.  

The laws of foreign countries are not admitted ex proprio vigore, but only ex comitate; and the judicial power will exercise a discretion with respect to the laws they may be called upon to sanction. If they are manifestly unjust or calculated to injure our own citizens, they ought to be rejected: Tappan v. Poor, 15 Mass., 419; Cambridge v. Lexington, 1 Pick. (Mass.), 506. Upon the principles of national comity, a contract made in a foreign place and to be there executed, if valid by the laws of that place, may be a valid ground of action in the courts of this state, although not valid or even prohibited by our laws, unless this State or its citizens would be injured by giving legal effect to it or it would be a pernicious and detestable example to them: Greenwood v. Curtis, 6 Mass., 358, 377.  

It is an invariable rule that the law of the forum controls the remedy: Leach v. Green, 116 Mass., 534; Taft v. Ward, 106 Mass., 518; Gott v. Dinsmore, 111 Mass., 45; Barringer v. King, 5 Gray (Mass.), 9; Ives v. Farmers’ Bank, 2 Allen (Mass.), 236; Erickson v. Nismith, 15 Gray (Mass.), 221. Interest upon a judgment is controlled by the law of the place where it was rendered: Wells v. Davis, 105 N.Y., 670; and where a note is silent as to interest, the law of the forum governs: Kapelki v. Kapelki, 112 Ind., 435; Titus v. Hobart, 5 Mass. (U.S.), 273; Robinson v. Campbell, 3 Wheat. (U.S.), 312; Blanchard v. Russell, 13 Mass., 15; Smith v. Spinolla, 2 John. (N.Y.), 198; Andrews v. Herriott, 4 Cow. (N.Y.), 508; Wood v. Malin, 10 N.J.L., 208; Ayres v. Auderbon, 2 Hill (S.C.), 601; Watson v. Brewster.

<br>

### --- Page 0565 ---

403 OF FOREIGN LAW.

The following are instances of the supremacy of the lex loci contractus according to the first general rule.

An acceptance void, or avoided by the law of the country where it is given, is not binding here. By the law of Leghorn, if a bill be accepted, if the drawer then fail, and the acceptor had not sufficient effects of the drawer in his hands at the time of acceptance, the acceptance becomes void. An acceptor at Leghorn, under these circumstances, instituted a suit at Leghorn, and his acceptance was thereupon vacated. Afterwards he was sued in England as acceptor, and now filed his bill for an injunction and relief. Lord Chancellor King granted a perpetual injunction enjoining the plaintiff at law from suing on the bill.

1 Penn. St., 381; Givins v. Western Bank, 2 Ala., 397; Smith v. Atwood, 3 McLean (U.S.C.C.), 545; McKissick v. McKissick, 6 Hump. (Tenn.), 75; Wood v. Watkinson, 17 Conn., 500; Broadhead v. Noyes, 9 Mo., 56; Dorsey v. Hardesty, Ibid., 157; Paine v. Drew, 44 N. H., 306; Fletcher v. Spalding, 9 Minn., 64.

1 Burrows v. Jemimo, 2 Sira., 753; Sel. C., 144; 2 Eq. Ab., 526; see Wynne v. Calendar, 1 Russ., 295. Where a note is made payable generally, the law of the place where it is made must determine the construction to be given to it, and the obligation and duty it imposes: Bank of Orange Co. v. Colby, 12 N. H., 520; Stacey v. Baker, 1 U1., 417; Rowell v. Buck, 14 Vt., 147; Bliss v. Houghton, 13 N. H., 126; Reddick v. Jones, 6 Ired. (N.C.), 107; Mendenhall v. Gately, 18 Ind., 149; Lawrence v. Bassett, 6 Allen (Mass.), 140; Butler v. Myer, 17 Ind., 77; Thorp v. Craig, 10 Iowa, 461; Collings Co. v. Burbham, 10 Mich., 283; Olyphant v. Atwood, 4 Bos. (N.Y.), 459; Stanford v. Pruet, 27 Ga., 243; Miller v. Mayfield, 37 Miss., 988; Kelly v. Smith, 1 Met. (Ky.), 313; Hawley v. Sloo, 12 La. Ann., 815; Young v. Harris, 4 B. Mon. (Ky.), 556; Vansant v. Arnold, 31 Geo., 210; Bank of Georgia v. Lewin, 45 Barb. (N.Y.), 340; Leo v. Selleck, 33 N. Y., 615; Tradue v. Short, 18 La. Ann., 257; Short v. Trabue, 4 Met. (Ky.), 299; Artisans’ Bank v. Park Bank, 41 Barb. (N.Y.), 590; Pecker v. Kennison, 46 N. H., 488; Hull v. Augustine, 23 Wis., 383; Stickney v. Jordan, 58 Me., 106; Harmon v. Hicks, 1 Duv. (Ky.), 322.

The making of a note and the indorsing or assigning the same are distinct contracts, each governed by the law of the place where the acts were respectively done: Nichol’s Exr’s v. Porter, 2 W. Va., 13.

The law of the place where a bill is drawn determines the rights and liabilities of the drawer: Crawford v. Branch Bank, 6 Ala., 12; Hunt v. Standart, 10 Ind., 33; Kuensio v. Elvers, 14 La. Ann., 391; Wood v. Gibbs, 35 Miss., 559; Raymond v. Holmes, 11 Tex., 54; Wilson v. Lazier, 11 Gratt. (Va.), 477. The damages recoverable from the drawer of a foreign bill of exchange which has been dishonored are those allowed by the law of the place where the bill was drawn: Price v. Page, 24 Mo., 65; Page v. Page, Ibid., 595; Bouldin v. Page, Ibid., 594. The law of the place where the bill is drawn must govern as to damages and interest when recourse is had to the drawer, and the liberality of the different indorsers is to be regulated by the law of the place where they were made: Bailey v. Heald, 17 Tex., 102. A. and B. were a mercantile firm, having business houses both in Cincinnati and New Orleans. C. drew a bill of exchange at Cincinnati addressed to “A. and B., New Orleans.” It was accepted by B. at Cincinnati for the New Orleans house, was pre- 

574

<br>

### --- Page 0566 ---

OF FOREIGN LAW. 403

A bill of exchange was drawn in France, and indorsed in blank in France without following the formalities prescribed by the French agents there at maturity and protested for non-payment; held that the drawers were liable for damages prescribed by statute on a bill drawn on a person out of the State: West v. Valley Bank, 6 Ohio St., 168; State Bank v. Rodgers, 3 Ind., 53. A bill drawn in and dated at Philadelphia in blank for the time it should run and for the names of the payee and acceptor was sent to an agent of the drawer in London to be filled up and negotiated, which was done by him. It was held that it was to be presumed that the drawers intended the bill to be received as having been drawn in Philadelphia at the time of the date, and that the party purchasing the bill in London was to be supposed as having in contemplation the law of Pennsylvania providing indemnity for dishonored bills: Lennig v. Ralston, 23 Penn. St., 137. A., domiciled here, accepts in Manchester, England, a bill drawn by B., an English merchant, resident there, payable to B. or order in London. B. sues A. here upon the bill. This is a foreign bill as if accepted payable in London: Grimshaw v. Bender, 6 Mass., 157. Where a house in New York drew a bill of exchange on a house in London, thereby creating a debt from the drawers to the acceptor, it was held that London was the place of the contract: Lizardi v. Cohen, 3 Gill (Md.), 430. A promissory note made between parties resident in New York, and there negotiated while still current, but paid by the maker before maturity, was afterwards sued in Vermont, in the name of a bona fide holder for value; held that the maker could not avail himself of the payment in defence, although by the law of Vermont, in force at the time of such payment, it would have afforded a good defence to the action: Harrison v. Edwards, 12 Vt., 648. The law of Mississippi, allowing to the maker of a promissory note the benefit of all defences against the indorser which he had against the payee before notice of the indorsement, applies to a suit brought in another State, on a note payable in Mississippi and indorsed in Mississippi: Brubston v. Gibson, 9 How. (U.S.), 266. Notes were given in New York for a usurious loan, both parties being there at the time. When the note became payable, a new contract was made by the parties for an extension of the time of payment, and new notes were made for the amount due, dated in New York, which were delivered to the lender in the State of Connecticut, where he was then staying, and the old notes were then given up. The notes were not made payable at any particular place. Held that the new contract was made in Connecticut, and to be governed by its laws as to its nature, validity and effect: Jacks v. Nichols, 5 Barb. (N. Y.), 38. Where the libellants took a promissory note of the owners of a ship in New York for materials there furnished, held that it was governed by the lex loci, by which the note was only conditional payment: Bark Chusan, 2 Story, (U. S. C. C.), 55.

The law of a place where a note is payable determines what is a default by the maker. But the contract of the indorser is regulated by that of the country where the indorsement is made: Hatcher v. McMorine, 4 Dey. (N. C.), 122; Dow v. Russell, 12 N. H., 49; Holt v. Salmon, 1 Rico (S. C.), 91; Donn v. Adams, 1 Ala., 527; Yeatman v. Cullen, 5 Blackf. (Ind.), 240; Lowry’s Adm. v. Western Bank, 7 Als., 120; Holbrook v. Vibbard, 2 Ill., 465; Mussen v. Lake, 4 How. (U. S.), 262; Cox v. Adams, 2 Kelly (Ga.), 158; Dundas v. Bowler, 3 McLean (U. S. C. C.), 397; Bank of Illinois v. Brady, Ibid., 268; Snow v. Perkins, 2 Mich., 238; Bernard v. Barry, 1 G. Greene (Iowa), 388; Raymond v. Holmes, 11 Tex., 54; Hunt v. Standart, 15 Ind., 33; Rose v. Thames Bank, Id., 292; Stanford v. Pruett, 27 Ga., 243; Everett v. Vendryes, 19 N. Y., 43; Bailey v. Heald, 17 Tex., 102; Mott v. Wright, 4 Biss. (U. S. C. 675

### --- Page 0567 ---

*403 OF FOREIGN LAW.

law. It was held that the indorsement, being void by the French law, was void here, for that the contract and indorsement being made in France must be governed by the law of France.

Where the defendant gave the plaintiff, in a foreign country where both were resident, a bill of exchange drawn by the defendant on a person in England, which bill was afterwards protested here for non-acceptance, and the defendant afterwards, while still resident abroad, became bankrupt there, and obtained a certificate of discharge by the law of that state, it was held that such certificate was a bar to an action here, founded upon an implied assumpsit to pay the amount of the bill, because the implied contract was made abroad. So payment of part in discharge of the whole of a debt, though ineffectual by the law of England, will nevertheless bar the whole debt even here, if the payment were made in a foreign country, by the law of which it would have that effect.

*404 But a discharge by the law of a place where the contract was neither made nor to be performed is not a discharge in any other country. Therefore, to an action against the acceptor of an English bill, the discharge of the acceptor under a colonial bankruptcy in Australia is no defence. Its other-

(C.), 53. Where by the law of one State the holder as collateral for pre-existing debt is a holder for value, it is not affected by the fact that the indorsement was made in another State: Ives v. Farmers’ Bank, 2 Allen (Mass.), 236.

The payee of a promissory note, which was executed and made payable in New York, having indorsed it in Indiana, was sued in Indiana on his indorsement. Held that the indorsement must be governed by the law of New York; and that if the diligence necessary by the law of that State to fix the indorsement has been used, the defendant was liable: Shanklin v. Cooper, 8 Blackf. (Ind.), 41. An indorser is liable for interest on a bill according to the law of the place on which it is drawn: Mullen v. Morris, 2 Penn. St., 50.

That a bill or note is to be construed according to the law of the place where it is payable, see Bright v. Judson, 47 Barb. (N.Y.), 29; Summers v. Mills, 21 Tex., 77; Hunt v. Hall, 1 Ala. Sel. Cas., 624; Chatham Bank v. Allison, 15 Iowa, 357; Fast v. Miller, 17 Gratt. (Va.), 47; Coffman v. Bank of Kentucky, 41 Miss., 212; Nichols v. Porter, 2 W. Va., 13; Backman v. Jenks, 55 Barb. (N.Y.), 468; Rose v. Park Bank, 20 Ind., 94; Shuessler v. Watson’s Admr’s, 37 Ala., 98; Campbell v. Nichols, 33 N.J.L., 81; Wilcox v. Williams, 5 Nev., 206; Akers v. Demond, 103 Mass., 318.

OF FOREIGN LAW. - 404

wise in the case of a Scotch bankruptcy, for that operates under a direct enactment of the imperial legislature.’

An I O U given for money lent in Germany, to play there at games of chance not illegal in Germany, is valid here.²

The following are cases in which the lex loci solutionis has been held to govern.

A promissory note or bill of exchange payable to bearer, made and payable in England, is transferable by delivery abroad, although by the law of the country where the delivery takes place, mere delivery is inoperative.³

The time of payment is to be calculated according to the law of the country where the bill is made payable.⁴ For example, the days of grace. Where the time of payment was postponed by law, it was held that the contract of the indorser was correspondingly enlarged.⁵

The notice of dishonor given and received in a foreign country must be regulated by the law of that country.

It has also been held that not only the protest but the notice of dishonor transmitted from a foreign country must be regulated by the law of the country where the bill is payable.⁶ A bill was drawn in England in favor of the defendant, a payee in England, on a house in Paris, and accepted in Paris, payable there, and indorsed to the plaintiff in England. The bill being dishonored by non-payment, notice was given to the plaintiff in England, which notice was good according to the French law, but too late according to the English law. The notice was transmitted the

B.), cited in Miller v. Race, Smith’s Leading Cases, vol. i., 43; Bradlaugh v. De Rin, L. R., 3 C. P., 538; decided on appeal in the Exchequer Chamber, L. R., 5 P., 473; 39 L. J., 234, the court holding the indorsement good, either as an indorsement or as a procuration (to which an indorsement in blank by the French laws is equivalent, see Code, art. 157 and 158).

⁶ The evidence, however, is regulated by the lex fori. The certificate of a foreign notary of demand and notice as to a promissory note, though evidence by the law of the place where the note is payable, is not therefore admissible evidence elsewhere; Kitland v. Wanzer, 2 Duer (N. Y.), 277.
